
## 4.5 Overhang and Support Decision Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SURF[Downward Facing Surface] --> Q1{Angle from Vertical}
    Q1 -->|Greater than 45 deg| OK[No Support Needed Self Supporting]
    Q1 -->|Between 20 and 45 deg| SUP[Add Minimal Supports]
    Q1 -->|Less than 20 deg| MAND[Mandatory Dense Support]
    Q1 -->|Zero degrees flat down| IMP[Impossible to Build Modify Geometry]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block above models a *physical phenomenon or design workflow* that DfAM engineers encounter. The flowcharts are deliberately abstracted to a functional architecture — equivalent to a process routing map. In your KTU answers, such diagrams score full marks when annotated with the relevant design rules.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**"Differentiate between Design for Manufacturing (DfM) and Design for Additive Manufacturing (DfAM)."**

**Model Answer:**

Design for Manufacturing (DfM) refers to designing parts such that they can be **easily and economically produced using traditional manufacturing processes** such as casting, forging, machining, or sheet-metal forming. DfM emphasizes simplicity, the avoidance of undercuts, generous fillet radii, draft angles, and accessibility for cutting tools.

Design for Additive Manufacturing (DfAM), on the other hand, is a design philosophy tailored to the **layer-by-layer free-form fabrication** capabilities of AM. DfAM embraces complexity, supports organic and topology-optimized geometries, encourages part consolidation, and utilizes lattice and internal features that would be impossible with conventional processes.

| Aspect | DfM | DfAM |
|---|---|---|
| Complexity | Avoided | Embraced |
| Internal Features | Avoided | Encouraged |
| Tooling Constraints | Strong | Negligible |
| Cost Driver | Material + machining time | Build time + post-processing |
| Design Heuristic | Simplify | Optimize and consolidate |

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for DfAM specifics, 1 for tabular contrast)*

---

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**"Explain the concept of the self-supporting angle in additive manufacturing with its significance in DfAM."**

**Model Answer:**

The self-supporting angle (also called the critical overhang angle) is the **minimum angle that a downward-facing surface can make with the vertical build axis without requiring support structures**. For most metal PBF processes, this angle is approximately **45°** from the vertical (i.e., the surface can overhang up to 45° from vertical without support). For EBM, due to higher build-plate temperature, the angle may be relaxed to ≈50°.

**Significance in DfAM:**
1. **Support Reduction:** Designing parts so that all overhangs exceed the self-supporting angle eliminates (or drastically reduces) support structures, saving material, build time, and post-processing cost.
2. **Surface Finish:** Unsupported overhangs typically have a rougher, droopy surface (dross formation); designing self-supporting surfaces improves as-built finish.
3. **Distortion Control:** Supports help anchor the part to the build plate, reducing thermal distortion. Reducing the need for supports therefore requires careful part orientation.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for typical value, 1 for DfAM significance)*

---

## PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ⭐ Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**"With neat sketches and derivations, explain the major design guidelines for metal additive manufacturing. Also derive an expression for the build time of a part in SLM and compute the staircase surface roughness for an inclined surface of angle 60° with a layer thickness of 30 µm."**

#### Part (a) — [7 Marks] Design Guidelines for Metal AM

**Model Solution:**

Metal AM (specifically Powder Bed Fusion via SLM/DMLS or Directed Energy Deposition) imposes unique geometric constraints. The key design guidelines are:

1. **Minimum Wall Thickness:** Walls must be at least 0.3 mm to ensure stable melt-pool formation. Thinner walls risk incomplete melting, warping, or burn-through.

2. **Self-Supporting Overhangs:** All downward-facing surfaces should make an angle ≥ 45° with the vertical. Below this angle, support structures are mandatory.

3. **Powder Removal:** Hollow internal volumes must include **drain holes** of at least 4 mm diameter to allow unfused powder to escape after the build.

4. **Hole and Channel Design:** Minimum printable hole diameter is 0.5–1.0 mm; holes with diameter less than 2 mm may close up due to surface tension of the melt pool.

5. **Wall-to-Feature Distance:** Distance from a wall to a hole should be at least half the wall thickness to prevent distortion.

6. **Avoid Large Flat Horizontal Surfaces:** These warp under residual stress; consider using **lattice supports** or **chamfers**.

7. **Assembly Clearances:** Loose tolerances — allow at least 0.1–0.3 mm clearance for press-fit features (compared to 0.01 mm in machining).

8. **Build Orientation:** Critical load-bearing features should be aligned perpendicular to the build plane (Z direction) for maximum mechanical strength, since Z-direction properties are typically slightly lower.

9. **Part Consolidation:** Multiple parts should be redesigned as a single monolithic component wherever feasible.

10. **Embedded Features:** Lattices, conformal cooling channels, and internal honeycomb structures should be exploited for lightweighting and thermal management.

*(7 marks — 1 mark each for 7 guidelines well explained, or 0.5 mark each for 14 sub-points)*

#### Part (b) — [7 Marks] Build Time Derivation and Staircase Roughness Calculation

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Build Time Derivation (Section 3.1 Recap):**

The build time of an SLM part is governed by the number of layers and the time required per layer.

$$
N_{layers} = \frac{H_{total}}{h_{layer}}
$$

$$
t_{build} = N_{layers} \times (t_{scan} + t_{recoat} + t_{lower})
$$

**Step 2 — Staircase Roughness Calculation:**

Given: $h_{layer} = 30\ \mu m = 0.03\ \text{mm}$, $\theta = 60°$ (angle from horizontal).

The peak-to-valley height of the staircase defect is equal to $h_{layer}$. The amplitude normal to the ideal inclined surface is:

$$
R_{a,stair} = \frac{h_{layer}}{2 \cdot \cos\theta}
$$

Substituting:

$$
R_{a,stair} = \frac{30}{2 \times \cos 60°} = \frac{30}{2 \times 0.5}
$$

$$
R_{a,stair} = 30\ \mu m
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The as-built inclined surface will have an average roughness of approximately 30 µm. To improve finish, the designer should either reorient the surface to be vertical (no staircase) or specify a finer layer thickness (e.g., 20 µm reduces the roughness by one-third).

*(7 marks — 2 for build-time derivation, 3 for staircase formula and calculation, 2 for interpretation and DfAM insight)*

---

### ⭐ Question B — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]

**"Explain the concepts of (i) Part Consolidation and (ii) Topology Optimization in the context of DfAM. Using a suitable cost model, compute the cost of a Ti6Al4V part of volume 50 cm³ given: powder cost Rs 8000/kg, machine cost Rs 2500/hr, build time 11 hr, setup cost Rs 5000, and density 4.43 g/cm³."**

#### Part (a) — [7 Marks] Part Consolidation and Topology Optimization

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Part Consolidation:**
Part consolidation is the DfAM strategy of redesigning a multi-component assembly as a single, monolithic AM part. Traditionally, an assembly might contain brackets, fasteners, gaskets, and welded sub-components. Each interface is a potential failure mode (fatigue, corrosion, loosening) and adds tolerance stack-up.

By consolidating all functions into one part:
- **Weight** is reduced (no fasteners, no overlap regions).
- **Assembly time and labor** are eliminated.
- **Quality and reliability** improve (no joint failures).
- **Functional integration** becomes possible (e.g., integrated sensor housings, cooling channels).

**Classic Example:** The GE LEAP fuel nozzle — originally 18 parts assembled by brazing — was redesigned as a single SLM-printed part. Result: 25% weight reduction, 5× life improvement, and significantly reduced manufacturing cost.

**(ii) Topology Optimization:**
Topology optimization is a computational, FEA-driven design method that removes material from low-stress regions of a design space to achieve the **minimum mass** for a given **stiffness** (or maximum stiffness for a given mass) under prescribed loads and boundary conditions.

The algorithm treats the design space as a continuum, assigning material density to each element based on its contribution to the overall structural performance. The resulting geometry is typically **organic, curved, and lattice-rich** — impossible to manufacture by conventional means but ideal for AM.

**AM-Specific Constraints** that should be applied to topology optimization:
- Minimum member size (manufacturability).
- Symmetry planes (if required for build orientation).
- Removal of overhangs below the self-supporting angle.

**Examples:** Aircraft bracket (35% weight reduction), satellite antenna support (40% lighter), Formula 1 suspension upright (organic optimized strut).

*(7 marks — 3 for Part Consolidation with example, 4 for Topology Optimization with method and example)*

#### Part (b) — [7 Marks] Cost Calculation

**Model Solution:**

Given Data:
- $V_{part} = 50\ \text{cm}^3$
- $C_m = \text{Rs } 8000/\text{kg}$
- $\rho = 4.43\ \text{g/cm}^3$
- $t_{build} = 11\ \text{hr}$
- $C_{mach} = \text{Rs } 2500/\text{hr}$
- $C_{setup} = \text{Rs } 5000$

**Cost Model:**

$$
C_{part} = C_m \cdot \rho \cdot V_{part} + C_{mach} \cdot t_{build} + C_{setup}
$$

**Step 1 — Convert Powder Cost to Rs/g:**

$$
C_m = \frac{8000}{1000} = \text{Rs } 8\ \text{per gram}
$$

**Step 2 — Material Cost:**

$$
C_{material} = 8 \times 4.43 \times 50
$$

$$
C_{material} = \text{Rs } 1772
$$

**Step 3 — Machine Cost:**

$$
C_{machine} = 2500 \times 11 = \text{Rs } 27{,}500
$$

**Step 4 — Total Cost:**

$$
C_{part} = 1772 + 27{,}500 + 5000
$$

$$
C_{part} = \text{Rs } 34{,}272
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

The machine time dominates the total cost (≈80.2%). To reduce per-part cost, the DfAM strategy should focus on:
- **Minimizing build height** (reduces $t_{build}$ linearly).
- **Nesting multiple parts in one build** (amortizes $C_{setup}$).
- **Using hollow or lattice internals** (reduces material cost without compromising function).

*(7 marks — 2 for cost model statement, 1 for unit conversion, 1 each for material/machine/setup cost calculation, 1 for total, 1 for DfAM-based interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the angle of inclination:** The angle θ in the staircase formula is measured from the **horizontal**, not from the vertical. Students often use $\sin\theta$ instead of $\cos\theta$ and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors:** Layer thickness in µm must be converted to mm before substituting; density must be in consistent units (g/cm³) with volume.
> 3. **Forgetting setup cost:** Many students omit $C_{setup}$ from the cost model. Examiners specifically test this.
> 4. **Defining "self-supporting angle" incorrectly:** It is the angle from the **vertical**, not the horizontal. A 45° overhang from the vertical means the surface tilts 45° outward.
> 5. **Confusing topology optimization with shape optimization:** Topology optimization *redistributes material in the design space*; shape optimization only adjusts boundaries of an existing topology. Examiners will deduct 1 mark for this distinction.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **DfAM** = designing explicitly to leverage AM's layer-wise, free-form fabrication; the opposite philosophy to DfM.
- **Complexity is free in AM**; cost scales with build volume and height, not with geometric intricacy.
- **Five pillars of DfAM:** Complexity, Consolidation, Multi-material, Customization, Lightweighting.
- **Self-supporting angle ≈ 45°** from the vertical for SLM; surfaces below this require support structures.
- **Minimum wall thickness:** 0.3 mm (steel), 0.4 mm (titanium). **Minimum hole diameter:** ≥ 0.5 mm.
- **Hollow parts need drain holes** (≥ 4 mm) for unfused-powder removal.
- **Staircase roughness:** $R_a \approx h_{layer} / (2 \cos\theta)$ — orient cosmetic surfaces vertically.
- **Build time model:** $t_{build} = (H_{total} / h_{layer}) \times (t_{scan} + t_{recoat} + t_{lower})$.
- **Cost model:** $C_{part} = C_m \rho V_{part} + C_{mach} t_{build} + C_{setup}$ — **machine time dominates** (~80% of cost).
- **Topology optimization** removes low-stress material; AM is the only practical way to manufacture the resulting organic, lattice-rich shapes.
- **Part consolidation** reduces assembly cost, weight, and failure modes (e.g., GE LEAP nozzle: 18 parts → 1 part).
- **Anisotropy:** Z-direction properties slightly lower than X-Y; align critical loads in-plane where possible.
- **Design rules checklist:** wall thickness, self-supporting angle, hole sizes, drain holes, escape holes, tolerances, support requirement — validate every part before slicing.
- **Energy density** governs melt-pool stability: $E = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t)$.
- **Industry applications:** Aerospace brackets, medical implants, conformal-cooled tooling, optimized heat exchangers, monolithic rocket thrust chambers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Topology Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET867 - METAL ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Topology Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Topology Optimization

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Topology Optimization (TO)** is a **mathematical, computer-aided design methodology** that determines the optimal distribution of material within a prescribed **design domain** ($\Omega$), subject to prescribed **loads** ($F$), **boundary conditions** ($u$), and **performance constraints**, by varying the **topology** (i.e., the connectivity, number, and shape of holes) of the structure — *not merely its dimensions*.

In the specific context of the **Metal Additive Manufacturing (MAM)** course, Topology Optimization is the **digital front-end engine** that drives the geometric freedom of AM. Because AM processes (SLM, DMLS, EBM) can fabricate **virtually any geometry that can be sliced**, TO becomes the natural design companion to exploit this freedom.

The general problem statement, in canonical notation used across the KTU 2024 MAM syllabus, is:

$$\text{Find } \chi(\mathbf{x}) \in \{0, 1\} \quad \text{that minimizes } c(\mathbf{u}) = \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{u}$$

subject to:

$$V(\chi) \leq V^*, \quad \mathbf{K}(\chi)\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{F}, \quad \text{and physical validity constraints.}$$

Where:
- $\chi(\mathbf{x})$ = **characteristic function** (1 = material present, 0 = void)
- $c(\mathbf{u})$ = **structural compliance** (measure of flexibility, lower = stiffer)
- $V^*$ = **allowable volume fraction** (e.g., 0.4 = use 40% of the original volume)
- $\mathbf{K}$ = global stiffness matrix.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Sculptor Architect":** Imagine you are given a *solid block of marble* ($\Omega$) shaped like a rectangular table, and you are told to design a **chair leg** that must support a **100 kg load** without breaking. A traditional engineer would simply pick a *cross-section* (circle, square) and calculate its size. A topology optimizer, however, *removes marble from every region where stress is low*, leaving a **bone-like, organic shape** — just like a tree trunk, a femur, or an aircraft bracket. The result is **minimum mass, maximum stiffness, with a shape that no human would draw by hand**. The reason this is revolutionary in MAM is that traditional CNC machining **cannot make this organic part** — but a **laser powder-bed fusion (LPBF)** machine *can*.

## 1.3 Why Topology Optimization is Indispensable to Metal AM

Three pillars link TO directly to the KTU MAM syllabus:

| Pillar | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|
| **Geometric Freedom** | AM imposes no shape penalty; TO produces lattice-filled, organic parts only AM can print. |
| **Mass Reduction** | Aerospace brackets achieve **40–70 % mass savings** using TO + AM (e.g., Airbus A350 cabin bracket). |
| **Multi-physics Performance** | TO can be coupled with **thermal, fluid, and fatigue** objectives — critical for jet engine and biomedical implants. |

## 1.4 Three Hierarchies of Structural Optimization (Foundational Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU syllabus requires students to **distinguish TO from Sizing and Shape Optimization**. Memorize this hierarchy:

1. **Sizing Optimization** — Optimizes *dimensions* (e.g., beam thickness $t$). Geometry is fixed.
2. **Shape Optimization** — Optimizes *boundary curves* (e.g., fillet radius $r$). Topology is fixed.
3. **Topology Optimization** — Optimizes *connectivity and material layout*. **Number of holes, their position, and existence** are variables.

This hierarchy is the **single most-tested conceptual question** in KTU Module 2 vivas.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Landscape of TO Methods

Topology Optimization is solved by discretizing the design domain into a **finite element mesh** and assigning each element a **design variable**. The three principal KTU-relevant families are:

### 2.1.1 Density-Based Methods (SIMP)
- Each element $e$ is assigned a **relative density** $\rho_e \in [0, 1]$.
- A **penalization power** $p$ (typically $p=3$) drives intermediate densities toward 0 or 1.
- This is the **most common industrial and exam-relevant method**.

### 2.1.2 Level-Set Method
- The boundary of the structure is described by a **level-set function** $\phi(\mathbf{x}) = 0$.
- Material exists where $\phi(\mathbf{x}) > 0$; void exists where $\phi(\mathbf{x}) < 0$.
- Gives **crisp, manufacturing-ready boundaries** — favored in research AM workflows.

### 2.1.3 Evolutionary Structural Optimization (ESO)
- Elements with **lowest strain energy** are progressively removed.
- Intuitive and easy to implement; predecessor of modern TO.

## 2.2 The SIMP Formulation (Detailed Logic Steps)

The **Solid Isotropic Material with Penalization** scheme, introduced by Bendsøe (1989) and Rozvany, is the **gold standard** for KTU exam answers on TO. The procedure follows these steps:

**Step 1 — Define the design variable.** Assign $\rho_e \in [\rho_{\min}, 1]$ to each finite element, where $\rho_{\min} = 10^{-3}$ (avoids singular stiffness).

**Step 2 — Interpolate material properties.** The Young's modulus is interpolated via:

$$E_e(\rho_e) = \rho_e^p \, E_0$$

where $E_0$ is the solid material modulus and $p \geq 3$ is the penalty exponent.

**Step 3 — State the compliance minimization problem.**

$$\text{Minimize } c(\boldsymbol{\rho}) = \sum_{e=1}^{N} (1 - p) \rho_e^p \mathbf{u}_e^T \mathbf{k}_0 \mathbf{u}_e$$

Wait — the *correct* form, after applying the SIMP interpolation to the stiffness, simplifies to the standard compliance:

$$c = \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{u}^T \mathbf{K}\mathbf{u} = \sum_{e=1}^{N} \rho_e^p \, \mathbf{u}_e^T \mathbf{k}_0 \mathbf{u}_e$$

**Step 4 — Apply the volume constraint.**

$$g(\boldsymbol{\rho}) = \sum_{e=1}^{N} v_e \rho_e - V^* \leq 0$$

**Step 5 — Apply bounds and solve** using Optimality Criteria (OC) or a Mathematical Programming method (MMA).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **minimum set of equations** a KTU 2024 student must memorize for any numerical or derivation question on Topology Optimization.

| # | Formula / Concept | Description / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_e(\rho_e) = \rho_e^p \, E_0$ | SIMP material interpolation. Used to link density to stiffness in compliance problems. |
| 2 | $c = \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{u}^T \mathbf{K}\mathbf{u}$ | Structural compliance (twice the strain energy). **Objective function** in stiffness design. |
| 3 | $V = \sum_e v_e \rho_e \leq f \cdot V_0$ | Volume fraction constraint. $f$ is typically **0.3 to 0.5** in aerospace AM parts. |
| 4 | $\frac{\partial c}{\partial \rho_e} = -p \rho_e^{p-1} \mathbf{u}_e^T \mathbf{k}_0 \mathbf{u}_e$ | Sensitivity of compliance w.r.t. density (note: sign convention is often taken as negative of the strain energy). Used in OC update. |
| 5 | $B_e = \frac{1}{p} \frac{\partial c / \partial \rho_e}{\lambda \, \partial V / \partial \rho_e}$ | Optimality Criteria (OC) ratio. Convergence criterion: $B_e \to 1$ for active elements. |
| 6 | $\rho_e^{\text{new}} = B_e^\eta \rho_e^{\text{old}}$ | Density update law with damping factor $\eta \in [0.3, 0.5]$. |
| 7 | $\text{Mass Reduction} = \frac{m_0 - m_{\text{opt}}}{m_0} \times 100\%$ | Performance metric. Airbus reports **> 55 %** on TO+AM cabin brackets. |
| 8 | $\sigma_{\text{vM}}(\mathbf{x}) \leq \sigma_{\text{yield}}$ | Local stress constraint (extension of SIMP for stress-constrained TO). |
| 9 | $\phi(\mathbf{x}, t) = 0$ | Level-set iso-surface. Material where $\phi > 0$. |
| 10 | $k_{xx} = \frac{EA}{L}, \; k_{yy} = \frac{12EI}{L^3}$ | Truss element stiffness — used in *ground structure* (analytical) TO. |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Mapping for KTU Viva Preparation:**
> - **Aerospace (GE Aviation, Airbus):** LEAP fuel nozzle — 20 parts consolidated into 1, **25 % lighter**, made by DMLS after TO.
> - **Automotive (Bugatti, BMW):** Topology-optimized brake calipers, topologically optimized chassis nodes.
> - **Biomedical (Stryker, Adler Ortho):** TO + lattice structures produce **bone-mimicking orthopedic implants** with controlled elastic modulus ($E \approx 15$ GPa vs. Ti6Al4V bulk $E \approx 110$ GPa — the **stress-shielding problem**).
> - **Generative Design** in Autodesk Fusion 360 / nTopology is essentially TO under the hood.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides the **complete analytical derivation** of the SIMP sensitivity and the Optimality Criteria update. These derivations are **high-yield 14-mark questions** in KTU ESE.

## 3.1 Derivation: Compliance Sensitivity in SIMP

**Given:** Stiffness matrix interpolation
$$ \mathbf{K}(\boldsymbol{\rho}) = \sum_{e=1}^{N} \rho_e^p \, \mathbf{k}_{0,e} $$

where $\mathbf{k}_{0,e}$ is the element stiffness matrix of element $e$ evaluated at **unit density** ($\rho_e = 1$).

**Goal:** Derive the sensitivity $\dfrac{\partial c}{\partial \rho_e}$ where $c = \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{u}$.

### Step 1 — Write the compliance in element-wise form

Since the global stiffness is a sum over elements, the compliance becomes:

$$ c = \mathbf{u}^T \mathbf{K} \mathbf{u} = \sum_{e=1}^{N} \rho_e^p \, \mathbf{u}_e^T \mathbf{k}_{0,e} \mathbf{u}_e $$

This is a direct consequence of the *element assembly rule* in finite elements.

### Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $\rho_e$

Treating $\mathbf{u}_e$ as *implicitly* dependent on $\rho_e$ (since $\mathbf{K}$ depends on $\rho_e$ and $\mathbf{K}\mathbf{u}=\mathbf{F}$ is fixed), we use the adjoint method. For an exact derivation, start with:

$$ c = \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{u}^T \mathbf{K} \mathbf{u} $$

Differentiating both sides:

$$ \frac{\partial c}{\partial \rho_e} = 2 \mathbf{u}^T \frac{\partial \mathbf{K}}{\partial \rho_e} \mathbf{u} + \mathbf{u}^T \mathbf{K} \frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial \rho_e} $$

The factor of 2 comes from the symmetry $\mathbf{K} = \mathbf{K}^T$.

### Step 3 — Evaluate $\frac{\partial \mathbf{K}}{\partial \rho_e}$

From the SIMP rule $\mathbf{K} = \sum_e \rho_e^p \mathbf{k}_{0,e}$, differentiation gives:

$$ \frac{\partial \mathbf{K}}{\partial \rho_e} = p \, \rho_e^{p-1} \, \mathbf{k}_{0,e} $$

This is the **chain rule applied to a power-law interpolation** — a key exam step.

### Step 4 — Eliminate $\frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial \rho_e}$ using equilibrium

From $\mathbf{K}\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{F}$, differentiate:

$$ \frac{\partial \mathbf{K}}{\partial \rho_e} \mathbf{u} + \mathbf{K} \frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial \rho_e} = 0 $$

Multiply from the left by $\mathbf{u}^T$:

$$ \mathbf{u}^T \frac{\partial \mathbf{K}}{\partial \rho_e} \mathbf{u} + \mathbf{u}^T \mathbf{K} \frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial \rho_e} = 0 $$

Solve for the implicit term:

$$ \mathbf{u}^T \mathbf{K} \frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial \rho_e} = -\mathbf{u}^T \frac{\partial \mathbf{K}}{\partial \rho_e} \mathbf{u} $$

### Step 5 — Substitute back into the compliance derivative

$$ \frac{\partial c}{\partial \rho_e} = 2 \mathbf{u}^T \frac{\partial \mathbf{K}}{\partial \rho_e} \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{u}^T \frac{\partial \mathbf{K}}{\partial \rho_e} \mathbf{u} $$

$$ \frac{\partial c}{\partial \rho_e} = \mathbf{u}^T \frac{\partial \mathbf{K}}{\partial \rho_e} \mathbf{u} $$

### Step 6 — Insert the SIMP derivative of $\mathbf{K}$

$$ \frac{\partial c}{\partial \rho_e} = p \, \rho_e^{p-1} \, \mathbf{u}_e^T \mathbf{k}_{0,e} \, \mathbf{u}_e $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam-Ready Result:**
> $$ \boxed{\frac{\partial c}{\partial \rho_e} = -p \, \rho_e^{p-1} \, \mathbf{u}_e^T \mathbf{k}_{0,e} \, \mathbf{u}_e} $$
>
> *(The sign is conventionally written as negative when $c$ is defined as compliance to be minimized; the negative indicates that increasing $\rho_e$ decreases compliance, i.e., increases stiffness.)*

## 3.2 Derivation: Optimality Criteria (OC) Update Law

The OC method solves the constrained problem using the **Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions**. For the problem:

$$ \min c(\boldsymbol{\rho}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad V(\boldsymbol{\rho}) \leq V^* $$

the Lagrangian is:

$$ \mathcal{L} = c(\boldsymbol{\rho}) + \lambda \left( V(\boldsymbol{\rho}) - V^* \right) $$

Setting $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial \rho_e = 0$ at optimality:

$$ \frac{\partial c}{\partial \rho_e} + \lambda \frac{\partial V}{\partial \rho_e} = 0 $$

Solving for $\rho_e$ assuming a separable, convex-like interpolation:

$$ \rho_e^* = \left( -\frac{\lambda \, \partial V / \partial \rho_e}{\partial c / \partial \rho_e} \right)^{1/p} $$

Defining the OC ratio:

$$ B_e = \frac{-\frac{\partial c}{\partial \rho_e}}{\lambda \, \frac{\partial V}{\partial \rho_e}} $$

The discrete update rule, with damping $\eta$, is:

$$ \rho_e^{\text{new}} = \begin{cases} \max(\rho_{\min}, \, \rho_e - m) & \text{if } \rho_e B_e^\eta \leq \max(\rho_{\min}, \rho_e - m) \\ \rho_e B_e^\eta & \text{if } \max(\rho_{\min}, \rho_e - m) < \rho_e B_e^\eta < \min(1, \rho_e + m) \\ \min(1, \, \rho_e + m) & \text{if } \min(1, \rho_e + m) \leq \rho_e B_e^\eta \end{cases} $$

Here $m$ is a **move limit** (typically $0.2$) to prevent oscillations.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style — 7 Mark Sub-Question)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A cantilever beam of length $L = 1\,\text{m}$, height $H = 0.1\,\text{m}$, modulus $E_0 = 200\,\text{GPa}$, is meshed into $N = 100$ elements. A tip load $P = 1000\,\text{N}$ acts downward. Volume constraint: $V^* = 0.4 \, V_0$. Compute the *initial compliance* $c_0$ analytically using Euler-Bernoulli beam theory, then the *target* compliance improvement ratio after TO.

**Analytical compliance of original beam:**

$$ c_0 = \int_0^L \frac{M^2(x)}{2 E_0 I} \, dx $$

For a cantilever with tip load $P$, $M(x) = P(L-x)$ and $I = \frac{bH^3}{12}$ for width $b=0.05\,\text{m}$.

$$ c_0 = \int_0^L \frac{P^2 (L-x)^2}{2 E_0 I} \, dx = \frac{P^2 L^3}{6 E_0 I} $$

Substituting values:

$$ c_0 = \frac{(1000)^2 (1)^3}{6 \times (200 \times 10^9) \times \left( \frac{0.05 \times (0.1)^3}{12} \right)} = \frac{10^6}{6 \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times 4.167 \times 10^{-6}} $$

$$ c_0 \approx 2.0 \times 10^{-1} \, \text{J} = 0.2 \, \text{N·m} $$

**After TO (40 % volume):** A well-converged SIMP run typically yields $c_{\text{opt}} \approx 1.6 \, c_0$ at 40 % mass — meaning *stiffness per unit mass* improves dramatically.

**Performance Metric (specific stiffness ratio):**

$$ \eta_{\text{perf}} = \frac{c_0 / m_0}{c_{\text{opt}} / m_{\text{opt}}} = \frac{0.2 / 1.0}{0.32 / 0.4} = \frac{0.2}{0.8} = 0.25 $$

This shows that the **specific stiffness is 4× better** for the optimized part — the engineering motivation behind AM+TO.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the TO+AM Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Design Domain Omega] --> B[FE Meshing]
    B --> C[Initial Density rho_e = 1]
    C --> D[SIMP Interpolation E = rho_p E0]
    D --> E[FE Analysis K u = F]
    E --> F[Sensitivity Computation dc/drho]
    F --> G{OC Convergence Check}
    G -- Not Converged --> H[Lagrange Multiplier Update]
    H --> I[Density Update rho_new = B_eta rho_old]
    I --> E
    G -- Converged --> J[Filtered and Thresholded Topology]
    J --> K[CAD Surface Reconstruction]
    K --> L[STL Slicing]
    L --> M[LPBF or EBM Build]
    M --> N[Post Processing HIP and Machining]
    N --> O[Final Topology Optimized AM Part]
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Structural Optimization (Mermaid Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SO[Structural Optimization] --> SZ[Sizing Optimization]
    SO --> SH[Shape Optimization]
    SO --> TP[Topology Optimization]
    SZ --> SZ1[Beam thickness t]
    SZ --> SZ2[Cross section A]
    SH --> SH1[Boundary curves]
    SH --> SH2[Fillet radius r]
    TP --> TP1[Material layout chi x]
    TP --> TP2[Number of holes]
    TP --> TP3[Connectivity]
    TP1 --> M[AM Compatible Output]
    TP2 --> M
    TP3 --> M
```

## 4.3 The TO Algorithm Loop (Detailed Computational Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Step 1 Initialize rho_e uniformly to 1] --> Q[Step 2 Loop Counter k equals 0]
    Q --> R[Step 3 FE Solve K rho k times u k equals F]
    R --> S[Step 4 Compute compliance c k equals u T K u]
    S --> T[Step 5 Compute sensitivity dc by d rho_e]
    T --> U[Step 6 Apply filtering and projection]
    U --> V[Step 7 Update lambda via bisection]
    V --> W[Step 8 Compute OC ratio B_e]
    W --> X{Step 9 Check convergence}
    X -- No --> Y[Step 10 Update densities]
    Y --> R
    X -- Yes --> Z[Step 11 Output binary topology]
```

## 4.4 Bridge Between TO Output and AM Manufacturability

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw TO Result 0 1 Greyscale] --> B[Minimum Size Control Filter]
    B --> C[Projection Threshold rmin to rmax]
    C --> D[Connected Feature Removal]
    D --> E[Overhang and Self Support Check]
    E --> F{Printable without Supports}
    F -- Yes --> G[Direct STL Generation]
    F -- No --> H[Multi Axis Repositioning or Sacrificial Supports]
    H --> G
    G --> I[LPBF or DMLS Print]
    I --> J[Finished Topology Optimized Part]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Hint for KTU 2024 Students:** Every block in the diagram above corresponds to a **specific code module** in commercial TO software (Altair Inspire, ANSYS Mechanical, Autodesk Generative Design). Knowing this pipeline is the difference between a **6-mark and 14-mark answer**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between **shape optimization** and **topology optimization** with one example relevant to additive manufacturing.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Shape Optimization | Topology Optimization |
|---|---|---|
| Design Variable | Boundary curves, fillet radii | Material distribution $\rho(\mathbf{x})$ |
| Topology Change | **Not allowed** | **Allowed** (holes can appear/vanish) |
| AM Example | Smoothing fillet of a 3D-printed bracket | Generative design of an aerospace bracket with internal lattice |

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** State the **SIMP interpolation law** and explain the role of the **penalty exponent** $p$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Solid Isotropic Material with Penalization law interpolates Young's modulus as $E(\rho) = \rho^p E_0$. The penalty exponent $p \geq 3$ drives intermediate densities (which have no physical meaning) toward either 0 (void) or 1 (solid), producing a **black-and-white, manufacturing-ready topology**. Without penalization, the optimizer fills the design space with grey (semi-dense) elements that cannot be fabricated.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**

> **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **step-by-step procedure of topology optimization** using the SIMP method for a cantilever bracket to be fabricated by **LPBF (Laser Powder Bed Fusion)**. Highlight how the output is converted into a printable STL file. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **compliance sensitivity** $\partial c / \partial \rho_e$ for the SIMP method starting from the global equilibrium equation. State the role of the **Lagrange multiplier** $\lambda$ in the volume constraint. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define the design domain** $\Omega$ (a rectangular region surrounding the expected bracket shape), the **boundary conditions** (fixed bolt holes, downward load $P$ at the tip), and the **volume fraction** $f = 0.4$.

**Step 2 — Mesh** $\Omega$ into ~50,000 tetrahedral elements for LPBF-scale resolution.

**Step 3 — Initialize** $\rho_e = 1$ for all $e$.

**Step 4 — Iterate** the SIMP loop (see Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3):
- Solve $\mathbf{K}(\boldsymbol{\rho})\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{F}$.
- Compute $c = \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{u}$.
- Compute $\partial c / \partial \rho_e = -p \rho_e^{p-1} \mathbf{u}_e^T \mathbf{k}_{0,e} \mathbf{u}_e$.
- Update densities using OC: $\rho_e^{\text{new}} = B_e^\eta \rho_e^{\text{old}}$ with move limit $m=0.2$.

**Step 5 — Apply manufacturability filters:**
- *Minimum member size* filter (radius $r_{\min} = 0.8$ mm — limited by LPBF laser spot size).
- *Overhang angle* check (must be > 45° from vertical to avoid support structures).
- *Connectedness* and *escape hole* check (un-melted powder removal).

**Step 6 — Convert** the 0/1 voxel field to a smooth **STL triangulation** using marching cubes algorithm.

**Step 7 — Slice** the STL into 30 µm layers, generate scan paths (hatching + contour), and print on an LPBF machine (e.g., EOS M290).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The full derivation is presented in **Section 3.1** above. Key results:

**Governing equation** $\mathbf{K}(\boldsymbol{\rho})\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{F}$

**SIMP law** $\mathbf{K} = \sum_e \rho_e^p \mathbf{k}_{0,e}$

**Sensitivity** (with sign convention for minimization):

$$ \frac{\partial c}{\partial \rho_e} = -p \, \rho_e^{p-1} \, \mathbf{u}_e^T \mathbf{k}_{0,e} \, \mathbf{u}_e $$

**Role of Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$:**
- Introduced to enforce the **volume constraint** $V \leq V^*$.
- Acts as a **"price"** of material: higher $\lambda$ means the optimizer must be given a strong compliance benefit to justify using more material.
- At optimum, $\lambda$ is found by **bisection** such that the volume constraint is exactly active.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **(a)** Discuss the **role of topology optimization in metal additive manufacturing**. List **four specific applications** in aerospace, automotive, and biomedical fields with quantitative outcomes. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A bracket design domain of $V_0 = 5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3$ must support a static load with maximum compliance $c_{\max} = 5\,\text{N·m}$. The solid material has $E_0 = 110\,\text{GPa}$. If the volume fraction is reduced to $f = 0.35$ by TO, compute the **final mass reduction percentage** (assume Ti6Al4V density $\rho_m = 4430\,\text{kg/m}^3$). Comment on the suitability of the part for LPBF. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Topology optimization is the **conceptual enabler** of AM because it exploits **geometric freedom without manufacturing penalty**. Four specific applications:

1. **Airbus A350 Cabin Bracket** — TO + DMLS; **55 % mass saving** (1.1 kg → 0.5 kg); part count consolidated.
2. **GE LEAP Fuel Nozzle** — 20 parts welded together replaced by 1 TO+AM nozzle; **25 % lighter**, 5× more durable.
3. **Bugatti Brake Caliper** — TO+SLM in Ti6Al4V; **41 % mass reduction** (4.9 kg → 2.9 kg); holds 1250 kN braking force.
4. **Stryker Acetabular Cup** (hip implant) — TO+SLM with lattice gyroid; modulus tuned to **15 GPa** to match cortical bone, eliminating stress shielding.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $V_0 = 5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3$, $f = 0.35$, $\rho_m = 4430\,\text{kg/m}^3$.

**Final volume:**
$$ V_f = f \cdot V_0 = 0.35 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 1.75 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3 $$

**Final mass:**
$$ m_f = \rho_m \cdot V_f = 4430 \times 1.75 \times 10^{-4} = 0.775\,\text{kg} $$

**Initial mass:**
$$ m_0 = 4430 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 2.215\,\text{kg} $$

**Mass reduction percentage:**
$$ \Delta m \% = \frac{m_0 - m_f}{m_0} \times 100\% = \frac{2.215 - 0.775}{2.215} \times 100\% \approx 65\% $$

**LPBF Suitability Comment:**
A 65 % mass reduction is **realistic and desirable** for a Ti6Al4V bracket. However, the TO output must be **filtered for minimum feature size** ($\geq 0.3$ mm for LPBF on EOS machines) and **overhang angle** ($\geq 45°$). Internal lattice cells should be **open and interconnected** to allow powder removal via tapping/blast holes — otherwise unmelted powder becomes a **fatal defect** in flight-critical aerospace use.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing TO with generative design** — TO is the *mathematical* core; Generative Design is the *software framework* wrapping TO with manufacturability rules. Do not use them interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the sign** in $\partial c / \partial \rho_e$ — KTU examiners will deduct 1 mark for an incorrect sign convention.
> 3. **Skipping the FE step** — TO is *not* a closed-form analytical method for arbitrary geometries. Always mention that a finite element solve is required at each iteration.
> 4. **Ignoring AM-specific filters** (overhang, minimum size, escape holes) — A TO result *without* manufacturability constraints is **not an AM-ready part**. Examiners frequently test this.
> 5. **Citing the wrong penalty exponent** — Use $p \geq 3$ for SIMP; $p=1$ gives a non-penalized, grey result that is unprintable.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition**: TO optimizes *material layout* (where material exists), not just *shape* (where the boundary is) or *size* (how thick the member is).
- **SIMP Law**: $E(\rho) = \rho^p E_0$, with $p = 3$ standard. Drives greys to black-and-white.
- **Compliance objective**: $c = \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{u}$ — minimize to maximize stiffness.
- **Volume constraint**: $V \leq f \cdot V_0$ — typical $f$ is **0.3–0.5** in AM aerospace parts.
- **Sensitivity formula**: $\dfrac{\partial c}{\partial \rho_e} = -p \rho_e^{p-1} \mathbf{u}_e^T \mathbf{k}_0 \mathbf{u}_e$.
- **Optimality Criterion (OC) update**: $\rho_e^{\text{new}} = B_e^\eta \rho_e^{\text{old}}$ with damping $\eta \in [0.3, 0.5]$ and move limit $m \approx 0.2$.
- **Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$**: enforces the volume constraint as a "shadow price" of material.
- **Level-set alternative**: $\phi(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ defines the structure boundary — crisp, no grey.
- **ESO (Evolutionary)**: removes low-strain-energy elements iteratively — predecessor method.
- **AM-specific filters** (must know!): minimum member size, overhang angle ≥ 45°, connected porosity, escape holes for powder removal.
- **Industrial giants of TO+AM**: Airbus A350 bracket, GE LEAP nozzle, Bugatti caliper, Stryker implants.
- **Software tools**: Altair Inspire, ANSYS Mechanical, Autodesk Generative Design, nTopology, Altair OptiStruct.
- **Specific stiffness** (stiffness per unit mass) is the correct performance metric — not raw stiffness.
- **Volume fraction 0.4 + 65 % mass reduction** is a typical KTU numerical benchmark answer.
- **KTU Module 2 emphasis**: TO bridges design and AM — questions will *always* ask for the AM manufacturability link.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metal AM Physics and Processes - PBF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET867 - METAL ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Metal AM Physics and Processes - PBF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 – Metal AM Physics and Processes: Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

**Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** is a metal additive manufacturing process family defined under **ISO/ASTM 52900 (2021)** in which a thermal energy source (laser or electron beam) selectively fuses regions of a pre-deposited powder bed, layer-by-layer, to build a fully dense metallic part. For engineering metals, PBF operates in two principal variants:

- **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** / **Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS)** — uses a focused **fiber laser** (typically **1070 nm wavelength**, near-infrared), operating under an **inert atmosphere** (Argon or Nitrogen) at residual O₂ levels below **1000 ppm** (often **<100 ppm**).
- **Electron Beam Melting (EBM)** — uses a **high-energy electron beam** (typically **60 kV accelerating voltage**) under **high vacuum** ($\leq 10^{-4}$ mbar), with powder pre-heated to elevated temperatures (often **650–750 °C** for Ti-6Al-4V).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Terminology Note:** The KTU syllabus uses "SLM" and "DMLS" interchangeably with the umbrella term **Laser Powder Bed Fusion (L-PBF)**. For examination purposes, students must distinguish L-PBF from EBM on the basis of **energy source, atmosphere, and preheat strategy**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "3D Welding Printer" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> Imagine a **conventional inkjet printer** that prints 2D pages one sheet at a time. Now replace the paper feed with a roller that lays down an extremely thin layer of **table salt (metal powder)**, and replace the ink cartridge with a **welding torch (laser)** that draws each cross-section by melting only the powder outline. The roller then deposits the next salt layer, and the torch draws the next cross-section — until a full 3D metal object emerges from the powder bed, which is later brushed away. The unmelted powder acts like **temporary support** (like scaffolding on a building) and is fully recycled.

**Key takeaways from the analogy:**

| Analogy Element | PBF Process Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Salt (powder) | Atomized metal powder (typically **15–45 µm** for SLM, **45–105 µm** for EBM) |
| Ink / Torch | Focused laser (L-PBF) or electron beam (EBM) |
| Paper feed roller | **Recoater blade or roller** depositing each layer |
| Page = cross-section | **2D slice (≈ 20–100 µm thick)** of the CAD model |
| Scaffolding | **Unmelted powder bed** supporting overhangs |

### 1.3 Why PBF Dominates Industrial Metal AM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PBF delivers the **highest part density** (typically **≥ 99.5 %** of theoretical density, often **> 99.9 %** for Ti-6Al-4V under optimized parameters) and the **best surface finish** (Ra ≈ **5–20 µm** as-built for SLM) among all metal AM routes, making it the process of choice for **aerospace, medical implants, and high-value tooling**.

Three physical reasons for this dominance:
1. **Confined melt pool** — powder bed acts as a thermal insulator, lowering heat loss.
2. **Small spot size** — laser/EB can focus to **≤ 100 µm**, enabling fine features.
3. **Layer-wise solidification control** — directional heat extraction yields columnar grains.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sub-Process Taxonomy (L-PBF vs EBM)

| Feature | L-PBF (SLM / DMLS) | EBM |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Fiber laser (≈ **200 W – 1 kW**) | Electron beam (≈ **3 kW**, 60 kV) |
| Atmosphere | Inert gas (Ar / N₂) | High vacuum |
| Powder size | **15–45 µm** | **45–105 µm** |
| Preheat | Optional (~ **100–200 °C**) | Mandatory (~ **650–750 °C**) |
| Build speed | Slower (per layer) | Faster (high scan speed) |
| Residual stress | Higher | Lower (due to preheat) |
| Surface roughness (Ra) | **5–20 µm** | **20–40 µm** |
| Materials | Steels, Al, Ti, Inconel, Cu | Mainly Ti and some steels |

## 2.2 The Five-Pillar Physics of PBF

### Pillar 1: Laser–Material Interaction (Beer–Lambert Absorption)

When the laser strikes the powder bed, energy is absorbed over a finite depth:

$$I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha z}$$

Where:
- $I_0$ = incident laser intensity (W/m²)
- $z$ = depth into the material (m)
- $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient (m⁻¹) — function of wavelength and material

For metals at the typical **1070 nm fiber laser wavelength**, $\alpha \approx 10^7 \text{ m}^{-1}$, meaning the absorption depth is only **≈ 0.1 µm** — predominantly **surface absorption**. This is why the powder surface is treated as an **opaque grey body** with reflectivity $R \approx 0.3$–$0.6$ for most engineering metals at this wavelength.

### Pillar 2: Melt Pool Dynamics

Four coupled forces drive the melt pool:

1. **Recoil pressure ($P_r$)** — vaporization pushes liquid metal downward, depressing the surface.
2. **Marangoni (surface-tension-gradient) flow** — hotter liquid has lower surface tension, driving **outward flow** when $\partial \gamma / \partial T < 0$ (most metals).
3. **Buoyancy** — natural convection due to density difference.
4. **Aerodynamic drag** — for EBM with metal vapor.

The dimensionless **Marangoni number** quantifies the dominance of thermocapillary flow:

$$Ma = -\frac{\partial \gamma}{\partial T} \cdot \frac{\Delta T \cdot L}{\mu \cdot \alpha_{th}}$$

Where $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity, $\alpha_{th}$ is thermal diffusivity, $L$ is a characteristic length, and $\partial \gamma / \partial T$ is the surface tension gradient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** If sulfur or oxygen is dissolved in the melt, $\partial \gamma / \partial T$ becomes **positive**, **reversing** Marangoni flow inward — this is the principle behind active oxide control in some commercial L-PBF machines.

### Pillar 3: Volumetric Energy Density (VED)

The single most important KTU formula:

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

Where:
- $E_v$ = volumetric energy density (J/mm³)
- $P$ = laser/beam power (**W**)
- $v$ = scan speed (**mm/s**)
- $h$ = hatch spacing (**mm**)
- $t$ = layer thickness (**mm**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical KTU benchmark values for Ti-6Al-4V in SLM:**
> $E_v$ between **60–120 J/mm³** yields densities > 99.5 %. Below ~ 40 J/mm³ → lack-of-fusion porosity; above ~ 200 J/mm³ → keyholing and vaporization defects.

### Pillar 4: Heat Transfer Modes

| Mode | Mechanism | Dominant In |
|---|---|---|
| Conduction | Powder bed (low $k$) + solid substrate | Bulk heat flow |
| Convection | Inert gas flow (for SLM) / vacuum (no convection for EBM) | Surface cooling |
| Radiation | High-temperature melt pool surface (≥ 2000 K) | EBM, also dominant at preheat |

The effective thermal conductivity of the powder bed is governed by the **Zehner–Bauer–Schlünder (ZBS)** model:

$$k_{eff} = k_g \left[ 1 - \sqrt{1 - \phi} + \frac{\sqrt{1 - \phi}}{1} \right] + \ldots \text{(ZBS closure terms)}$$

where $\phi$ is the packing fraction (typically **0.55–0.65** for gas-atomized metal powders).

### Pillar 5: Solidification Microstructure

- **Cooling rates:** $10^3$–$10^6$ K/s (vs 1–10² K/s in casting).
- **Grain morphology:** **Columnar** (along build direction) for EBM and parameter-tuned SLM; **equiaxed** achievable with high-thermal-gradient strategy.
- **Cell spacing:** $d \approx (G \cdot R)^{-1/3}$ where $G$ = thermal gradient, $R$ = solidification rate.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a high-frequency reference for KTU PEMET867 numerical and derivations questions.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_v = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t)$ | Volumetric Energy Density | **J/mm³** |
| 2 | $E_L = P / v$ | Linear Energy Density | **J/mm** |
| 3 | $E_A = P / (v \cdot h)$ | Areal Energy Density | **J/mm²** |
| 4 | $I(z) = I_0 e^{-\alpha z}$ | Beer–Lambert absorption | W/m² |
| 5 | $\eta_a = 1 - R$ | Absorptivity at a wavelength | dimensionless |
| 6 | $A = \text{absorption depth} = 1/\alpha$ | Optical penetration depth | m |
| 7 | $\dot{Q}_{melt} = \rho \cdot V \cdot [c_p \Delta T + L_f]$ | Energy to melt a powder voxel | W |
| 8 | $d_{cell} \propto (G \cdot R)^{-1/3}$ | Cellular spacing (solidification) | µm |
| 9 | $\rho_{bulk} = \rho_{solid} \cdot \phi_{rel}$ | Powder bed bulk density | g/cm³ |
| 10 | $\Phi_{powder} = \text{tap density}/\text{true density}$ | Relative packing fraction | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\text{Build Rate} = A_{xy} \cdot t / t_{layer}$ | Volumetric build rate | cm³/h |
| 12 | $\Delta T_{substrate} = q'' \cdot \sqrt{\pi t / (k \rho c_p)}$ | Semi-infinite thermal pulse | °C |
| 13 | $P_r = 0.54 \, P_0 \, \sqrt{T/T_0}$ | Recoil pressure (Knudsen layer model) | Pa |

> [!WARNING]
> **No-pipe rule:** All "or" and "per" separators above use the slash `/`. **Never write $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table** — use $\vert x \vert$ typeset in LaTeX, or break out of the table.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Why PBF is chosen | Typical Component |
|---|---|---|
| Aerospace | Topology-optimized weight saving, part consolidation | Brackets, turbine blades (Ti-6Al-4V) |
| Medical | Patient-specific implants, porous bone interfaces | Spinal cages, dental crowns (CoCr) |
| Tooling | Conformal cooling channels impossible via CNC | Injection mold inserts (H13, maraging steel) |
| Energy | High-temperature alloys with complex internal flow | Heat exchangers (Inconel 718) |
| Automotive | Lightweight lattice structures | Formula-1 structural nodes |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volumetric Energy Density from First Principles

We begin from the definition: **energy delivered per unit volume** of fused material.

**Step 1 — Energy deposited per unit length of a single scan track:**

The laser deposits energy at a rate equal to the **net absorbed power** $\eta_a P$, where $\eta_a$ is the absorptivity at the powder surface. Over a unit length $dx = v \, dt$, the dwell time is $dt = dx / v$. Therefore:

$$
E_{length} = \frac{\eta_a \, P \, dt}{dx} = \frac{\eta_a \, P}{v}
$$

This is the **linear energy density** $E_L$ (in J/mm), and it represents energy delivered per millimetre of scan track.

**Step 2 — Multiply by overlap factor for neighbouring tracks:**

Each scan track overlaps its neighbour by the hatch spacing $h$. A unit area ($1 \text{ mm}^2$) of the melt plane is therefore covered by approximately $1/h$ scan tracks per unit length. The areal energy density becomes:

$$
E_A = \frac{E_L}{h} = \frac{\eta_a \, P}{v \, h}
$$

**Step 3 — Extend to volume by including layer thickness:**

In a build of layer thickness $t$, the volumetric energy density (energy per mm³) is:

$$
E_v = \frac{E_A}{t} = \frac{\eta_a \, P}{v \, h \, t}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering conclusion:** For a given laser, doubling the scan speed $v$ **halves** the energy density, and so does doubling the hatch spacing $h$ or the layer thickness $t$. KTU questions frequently test this proportional reasoning.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Threshold Energy Density for Continuous Track (Bearing Failure Onset)

A "continuous track" requires that the energy supplied exceeds the energy needed to:
1. Heat the powder from ambient to melting.
2. Supply the latent heat of fusion.
3. Compensate for conductive losses into the substrate and surroundings.

For a track of width $w$ and depth $d$ formed per unit length:

$$
E_L^{threshold} = \rho \cdot w \cdot d \cdot \left[ c_p (T_m - T_0) + L_f \right] / \eta_a
$$

**Step-by-step substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_L^{threshold} &= \frac{\rho \, w \, d \, c_p (T_m - T_0)}{\eta_a} + \frac{\rho \, w \, d \, L_f}{\eta_a} \\[4pt]
&= \underbrace{\frac{\rho \, w \, d \, c_p \, \Delta T_s}{\eta_a}}_{\text{sensible part}} + \underbrace{\frac{\rho \, w \, d \, L_f}{\eta_a}}_{\text{latent part}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Ti-6Al-4V** at $\rho = 4430 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 546 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $T_m - T_0 = 1627$ K, $L_f = 2.86 \times 10^5$ J/kg, $w \approx d \approx 100$ µm $= 10^{-4}$ m, $\eta_a \approx 0.4$:

- Sensible term $\approx \dfrac{4430 \cdot 10^{-8} \cdot 546 \cdot 1627}{0.4} \approx 98$ J/mm
- Latent term $\approx \dfrac{4430 \cdot 10^{-8} \cdot 2.86 \times 10^5}{0.4} \approx 31.7$ J/mm

**Total threshold** $\approx 130$ J/mm, which corresponds to a VED in the **40–80 J/mm³** range when multiplied by $1/(h \cdot t)$ factors — consistent with experimental practice.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Marangoni Velocity Scale in the Melt Pool

A useful scale for the surface-tension-driven velocity in a melt pool of width $W$ and surface temperature difference $\Delta T$:

$$
v_{Ma} = \frac{\partial \gamma / \partial T \cdot \Delta T}{\mu} \cdot \frac{W}{L_{ref}}
$$

For pure **Ti-6Al-4V** with $\partial \gamma / \partial T \approx -0.26$ mN/(m·K), $\mu \approx 5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\Delta T \approx 1500$ K, and a characteristic length $L_{ref} \approx 100$ µm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{Ma} &= \frac{-0.26 \times 10^{-3} \cdot (-1500)}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \cdot \frac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{100 \times 10^{-6}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.39}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \cdot 1 = 78 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This velocity is on the order of **tens of m/s**, confirming that Marangoni flow is the **dominant** fluid-dynamic mechanism inside the melt pool — far stronger than buoyancy for sub-millimetre pools.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Solidification Cell Spacing (KTU favourite)

From classic solidification theory, the cellular/primary dendrite arm spacing $\lambda_1$ scales as:

$$
\lambda_1 = K \cdot G^{-a} \cdot R^{-b}
$$

where $G$ is the thermal gradient (K/m), $R$ is the solidification rate (m/s), and $K, a, b$ are alloy-dependent constants. For Ti-6Al-4V, an empirical fit gives $a \approx 0.5$, $b \approx 0.3$, so:

$$
\lambda_1 \approx K \cdot G^{-0.5} \cdot R^{-0.3}
$$

**Numerical example:** With $G = 5 \times 10^6$ K/m, $R = 0.1$ m/s, and $K \approx 60$ µm·(K/m)$^{0.5}$·(m/s)$^{0.3}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_1 &\approx 60 \cdot (5 \times 10^6)^{-0.5} \cdot (0.1)^{-0.3} \\[4pt]
&\approx 60 \cdot 4.47 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 1.995 \\[4pt]
&\approx 5.3 \text{ µm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the **5–10 µm cellular width** observed experimentally in SLM Ti-6Al-4V.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Operating Window Mapping

The relationship between **VED, defects, and densification** can be summarised:

| VED Range (J/mm³) | Outcome | Dominant Defect |
|---|---|---|
| < 40 | Lack of fusion | Large irregular pores, poor bonding |
| 40 – 80 | Optimal (most alloys) | Near-full density (> 99.5 %) |
| 80 – 150 | Acceptable for Ti, steels | Minor keyhole pores |
| 150 – 250 | Keyholing regime | Spherical entrapped pores |
| > 250 | Evaporation, spatter | Cracks, balling, delamination |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Melt Pool Dynamics Physics

```mermaid
    participant Melt as Melt Pool

    Powder->>Powder: Absorb via eta_a 1 minus R
    Powder->>Melt: Heat to melting T_m
    Melt->>Melt: Recoil pressure P_r depresses surface
    Melt->>Melt: Marangoni flow redistributes liquid
    Melt->>Substrate: Conductive heat loss

    Melt-->>Melt: Cellular solidification lambda_1
```

---
