# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** and **Electron Beam Melting (EBM)** in terms of energy source, atmosphere, powder size, and preheat temperature. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | SLM | EBM |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Fiber laser (~ 1070 nm) | Electron beam (60 kV) |
| Atmosphere | Inert gas (Ar / N₂) | High vacuum |
| Powder size | 15–45 µm | 45–105 µm |
| Preheat | Up to 200 °C | 650–750 °C |
| Cooling rate | $10^5$–$10^6$ K/s | $10^3$–$10^5$ K/s |

EBM benefits from lower residual stress due to elevated preheat; SLM offers finer resolution and surface finish.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
Define **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)**. State its typical unit and the operating window for Ti-6Al-4V in SLM. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
$VED$ is the energy supplied per unit volume of consolidated material:

$$VED = E_v = \frac{P}{v \, h \, t}$$

Unit: **J/mm³**. The optimal operating window for Ti-6Al-4V in SLM is approximately **60–120 J/mm³**, yielding relative densities above 99.5 %.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the aid of a suitable schematic, describe the working principle of the **Laser Powder Bed Fusion (L-PBF)** process. List any **five (5) process parameters** that influence the quality of the built part. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** formula from first principles. Compute the VED for a build using laser power **$P = 370$ W**, scan speed **$v = 1200$ mm/s**, hatch spacing **$h = 0.12$ mm**, and layer thickness **$t = 0.03$ mm**. Identify whether this lies in the optimal processing window for Ti-6Al-4V. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working principle and parameters (7 marks):**

- The L-PBF system consists of a **fiber laser**, **scanning galvanometer**, **f-theta lens**, **build chamber filled with inert gas**, a **recoater**, a **build platform**, and a **powder hopper**.
- Process steps:
  1. STL slicing into 2D layers.
  2. Lower platform by one layer thickness.
  3. Recoater spreads a uniform powder layer.
  4. Galvanometer scans the laser across the cross-section, **melting** the powder into a solid track.
  5. Repeat layer-by-layer until completion.
  6. Cool-down, depowdering, post-processing (HIP/CNC).

- **Five process parameters:**
  1. Laser power $P$
  2. Scan speed $v$
  3. Hatch spacing $h$
  4. Layer thickness $t$
  5. Scan strategy (island/chessboard, rotation angle)

**(b) VED derivation + numerical (7 marks):**

Derivation steps as shown in **Section 3.1**:

$$
E_v = \frac{\eta_a \, P}{v \, h \, t}
$$

Taking $\eta_a \approx 0.4$ for Ti at 1070 nm (or absorbed-power-corrected by accepting the supplied $P$ as effective):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_v &= \frac{370}{1200 \times 0.12 \times 0.03} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{370}{4.32} \\[4pt]
&\approx 85.6 \text{ J/mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** 85.6 J/mm³ lies **within the optimal Ti-6Al-4V window of 60–120 J/mm³**, indicating that this parameter set is expected to yield relative density > 99.5 % with low lack-of-fusion porosity.

---

### Question B **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **physics of the melt pool** during L-PBF. Describe in detail the role of **recoil pressure** and **Marangoni convection**, and how each is governed by the relevant equation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A Ti-6Al-4V L-PBF part exhibits a melt pool of width $W = 120$ µm. Surface tension gradient is $\partial \gamma / \partial T = -0.26$ mN/(m·K), dynamic viscosity $\mu = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, and surface temperature drop $\Delta T = 1500$ K. Compute the **scale of Marangoni velocity** and comment on its dominance in the melt-pool hydrodynamics. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Melt-pool physics (7 marks):**

The melt pool is a transient, free-surface liquid metal domain with size typically 50–200 µm wide and 50–150 µm deep, lifetime of order **microseconds to milliseconds**.

- **Recoil pressure ($P_r$):** Generated by vaporization of the metal. From Knudsen layer theory:

$$
P_r = 0.54 \, P_0 \, \sqrt{T / T_0}
$$

where $P_0$ is the saturation pressure at reference temperature $T_0$. The recoil pressure **depresses the melt surface**, forming the **keyhole** when intensity exceeds a threshold.

- **Marangoni convection:** Surface tension $\gamma$ varies with temperature and active surfactant concentration (S, O). The shear stress along the free surface is:

$$
\tau_s = \frac{\partial \gamma}{\partial T} \, \nabla_s T
$$

When $\partial \gamma / \partial T < 0$ (clean metal), hotter centre → lower surface tension → **outward flow**. With $\partial \gamma / \partial T > 0$ (sulfur/oxygen rich), the flow reverses inward, deepening the pool.

**(b) Marangoni velocity computation (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{Ma} &= \frac{\lvert \partial \gamma / \partial T \rvert \cdot \Delta T \cdot W}{\mu \cdot L_{ref}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.26 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1500 \cdot 120 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 120 \times 10^{-6}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{4.68 \times 10^{-5}}{6 \times 10^{-7}} \\[4pt]
&\approx 78 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The Marangoni velocity is of order **tens of m/s**, far exceeding buoyancy-driven flow (typically < 1 m/s for sub-millimetre pools). Hence Marangoni convection is the **dominant fluid-dynamic mechanism**, controlling melt-pool shape, mixing, and ultimately the homogenization of alloying elements and the entrapment of gas pores.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Most students forget that VED is in **J/mm³** (not J/m³). A 1000× factor error loses 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Definition missing:** Writing VED as just $P/(v h t)$ **without naming absorptivity** $\eta_a$ loses 1 mark — examiners expect the full formulation $E_v = \eta_a P / (v h t)$.
> 3. **Confusing SLM with DMLS:** In KTU, both are L-PBF variants; do not write DMLS uses a different physics — only a different historical brand (EOS vs others).
> 4. **Sign of $\partial \gamma / \partial T$:** Always state the sign and its **flow direction consequence** — examiners specifically look for this in any melt-pool question.
> 5. **Numerical units in Marangoni:** Convert µm → m consistently **before** computing, otherwise an answer off by $10^6$ loses all 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PBF = L-PBF (laser + Ar) OR EBM (electron beam + vacuum + preheat)**.
- **VED** $= P/(v h t)$ — **single most important formula** for KTU PBF problems.
- **Optimal VED** for Ti-6Al-4V (SLM): **60–120 J/mm³**; below → lack of fusion, above → keyholing.
- **Beer–Lambert absorption:** $I(z) = I_0 e^{-\alpha z}$; metals at 1070 nm have $\alpha \approx 10^7$ m⁻¹ → surface absorption.
- **Recoil pressure** creates the **keyhole** at high intensity.
- **Marangoni flow** is the dominant melt-pool convection; sign of $\partial \gamma / \partial T$ decides flow direction.
- **Cellular spacing:** $\lambda_1 \propto G^{-a} R^{-b}$; faster cooling → finer microstructure.
- **EBM advantages:** lower residual stress, faster build; **disadvantage:** coarser surface, vacuum limits.
- **SLM advantages:** finer resolution, wider alloy range, lower build plate temperature.
- **Process parameter hierarchy:** P (laser power) and v (scan speed) are first-order; h and t are second-order.
- **Build rate** = cross-section area × layer thickness / (time per layer); EBM generally has 3–10× higher build rate than SLM.
- **Defect–energy density map:** Lack of fusion → optimum → keyhole → balling, as $E_v$ increases.
- **Atmosphere control:** O₂ < 1000 ppm (often < 100 ppm) for Ti and Al alloys to prevent oxide inclusions.
- **KTU favourite terminology:** Always say "laser **melts** the powder fully" (SLM), never "sinters" (which is the older DMLS name).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Binder Jetting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET867 - METAL ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Binder Jetting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Binder Jetting (BJT)** is a powder-bed fusion–class additive manufacturing process defined by **ISO/ASTM 52900 (2021)** under the category of *"Material Jetting"* combined with *"Powder Bed Fusion"* characteristics. In Binder Jetting, a liquid bonding agent (binder) is selectively jetted — drop-by-drop — onto a bed of metal powder particles using a thermal or piezoelectric inkjet printhead. The binder droplets locally wet the powder grains, fill inter-particle voids, and form **menisci** between adjacent particles due to surface tension. After the binder cures/dries, the bonded powder region constitutes a "green" part. Subsequent furnace operations (curing, de-binding, and sintering/infiltration) transform the fragile green part into a dense, functional metallic component.

The ISO/ASTM standardized term is **"Binder Jetting"** (abbreviated **BJT**), and the parent process category is **"Material Jetting"**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Binder Jetting is one of the seven canonical AM process categories recognized by ISO/ASTM 52900. Unlike laser/electron-beam powder bed fusion, BJT uses **no directed thermal energy source** during layer formation — making it a **room-temperature printing** process. All densification happens in a downstream furnace.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are building a sandcastle, but instead of using your hands to dig and shape the sand, you have a tiny **glue gun** that flies over a flat tray of dry sand. The glue gun shoots microscopic drops of glue in a precise pattern — wherever the drops land, the sand grains stick together. The tray then drops down a tiny bit, a fresh layer of sand is rolled on top, and the glue gun shoots another pattern. Layer by layer, a 3D shape of glued sand emerges inside the sand bed. When you finish, you gently blow away the loose, unglued sand, and what remains is a fragile "green" sandcastle glued together. To make it strong, you bake it in an oven so the sand grains fuse permanently.

**The "glue"** = binder (often a polymer solution or colloidal silica)
**The "sand"** = metal powder (steel, stainless steel, titanium, etc.)
**The "oven"** = sintering furnace (or infiltration furnace for bronze infiltration)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Examiners frequently begin questions with analogy-style framing such as *"Explain Binder Jetting as a 'printing with glue on metal sand' process."* Mastering this analogy gives you 3 free marks in the definition sub-part.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Binder drop volume:** typically **1 – 80 picolitres (pL)**, corresponding to drop diameters of **10 – 80 µm**.
- **Layer thickness:** typically **30 – 100 µm** for metal BJT.
- **Printhead resolution:** up to **1200 dpi** (dots per inch).
- **Binder viscosity for stable jetting:** **1 – 30 mPa·s** (centipoise).
- **Build envelope:** industrially up to **800 mm × 500 mm × 400 mm**.
- **Surface roughness (as-sintered):** **Ra 4 – 11 µm**.
- **Green part density (after printing, before sintering):** typically **55 – 65 %** of theoretical.
- **Sintered density (after furnace):** **90 – 97 %** for single-material sintering; **>95 %** with bronze infiltration.
- **Shrinkage during sintering:** **linear shrinkage of 12 – 25 %** must be compensated digitally (isotropic scaling factor) in the CAD model.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Process Flow (Operational Decomposition)

The Binder Jetting workflow is divided into **five sequential stages**. Examiners love this list — it almost always appears as a 7-mark sub-question.

**Stage 1 — Powder Spreading (Recoating)**
- A roller or blade spreads a thin, uniform layer of metal powder from a feed bed onto the build platform.
- Powder flowability is critical; poor flow causes voids and short-feeding defects.
- Layer thickness $h_l$ is set by the recoater gap (typically 30–100 µm).

**Stage 2 — Binder Jetting (Printing)**
- The printhead traverses the powder bed in a raster pattern.
- For each voxel, the printhead ejects micro-droplets of binder.
- Droplet volume $V_d$ is governed by the **Rayleigh–Plateau instability** for stable jetting:

$$V_d = \frac{\pi d_j^3}{6}$$

where $d_j$ is the jet orifice diameter (typically 10–80 µm).

- The **droplet impact Weber number** must satisfy $We = \frac{\rho v^2 d_d}{\sigma} < 50$ to avoid splashing, where $v$ is droplet velocity, $d_d$ is droplet diameter, $\sigma$ is binder surface tension, $\rho$ is binder density.
- After jetting, the layer is held briefly (1–3 s) to allow wicking and capillary bonding.

**Stage 3 — Layer Translation (Z-axis Step)**
- The build platform descends by exactly one layer thickness $h_l$.
- A new powder layer is recoated, and the printhead prints the next cross-section.

**Stage 4 — Depowdering (Green Part Recovery)**
- After the entire build is printed, the powder bed is transferred to a depowdering station.
- Loose, unbound powder is removed by brushing, vacuuming, or compressed air.
- The fragile "green" part has typical handling strength of only **1 – 5 MPa** in flexure.

**Stage 5 — Post-Processing (Curing → Sintering → Optional Infiltration)**
- **Curing:** The green part is placed in a low-temperature oven (180–200 °C) to fully polymerize the binder.
- **De-binding (optional):** Removes residual binder before high-temperature sintering.
- **Sintering:** The part is heated to **0.7 – 0.9 × T_m** (where $T_m$ is the melting point in Kelvin) for 1–6 hours, causing solid-state diffusion bonding.
- **Infiltration (optional):** A lower-melting metal (e.g., bronze, Cu-Sn) is melted and drawn into the porous sintered skeleton, raising density to >95 %.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Distinction:** BJT produces a **green part that is NOT a usable metal part**. The as-printed body is essentially a powder compact held together by dried polymer. The mechanical integrity only emerges *after* sintering or infiltration. This distinguishes BJT from Selective Laser Melting (SLM), which produces a near-fully-dense metal part directly.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage — The Underlying Physics

| Stage | Dominant Physics | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Powder Spreading | Granular flow, Jenike flow index | Bad flow = uneven layers = porosity defects |
| Binder Jetting | Capillarity, viscous dissipation, Rayleigh jetting | Drop placement accuracy & wetting control voxel-level geometry |
| Capillary Wicking | Young–Laplace equation, Darcy flow | Determines binder penetration depth & inter-particle bond strength |
| Curing | Polymer cross-linking (thermoset) or solvent evaporation (thermoplastic binder) | Develops initial "green" strength |
| Sintering | Solid-state diffusion (Nabarro–Herring, Coble creep) | Drives densification and grain growth |
| Infiltration | Capillary-driven melt flow (Washburn equation) | Fills residual porosity to >95 % density |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_d = \frac{\pi d_j^3}{6}$ | Spherical droplet volume from jet orifice | Drop volume calibration for printhead |
| 2 | $We = \frac{\rho v^2 d_d}{\sigma}$ | Weber number for droplet impact | Predict splashing; design inkjet parameters |
| 3 | $Re = \frac{\rho v d_d}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number for droplet impact | Predict spreading vs. recoil |
| 4 | $\Delta P = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{r_p}$ | Capillary pressure (Young–Laplace) between particles of radius $r_p$ with contact angle $\theta$ | Determines inter-particle bond force |
| 5 | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{sv} = A \cdot \frac{\sigma \Omega \delta D}{k T d^3}$ | Coble creep strain rate for sintering (where $D$ is diffusion coefficient, $d$ is grain size) | Predicts densification rate |
| 6 | $\frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = (1 - \rho_{green}/\rho_{final})^{1/3}$ | Linear shrinkage from green to final density (isotropic, for cubic packing) | Compensates CAD geometry pre-build |
| 7 | $h_{wick} = \sqrt{\frac{r_p \gamma \cos\theta}{2\mu} \cdot t}$ | Washburn penetration depth over time $t$ | Predicts binder infiltration depth into powder |
| 8 | $v_{droplet} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P_{chamber}}{\rho}}$ | Droplet ejection velocity from chamber pressure $\Delta P_{chamber}$ | Tune printhead firing parameters |
| 9 | $\rho_{sintered} = \rho_{th} \left(1 - \frac{\pi d_p^2 N_v}{6}\right)$ | Residual porosity estimate (approx) | Quality control metric post-sintering |
| 10 | $S_{iso} = 1 + \alpha (T - T_0)$ | Isotropic thermal scaling (with CTE $\alpha$) | Pre-build geometry compensation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Formulas 4 (capillary pressure) and 6 (shrinkage compensation) are **the most frequently tested** in KTU university exams. Memorize them with units.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Binder Jetting is the only AM process that can economically produce **large-format metal parts (up to ~1 m scale)** without the geometric constraints of a laser chamber. Industrial production systems (e.g., ExOne, Digital Metal, HP Metal Jet, Desktop Metal) exploit this for:
- **Automotive:** Medium-volume production of brackets, manifolds, and gear-housing prototypes.
- **Medical:** Patient-specific orthopaedic implants (titanium and stainless-steel BJT) with controlled porosity for bone in-growth.
- **Tooling:** Conformal cooling inserts for injection moulds (complex internal channels that cannot be machined).
- **Energy & Filtration:** Porous sintered bronze filters (15–50 % controlled porosity) for gas/liquid separation.
- **Sand casting:** BJT-printed sand cores and moulds (using furan binder + silica sand) — the original commercial BJT application.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Droplet Volume from Rayleigh–Plateau Jetting

The printhead ejects a liquid column through an orifice of diameter $d_j$. For stable jetting, the column breaks into droplets of volume equal to the cylinder that contained one wavelength of the Rayleigh instability, $\lambda_R = \pi d_j$.

**Step 1 — Cylinder volume of one instability wavelength:**

$$V_{cyl} = \frac{\pi d_j^2}{4} \cdot \lambda_R = \frac{\pi d_j^2}{4} \cdot (\pi d_j)$$

**Step 2 — Simplify:**

$$V_{cyl} = \frac{\pi^2 d_j^3}{4}$$

**Step 3 — During pinch-off, the cylinder collapses into a single spherical droplet. Conservation of volume gives:**

$$V_d = V_{cyl} = \frac{\pi^2 d_j^3}{4}$$

**Step 4 — However, experimental measurement and the more rigorous Rayleigh analysis show that two satellite droplets also form, so the main droplet is slightly smaller. The practical engineering formula is therefore:**

$$V_d \approx \frac{\pi d_j^3}{6}$$

This corresponds to a sphere whose diameter equals the orifice diameter $d_j$. The numerical coefficient (1/6 instead of $\pi^2/4 \approx 2.47$) reflects the post-pinch-off volume distribution.

**Engineering use:** For a typical $d_j = 30 \,\mu m$ orifice:

$$V_d = \frac{\pi (30 \times 10^{-6})^3}{6} = \frac{\pi \cdot 2.7 \times 10^{-14}}{6} \approx 1.41 \times 10^{-14} \, m^3 = 14.1 \, pL$$

This matches the manufacturer's specified drop volume of 10–30 pL for typical printheads.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Sintering Shrinkage Compensation Factor

The green part has density $\rho_g$ (relative to theoretical $\rho_{th}$, so green relative density $\phi_g = \rho_g / \rho_{th}$). After sintering, the part has density $\phi_f = \rho_f / \rho_{th}$ with $\phi_f > \phi_g$.

**Step 1 — Mass conservation (no material added or lost):**

$$m_{green} = m_{sintered}$$

$$\rho_g \cdot V_g = \rho_f \cdot V_f$$

**Step 2 — Express the volume ratio:**

$$\frac{V_f}{V_g} = \frac{\rho_g}{\rho_f} = \frac{\phi_g}{\phi_f}$$

**Step 3 — Assume isotropic shrinkage. For a cube of side $L$:**

$$V = L^3 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{V_f}{V_g} = \left(\frac{L_f}{L_g}\right)^3$$

**Step 4 — Equate and take the cube root:**

$$\left(\frac{L_f}{L_g}\right)^3 = \frac{\phi_g}{\phi_f}$$

$$\frac{L_f}{L_g} = \left(\frac{\phi_g}{\phi_f}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 5 — The shrinkage strain (negative) is:**

$$\varepsilon_{shrink} = \frac{L_f - L_g}{L_g} = \left(\frac{\phi_g}{\phi_f}\right)^{1/3} - 1$$

**Step 6 — Compensation factor (scale CAD up by this factor):**

$$S = \frac{L_{CAD}}{L_{final}} = \left(\frac{\phi_f}{\phi_g}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Numerical Example (typical metal BJT):**
Given $\phi_g = 0.55$ (55 % green density) and $\phi_f = 0.93$ (93 % sintered density):

$$S = \left(\frac{0.93}{0.55}\right)^{1/3} = (1.691)^{1/3} \approx 1.191$$

So the CAD must be scaled up by **19.1 %** in every dimension to yield a 93 %-dense final part. This is consistent with the **12 – 25 %** linear shrinkage band observed in industry.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Capillary Bonding Force Between Two Powder Particles

Consider two identical spherical powder particles of radius $r_p$ joined by a binder meniscus forming a "liquid bridge" of contact angle $\theta$.

**Step 1 — Pressure inside the meniscus (Young–Laplace):**

For a meniscus with two principal radii of curvature $R_1$ and $R_2$:

$$\Delta P = \sigma \left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — For a small bridge, the meridional radius is small (≈ $r_p$ contribution) and the azimuthal radius is much smaller. The dominant negative capillary pressure (suction) inside the bridge is approximately:**

$$\Delta P \approx -\frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{r_p}$$

**Step 3 — The attractive (capillary) force between the two particles is:**

$$F_{cap} = \Delta P \cdot A_{contact} \approx -\frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{r_p} \cdot \pi r_p^2 = -2\pi r_p \sigma \cos\theta$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude of the attractive force:**

$$F_{cap} = 2\pi r_p \sigma \cos\theta$$

**Step 5 — Numerical estimate** (typical values: $r_p = 20 \, \mu m$, $\sigma = 0.05 \, N/m$, $\theta = 30°$, $\cos\theta \approx 0.866$):

$$F_{cap} = 2\pi (20 \times 10^{-6}) (0.05)(0.866) \approx 5.44 \times 10^{-6} \, N = 5.44 \, \mu N$$

This micro-scale force, summed over billions of inter-particle contacts, gives the green part its **1 – 5 MPa flexural strength**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters for KTU:** This is the most direct physical justification for "why does the green part hold together at all?" Examiners often ask it as a 7-mark application question.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Process Parameters → Defect Map

| Process Parameter | Effect When Increased | Resulting Defect (if too high) | Resulting Defect (if too low) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Binder saturation $S_b = V_{binder}/V_{voids}$ | Higher local binder content | Binder bleed, oversize features, warping | Weak green strength, powder pull-out |
| Layer thickness $h_l$ | Faster build, lower resolution | Poor inter-layer bonding, delamination | Loss of fine features, slow build |
| Powder size $d_{p50}$ | Larger voids, faster flow | Lower sintered density, rough surface | Poor flow, agglomeration, recoater jam |
| Sintering temperature $T_s$ | Faster diffusion, more shrinkage | Excessive grain growth, slumping | Incomplete densification, residual porosity |
| Sintering time $t_s$ | Higher density | Coarsening, distortion | Under-sintering, low strength |
| Cooling rate $\dot{T}$ | Faster cycle | Thermal shock cracks | Long cycle time |
| Printhead frequency $f$ | Higher speed | Misplaced drops, satellite drops | Slow production |
| Powder bed temperature $T_{bed}$ | Reduced thermal mismatch | — | Binder evaporation, poor wetting |

**Definition used:** Binder saturation $S_b = \dfrac{V_{binder}}{V_{voids}}$ where $V_{voids}$ is the theoretical inter-particle void volume in the powder bed (typically 35–45 % of bed volume for random close packing).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: BJT System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD File STL] --> B[Slicing Software]
    B --> C[Build File per Layer]
    C --> D[Powder Feed Bed]
    D --> E[Recoater Roller]
    E --> F[Build Platform Powder Layer]
    F --> G[Thermal Inkjet Printhead]
    G --> H[Liquid Binder Reservoir]
    H --> G
    G --> I[Selective Binder Deposition Voxel by Voxel]
    I --> J[Capillary Wicking Between Particles]
    J --> K[One Layer Complete]
    K --> L[Build Platform Lowers by h_l]
    L --> F
    K --> M[Full Build Complete]
    M --> N[Depowdering Station Vacuum and Brush]
    N --> O[Green Part Recovery]
    O --> P[Low Temp Curing Oven 180 to 200 C]
    P --> Q[Optional De binding Stage]
    Q --> R[High Temp Sintering Furnace 0.7 to 0.9 T_m]
    R --> S[Optional Bronze Infiltration]

    T --> U[Support Removal and Finishing]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow: Stages of Binder Jetting

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stage1[Stage 1 Powder Spreading]
        A1[Feed Powder] --> A2[Roller Recoat] --> A3[Uniform Layer on Build Plate]
    end
    subgraph Stage2[Stage 2 Binder Jetting]
        A3 --> B1[Printhead Raster Scan] --> B2[Micro Drop Ejection 1 to 80 pL] --> B3[Selective Wetting of Powder]
    end

        B3 --> C1[Build Plate Descends by h_l] --> A3
    end

    end
    subgraph Stage5[Stage 5 Furnace Post Processing]

    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison: BJT vs. Laser PBF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Additive Manufacturing Powder Bed Family]
    A --> B[Binder Jetting BJT]
    A --> C[Laser Powder Bed Fusion LPBF]

    B --> B2[Material State: Powder Plus Binder Liquid]
    B --> B3[Densification: Furnace Sintering Later]
    B --> B4[Build Speed: Very High]
    B --> B5[Support Structures: Not Required]
    C --> C1[Heat Source: High Power Laser 200 to 500 W]
    C --> C2[Material State: Powder Only]

```

### 4.4 Mermaid Defect-Cause Tree

```mermaid

    C --> C4[Cracking During Sintering]

```

Labels are plain uppercase text with no markdown formatting inside the quoted strings, ensuring clean rendering in GitHub, VS Code, and Confluence.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Binder Jetting. List any two advantages of BJT over Selective Laser Melting (SLM).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (1.5 marks):** Binder Jetting is an additive manufacturing process in which a liquid bonding agent is selectively jetted from a printhead onto a bed of metal powder to bond the particles together layer by layer, after which the "green" part is densified in a sintering furnace.

**Two advantages over SLM (1.5 marks):**
1. **No support structures required** because the part is supported by the surrounding unbound powder at all times.
2. **Higher build speed and larger build envelopes** because no in-situ melting is performed.
3. (Bonus optional) Lower equipment cost since no laser, no inert gas chamber required.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of a "green part" in Binder Jetting? Why is it necessary to sinter it?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **green part** is the as-printed body after binder jetting and depowdering. It consists of metal powder particles held together by dried/cured polymer binder (1 mark). Its flexural strength is only ~1–5 MPa (1 mark). **Sintering is necessary** to develop the metallic bonds via solid-state diffusion at 0.7–0.9 T_m, increasing density from ~55–65 % to >90 % and giving the part its final mechanical strength (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. You answer EITHER the entire Q-A or the entire Q-B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Description

**Q-A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working principle of Binder Jetting for metal parts. Describe each stage of the process.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the post-processing operations in BJT in detail. Why is shrinkage compensation a critical step, and how is the scaling factor calculated?

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working Principle & Schematic [7 marks]**

Binder Jetting produces metal parts by selectively depositing liquid binder onto metal powder, layer by layer, followed by furnace densification. (1 mark)

**Schematic Description (refer to SECTION 4.1 Mermaid diagram):** (2 marks for diagram / description)
The system consists of a **build chamber** with two powder beds (feed and build), a **recoater roller**, a **thermal/piezoelectric printhead**, a **binder reservoir**, and a **z-axis build platform**.

**Stages (1 mark each):**
1. **Powder spreading:** Roller lays down a uniform 30–100 µm layer from the feed bed onto the build platform.
2. **Binder jetting:** Printhead scans in a raster pattern, ejecting 1–80 pL droplets at positions defined by the sliced CAD model. The binder wicks into the powder by capillarity and bonds the particles.
3. **Layer step-down:** Build platform descends by exactly $h_l$ and a new layer is spread.
4. **Depowdering:** After all layers, unbound powder is removed to reveal the green part.
5. **Furnace post-processing:** Curing, de-binding, sintering (and optional infiltration).

**(b) Post-Processing & Shrinkage Compensation [7 marks]**

**Post-processing (3 marks):**
- **Curing:** 180–200 °C oven; polymerizes binder; develops green strength.
- **De-binding:** Removes residual binder (thermal or solvent).
- **Sintering:** Heated to 0.7–0.9 T_m for several hours; solid-state diffusion densifies the part to 90–97 %.
- **Infiltration (optional):** Low-melt metal (e.g., bronze) wicks into residual pores, raising density to >95 %.

**Why shrinkage compensation is critical (2 marks):**
Sintering causes **12–25 % linear shrinkage** in all dimensions due to pore elimination. If uncompensated, the final part is too small and dimensionally incorrect. The CAD must therefore be **uniformly scaled up** by a pre-calculated factor before slicing.

**Scaling factor derivation (2 marks):**
From Section 3.2, mass conservation gives:

$$S = \left(\frac{\phi_f}{\phi_g}\right)^{1/3}$$

For typical $\phi_g = 0.55$ and $\phi_f = 0.93$:

$$S = \left(\frac{0.93}{0.55}\right)^{1/3} = 1.191$$

So the CAD is scaled up by **19.1 %** in every dimension.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q-B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the capillary bonding mechanism in Binder Jetting. Derive an expression for the inter-particle capillary force.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare Binder Jetting with Selective Laser Melting (SLM) on the basis of: (i) heat source, (ii) build speed, (iii) need for support structures, (iv) green vs. finished density, (v) typical accuracy, (vi) post-processing, and (vii) typical material compatibility.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Capillary Bonding Mechanism [7 marks]**

**Concept (2 marks):** When binder droplets land on the powder bed, they spread by capillary action into the inter-particle voids, forming tiny **liquid bridges** (menisci) between adjacent powder particles. The concave meniscus creates a **negative Laplace pressure** inside the bridge, pulling the particles together and bonding them. Upon solvent evaporation or polymer curing, the bridge solidifies into a rigid bond.

**Governing equation — Young–Laplace pressure (2 marks):**

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{r_p}$$

This is negative (suction) for $\theta < 90°$, creating an attractive force.

**Derivation of capillary force (3 marks):**
Multiply the suction pressure by the contact area $A_{contact} \approx \pi r_p^2$:

$$F_{cap} = \Delta P \cdot A_{contact} = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{r_p} \cdot \pi r_p^2 = 2\pi r_p \sigma \cos\theta$$

For $r_p = 20 \,\mu m$, $\sigma = 0.05 \,N/m$, $\theta = 30°$:

$$F_{cap} = 2\pi (20 \times 10^{-6})(0.05)(0.866) \approx 5.44 \, \mu N$$

The aggregate of billions of such micro-bonds gives the green part its macroscopic 1–5 MPa green strength.

**(b) BJT vs. SLM Comparison [7 marks]** (1 mark per correct comparison)

| # | Parameter | Binder Jetting (BJT) | Selective Laser Melting (SLM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| i | Heat source | None during print (room temp) | High-power fibre laser (200–500 W) |
| ii | Build speed | Very high (no melt) | Slower (melt & solidify each voxel) |
| iii | Support structures | Not required (powder bed supports) | Required for overhangs >45° |
| iv | Green / finished density | Green: 55–65 %; Finished: 90–97 % | Finished: >99 % (no separate green stage) |
| v | Typical accuracy | ±0.2–0.4 mm (plus sintering distortion) | ±0.05–0.1 mm |
| vi | Post-processing | Extensive (curing + sintering + infiltration) | Minimal (only stress relief & support removal) |
| vii | Material compatibility | Wide: steels, Ti, bronze, sand | More limited: weldable alloys only |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse BJT with Material Jetting of photopolymers** — they share the printhead principle but build different materials. In BJT, the binder is the *bonding agent* on a *metal/sand powder*, not the bulk material itself.
> 2. **Do NOT claim that BJT gives >99 % density directly** — it is a two-step (printing + sintering) process and rarely exceeds 97 % without infiltration. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this error.
> 3. **Do NOT omit the shrinkage compensation step** in process-flow answers. It is mandatory in KTU marking schemes.
> 4. **In derivation questions,** writing the formula but not defining each variable loses 1–2 marks. Always state: $r_p$ = particle radius, $\sigma$ = binder surface tension, $\theta$ = contact angle, $\phi$ = relative density.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for Binder Jetting (Module 2):**

- ✅ **Definition:** BJT is a powder-bed AM process using a jetted liquid binder; classified under ISO/ASTM 52900 "Material Jetting" category.
- ✅ **Five Stages:** Powder Spreading → Binder Jetting → Layer Step-Down → Depowdering → Post-Processing (Cure + Sinter + optional Infiltrate).
- ✅ **No heat during printing:** This is BJT's defining feature vs. SLM/DMLS/EBM.
- ✅ **Green density:** 55–65 % theoretical; **Sintered density:** 90–97 %; **With infiltration:** >95 %.
- ✅ **Linear shrinkage during sintering:** 12–25 %, compensated by scaling factor:

$$S = \left(\frac{\phi_f}{\phi_g}\right)^{1/3}$$

- ✅ **Capillary bonding force:**

$$F_{cap} = 2\pi r_p \sigma \cos\theta$$

- ✅ **Green strength:** 1–5 MPa; final metallic strength emerges *only* after sintering.
- ✅ **No support structures required** — unbound powder supports the part at all times.
- ✅ **Typical layer thickness:** 30–100 µm; **drop volume:** 1–80 pL; **droplet diameter:** 10–80 µm.
- ✅ **Sintering temperature:** 0.7–0.9 × T_m (in Kelvin).
- ✅ **Key advantage:** Large build envelopes, high throughput, no thermal residual stress.
- ✅ **Key limitation:** Post-processing is mandatory; distortion control is non-trivial.
- ✅ **Common materials:** 316L & 17-4 PH stainless steel, M2 tool steel, titanium, bronze (for infiltration), silica sand (for casting moulds).
- ✅ **Industry systems to remember:** ExOne (now part of Desktop Metal), Digital Metal, HP Metal Jet, Voxeljet.
- ✅ **Standards tag:** ISO/ASTM 52900 process category — **"Material Jetting"** with **"Binder Jetting"** as the powder-based variant.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET867 - METAL ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DMLS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is DMLS? — Formal Academic Definition

**Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS)** is a powder bed fusion (PBF) additive manufacturing (AM) process classified under **ISO/ASTM 52900** as a *laser powder bed fusion (L-PBF)* technology. In DMLS, a high-power fiber laser (typically a ytterbium-fiber laser operating in the near-infrared region, $\lambda \approx 1070~\text{nm}$) selectively scans and fully melts regions of a thin, evenly spread layer of metal powder, fusing them together on a build platform inside an inert (argon or nitrogen) atmosphere to fabricate fully dense, near-net-shape metallic parts layer-by-layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** DMLS falls under the *Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)* family of metal AM. Unlike its cousin **SLM (Selective Laser Melting)**, which also fully melts the powder, DMLS was the original patented process introduced by **EOS GmbH** (Electro Optical Systems, Germany) in 1995 and is technically a trade-marked term. In contemporary academic literature, both DMLS and SLM are treated under the same physics regime of *laser powder bed fusion of metals*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Layered Cake of Molten Metal" Picture

Imagine baking a multi-tiered cake, but instead of frosting layers of sponge, you do this:

1. A very thin blanket of fine metallic flour (metal powder, particle size typically **15–45 µm**) is spread evenly on a tray.
2. A focused laser (think of a magnifying glass focusing sunlight, but $10^6$ times more intense) traces a 2D pattern on the flour, completely **melting** only the desired cross-section into a solid metal slice.
3. The tray drops by exactly the thickness of one slice (typically **20–40 µm**), a fresh blanket of flour is rolled out, and the laser melts the next layer right on top.
4. After 1000–10000 such layers, a complex 3D metal part (e.g., a turbine blade with internal cooling channels) emerges from the powder bed, ready for stress-relief heat treatment and support removal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why an inert atmosphere?** Reactive metals (titanium, aluminum alloys) oxidize rapidly above ~500 °C. Oxygen levels in the build chamber are typically maintained below **50 ppm** (often **< 10 ppm** for titanium and reactive alloys) using argon or nitrogen purging to prevent oxide inclusions that would embrittle the part.

## 1.3 Distinguishing DMLS from Related Processes

| Process | Melting Mode | Heat Source | Typical Materials | Patent/Generic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DMLS** | Full melting | Yb-fiber laser (1070 nm) | Steel, Ti, Al, Inconel | EOS trademark |
| **SLM** | Full melting | Yb-fiber laser (1070 nm) | Same as DMLS | Generic term (often same physics) |
| **SLS (polymers)** | Sintering (no melt) | CO₂ laser | Nylon, TPU | Generic |
| **EBM** | Full melting | Electron beam (vacuum) | Ti, CoCr | Arcam trademark |
| **Binder Jetting** | No melting (binder) | No thermal source | Metal + binder | Generic |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU examinations, treat **DMLS and SLM as functionally equivalent** unless the question specifically contrasts them. The examiner's intent is to test your understanding of the *laser powder bed fusion (L-PBF) of metals* process.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DMLS Process — Step-by-Step Operational Sequence

The DMLS process follows a strict cyclic sequence governed by a build-chamber controller and slicing software. Each cycle is called a **layer** and typically takes **8–25 seconds** depending on part area and laser parameters.

1. **Substrate Preparation & Sealing**
   - A build platform (substrate plate) of the same base material as the powder is mounted on a Z-axis piston inside a sealed build chamber.
   - The chamber is purged with argon (or nitrogen for certain steels) to reduce O₂ content below the specified threshold.

2. **Powder Deposition**
   - A powder reservoir piston rises by one **layer thickness** $t_l$ (typically 20–40 µm).
   - A recoater blade (or counter-rotating roller) spreads powder across the build platform to form a uniform layer.

3. **Laser Scanning (Cross-Linking)**
   - The high-power fiber laser (200 W – 1 kW typical, up to 1 kW in modern systems) is steered by a **galvanometer scanner** with F-theta lenses.
   - The laser traces the 2D cross-section, dividing it into **scan vectors** separated by **hatch spacing** $h$.

4. **Layer Solidification**
   - The molten track (called a *melt pool*) rapidly solidifies at cooling rates of $10^4$ – $10^6~\text{K/s}$.
   - This rapid solidification produces **fine martensitic** or cellular microstructures.

5. **Platform Lowering & Recoating**
   - The build platform descends by $t_l$, and the cycle repeats.

6. **Cooling, Depowdering & Post-Processing**
   - The build chamber cools under inert gas (cycle times of 8–24 hours).
   - Loose powder is removed via vacuum or brushing. Parts undergo **stress relief** (typically 600–700 °C for steels) and may be **HIPed** (Hot Isostatic Pressing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Support Structures:** Parts with overhangs > 45° from the build plate require metal support structures (thin lattice lattices attached to the build platform) that act as both **thermal conductors** (to draw heat away and prevent warping) and **mechanical anchors** (to resist residual stresses).

## 2.2 Key DMLS Process Parameters — The "Big Four"

The quality, density, and mechanical properties of DMLS parts are governed by four primary parameters. Understanding their interplay is **the most heavily tested concept** in KTU examinations on this topic.

### (a) Laser Power ($P$)
- Unit: Watts (W)
- Typical range: 170 W – 1000 W
- Higher $P$ increases melt pool depth and width.

### (b) Scan Speed ($v$)
- Unit: mm/s
- Typical range: 200 mm/s – 2000 mm/s
- Higher $v$ reduces heat input per unit length.

### (c) Hatch Spacing ($h$)
- Unit: µm
- Typical range: 60 µm – 200 µm
- Distance between adjacent scan vectors.

### (d) Layer Thickness ($t_l$)
- Unit: µm
- Typical range: 20 µm – 60 µm
- Vertical resolution.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — DMLS High-Yield Equations

| # | Equation / Parameter | Symbols & Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** | $E_V = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t_l} \quad [\text{J/mm}^3]$ | Total laser energy delivered per unit volume of powder. The most critical single metric for process window tuning. |
| 2 | **Linear Energy Density (LED)** | $E_L = \dfrac{P}{v} \quad [\text{J/mm}]$ | Energy per unit length of scan track. Governs melt pool depth. |
| 3 | **Areal Energy Density (AED)** | $E_A = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h} \quad [\text{J/mm}^2]$ | Energy per unit area of scanned region. Governs melt pool width. |
| 4 | **Melt Pool Width Approximation** | $w_m \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{4 P}{\pi \rho c_p v (T_m - T_0)}}$ | Empirical relation linking laser power and scan speed to melt pool width. |
| 5 | **Cooling Rate Estimate** | $\dot{T} \approx \dfrac{2 \pi k (T_m - T_0)^2}{P / v}$ | Approximate cooling rate $\dot{T}$ (K/s); $k$ = thermal conductivity of powder. |
| 6 | **Relative Density Target** | $\rho_{\text{rel}} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{part}}}{\rho_{\text{theoretical}}} \geq 99.5\%$ | Industry standard for "fully dense" DMLS parts. |
| 7 | **Build Rate (Volumetric)** | $\dot{V}_B = v \cdot h \cdot t_l \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{s}]$ | Instantaneous material deposition rate. |
| 8 | **Layer Time** | $t_{\text{layer}} = \dfrac{A_{\text{part}}}{v \cdot h} + t_{\text{setup}}$ | Time per layer = scan time + powder spread time. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse $E_V$, $E_L$, and $E_A$. Memorize the units first — $\text{J/mm}^3$ (volume), $\text{J/mm}$ (length), $\text{J/mm}^2$ (area). The volumetric form $E_V$ is the most commonly tested in problems.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of DMLS

- **Aerospace:** Topology-optimized brackets, fuel injectors, turbine blades with conformal cooling channels.
- **Medical:** Patient-specific Ti-6Al-4V cranial implants, orthopedic hip cups with trabecular lattices.
- **Automotive:** Prototype and low-volume functional components (e.g., Formula 1, electric vehicle battery housings).
- **Tooling:** Conformal-cooled injection mold inserts with internal channels impossible to machine conventionally.

> [!TIP]
> The ability to manufacture **internal conformal cooling channels** is a *killer application* of DMLS. Conventional CNC drilling can only produce straight channels, but DMLS can create curved, branching internal networks that reduce mold cycle time by 20–40%.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Energy Density from First Principles

The energy delivered by a laser to a scanned region can be derived by considering a single scan track of length $L$, scanned at speed $v$ with laser power $P$.

**Step 1 — Time for one scan track of length $L$:**
$$t = \dfrac{L}{v}$$

**Step 2 — Total energy delivered along this scan track:**
$$E_{\text{track}} = P \cdot t = P \cdot \dfrac{L}{v} \quad [\text{J}]$$

**Step 3 — The scanned track has a cross-section of width $h$ (hatch spacing) and depth $t_l$ (layer thickness). Hence the melted volume is:**
$$V_{\text{track}} = h \cdot t_l \cdot L \quad [\text{mm}^3]$$

**Step 4 — Energy per unit volume (Volumetric Energy Density):**
$$E_V = \dfrac{E_{\text{track}}}{V_{\text{track}}} = \dfrac{P \cdot L / v}{h \cdot t_l \cdot L}$$

Cancel $L$ from numerator and denominator:
$$\boxed{E_V = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t_l} \quad [\text{J/mm}^3]}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

> **[KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 2 Typical Problem]**
> A DMLS machine is processing **316L stainless steel** powder with the following parameters:
> - Laser power: $P = 285~\text{W}$
> - Scan speed: $v = 1100~\text{mm/s}$
> - Hatch spacing: $h = 0.10~\text{mm}$
> - Layer thickness: $t_l = 0.030~\text{mm}$
>
> **(a)** Calculate the Volumetric Energy Density (VED).
> **(b)** Comment on whether the process window lies in the *lack of fusion*, *optimal*, or *keyhole* regime.
> **(c)** If the hatch spacing is reduced to $0.08~\text{mm}$ while keeping all other parameters constant, by what percentage does the VED change?

### Solution

**Part (a) — Calculate VED:**

$$E_V = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t_l} = \dfrac{285}{1100 \times 0.10 \times 0.030}$$

Compute the denominator step-by-step:
- $1100 \times 0.10 = 110$
- $110 \times 0.030 = 3.3$

$$E_V = \dfrac{285}{3.3} = 86.36~\text{J/mm}^3 \approx 86.4~\text{J/mm}^3$$

**Part (b) — Regime Identification:**

| Regime | VED Range for 316L | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Lack of Fusion | $E_V < 50~\text{J/mm}^3$ | Porosity, weak inter-layer bonding |
| **Optimal Process Window** | $50 \leq E_V \leq 110~\text{J/mm}^3$ | $> 99.5\%$ density, balanced properties |
| Keyhole / Over-melting | $E_V > 120~\text{J/mm}^3$ | Vapor-driven pores, balling, surface roughness |

Since $E_V = 86.4~\text{J/mm}^3$ falls in the **optimal process window** for 316L steel, this parameter set should yield a near-fully-dense part.

**Part (c) — New VED after reducing hatch spacing to $h_2 = 0.08~\text{mm}$:**

$$E_{V,2} = \dfrac{285}{1100 \times 0.08 \times 0.030}$$

- $1100 \times 0.08 = 88$
- $88 \times 0.030 = 2.64$

$$E_{V,2} = \dfrac{285}{2.64} = 107.95~\text{J/mm}^3 \approx 108.0~\text{J/mm}^3$$

Percentage change:
$$\Delta E_V\,(\%) = \dfrac{E_{V,2} - E_{V,1}}{E_{V,1}} \times 100 = \dfrac{108.0 - 86.4}{86.4} \times 100$$

$$\Delta E_V = \dfrac{21.6}{86.4} \times 100 = 25\,\%$$

> **The VED increases by 25 %** when the hatch spacing is reduced from 0.10 mm to 0.08 mm. (Note: VED is inversely proportional to hatch spacing, so reducing $h$ by 20 % increases VED by exactly 25 %.)

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameter Variation

The table below summarizes the directional effect of each parameter on the VED and the resulting part quality. This is **frequently tested** in KTU short-answer questions.

| Parameter ↑ (increase) | Effect on $E_V$ | Effect on Melt Pool | Risk of Defect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Laser power ↑ | $E_V$ ↑ | Larger, deeper melt | Keyhole porosity |
| Scan speed ↑ | $E_V$ ↓ | Smaller, shallower melt | Lack of fusion |
| Hatch spacing ↑ | $E_V$ ↓ | Wider gaps between tracks | Lack of fusion |
| Layer thickness ↑ | $E_V$ ↓ | Less re-melting of previous layer | Lack of fusion, anisotropy |

## 3.4 Derivation: Build Time Estimate

For a part of total height $H$ and total scan area per layer $A$:

$$N_{\text{layers}} = \dfrac{H}{t_l}$$

$$T_{\text{build}} = N_{\text{layers}} \times t_{\text{layer}} = \dfrac{H}{t_l} \times \left( \dfrac{A}{v \cdot h} + t_{\text{setup}} \right)$$

**Example (Quick Check):** For $H = 50~\text{mm}$, $t_l = 0.030~\text{mm}$, $A = 100~\text{mm}^2$, $v = 1000~\text{mm/s}$, $h = 0.10~\text{mm}$, ignoring setup time:
- $N_{\text{layers}} = 50 / 0.030 = 1667$ layers
- Scan time per layer = $100 / (1000 \times 0.10) = 1.0~\text{s}$
- $T_{\text{build}} = 1667 \times 1.0 = 1667~\text{s} \approx 28~\text{min}$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DMLS System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the functional subsystems of a DMLS machine, focusing on **information flow, thermal control, and material flow** rather than physical geometry. This representation is preferred for KTU theory examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[STL CAD File] --> A2[Slicer Software]
    A2 --> A3[Layer by Layer Slicing at thickness t_l]
    A3 --> A4[Scan Path Generation with hatch h]
    A4 --> A5[Build File Upload]

    B1[Argon Cylinder] --> B2[Oxygen Sensor]
    B2 --> B3[Inert Build Chamber with lt 50 ppm O2]
    B3 --> B4[Build Platform Z Axis Piston]
    B3 --> B5[Powder Feed Piston]
    B5 --> B6[Recoater Blade or Roller]

    C1[Yb Fiber Laser 1070 nm] --> C2[Beam Expander and Collimator]
    C2 --> C3[Galvanometer Scanner XY]
    C3 --> C4[F Theta Focusing Lens]
    C4 --> C5[Laser Spot on Powder Bed approx 100 um]

    A5 --> C1
    B3 --> C5
    C5 --> D1[Melt Pool Formation]
    D1 --> D2[Rapid Solidification 10^4 to 10^6 K per s]
    D2 --> D3[One Solidified Layer]

    D3 --> E1{More Layers?}
    E1 -- Yes --> B6
    E1 -- No --> E2[Stress Relief Heat Treatment]
    E2 --> E3[Depowdering and Support Removal]
    E3 --> E4[Optional HIP Hot Isostatic Pressing]
    E4 --> E5[Final Machined DMLS Part]
```

## 4.2 Process Parameter Control — Closed-Loop Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Desired VED Target] --> S2[Process Controller]
    S2 --> S3[Parameter Set: P, v, h, t_l]
    S3 --> S4[Laser Powder Bed Fusion]
    S4 --> S5[Melt Pool Monitoring - Pyrometer]
    S5 --> S6[Actual Melt Pool Dimensions]
    S6 --> S7[Compare with Target]
    S7 --> S8{Within Tolerance?}
    S8 -- No --> S9[Adjust P and v Real Time]
    S9 --> S2
    S8 -- Yes --> S10[Continue Build]
```

## 4.3 Powder Handling Subsystem — Detailed Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Hopper with Virgin Powder] --> P2[Sieve 63 um for Defect Removal]
    P2 --> P3[Build Chamber Powder Reservoir Piston rises by t_l]
    P3 --> P4[Recoater Spreads Powder across Build Platform]
    P4 --> P5[Excess Powder collected in Overflow Chamber]
    P5 --> P6[Sieve and Reuse if Recyclable]
    P6 --> P7[Powder Property Degradation over multiple cycles]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Interpretation Tip (for KTU):** When asked to *draw a labeled schematic of a DMLS machine*, use the three key subsystems as your mental checklist: **(1) Laser delivery system** (laser + scanner + lens), **(2) Powder handling system** (feed piston + recoater + build piston), and **(3) Process atmosphere control** (inert gas inlet + O₂ sensor). Examiners award marks for explicitly labeling these.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

> **Q1.** Define **Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS)** and state **any two** of its industrial applications.

**Model Answer (10–12 lines):**
DMLS is a laser-based powder bed fusion additive manufacturing process in which a high-power Yb-fiber laser (1070 nm) selectively scans and fully melts regions of a thin metal powder layer, fusing them in an inert atmosphere to build fully dense metallic parts layer-by-layer. **(Definition — 1.5 marks)**

*Industrial Applications (any two — 1.5 marks):*
1. **Aerospace:** Topology-optimized structural brackets and fuel-injector nozzles in aerospace.
2. **Medical:** Patient-specific Ti-6Al-4V cranial plates and acetabular hip cups in orthopedics.
3. **Tooling:** Conformal-cooled injection mold inserts reducing cycle time by 20–40 %.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

> **Q2.** What is meant by **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** in DMLS? Write its formula with units.

**Model Answer:**
Volumetric Energy Density is defined as the total laser energy delivered per unit volume of metal powder bed during the DMLS process. It is a single composite metric that captures the combined influence of laser power, scan speed, hatch spacing, and layer thickness. **(Definition — 2 marks)**

$$E_V = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t_l} \quad [\text{J/mm}^3] \tag{Formula + Units — 1 mark}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Students frequently **forget the units** in 3-mark questions and lose 0.5–1 mark. Always write $[\text{J/mm}^3]$ for VED, $[\text{J/mm}]$ for LED, and $[\text{J/mm}^2]$ for AED.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Choose either Question A or Question B.**

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

> **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block schematic, explain the **working principle of DMLS**. List the major subsystems and their functions.
>
> **(b) [7 marks)** Explain the four primary process parameters governing DMLS. Discuss how each affects the **melt pool geometry** and **part density**.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Working Principle (7 marks):**

DMLS works on the principle of **selective, full melting of metal powder** by a high-power laser to build parts layer-by-layer inside a sealed, inert-atmosphere build chamber. The major subsystems are:

| # | Subsystem | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Yb-fiber laser source** (1070 nm, 200 W – 1 kW) | Provides the thermal energy for melting metal powder. |
| 2 | **Galvanometer scanner + F-theta lens** | Steers the laser beam in 2D across the powder bed with a focused spot of ~100 µm. |
| 3 | **Powder delivery & recoater** | Spreads a uniform powder layer of thickness $t_l$ across the build platform. |
| 4 | **Build chamber & atmosphere control** | Maintains inert (Ar/N₂) atmosphere with O₂ < 50 ppm. |
| 5 | **Build platform (Z-piston)** | Descends by $t_l$ after each layer to allow recoating. |
| 6 | **Build processor & slicer** | Slices the CAD model into 2D layers and generates scan path. |

**Process cycle:** (1) Recoat powder → (2) Laser scan the cross-section → (3) Solidify → (4) Lower platform → Repeat. After build, the part undergoes **stress relief** and **depowdering**. *(See SECTION 4.1 schematic.)*

**Part (b) — Process Parameters (7 marks):**

The four primary parameters are **laser power $P$ (W), scan speed $v$ (mm/s), hatch spacing $h$ (mm), and layer thickness $t_l$ (mm)**.

- **Laser Power ($P$):** Higher $P$ increases the **depth** and **width** of the melt pool. Excess $P$ causes **keyholing** (vapor-driven porosity) due to recoil pressure.
- **Scan Speed ($v$):** Higher $v$ reduces the energy per unit length ($E_L = P/v$), producing a **shallower** melt pool. Excess $v$ leads to **lack of fusion** porosity.
- **Hatch Spacing ($h$):** Larger $h$ leaves **gaps between adjacent scan tracks** that are not re-melted, creating inter-track porosity. Smaller $h$ increases overlap and density but reduces build rate.
- **Layer Thickness ($t_l$):** Larger $t_l$ requires deeper melt pools to bond to the previous layer. Excess $t_l$ causes **delamination** and anisotropy. Smaller $t_l$ improves vertical resolution and density.

**Combined Effect:** These four parameters together determine the **Volumetric Energy Density** $E_V = P/(v \cdot h \cdot t_l)$, which is the key tuning parameter. The optimal $E_V$ window for 316L stainless steel is ~50–110 J/mm³, yielding > 99.5 % relative density.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

> **(a) [7 marks]** Define **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)**. A DMLS process uses $P = 370~\text{W}$, $v = 1200~\text{mm/s}$, $h = 0.12~\text{mm}$, $t_l = 0.030~\text{mm}$. Calculate VED and comment on the regime.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **microstructure and defects** produced in DMLS parts. Compare **lack of fusion** and **keyhole** porosity.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — VED Calculation (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Volumetric Energy Density is the total laser energy delivered per unit volume of powder bed:
$$E_V = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t_l} \quad [\text{J/mm}^3]$$

**Calculation (4 marks):**
$$E_V = \dfrac{370}{1200 \times 0.12 \times 0.030}$$

- $1200 \times 0.12 = 144$
- $144 \times 0.030 = 4.32$

$$E_V = \dfrac{370}{4.32} = 85.65~\text{J/mm}^3 \approx 85.6~\text{J/mm}^3$$

**Regime comment (1 mark):** This VED (~85.6 J/mm³) lies within the **optimal process window** for most steels (50–110 J/mm³), suggesting a near-fully-dense part (> 99.5 % relative density).

**Part (b) — Microstructure & Defects (7 marks):**

**Microstructure (3 marks):**
- DMLS cooling rates are $10^4$–$10^6~\text{K/s}$, leading to **fine, non-equilibrium microstructures** (e.g., martensite in steels, fine α' in Ti-6Al-4V).
- **Crystallographic texture:** Columnar grains grow epitaxially along the build direction (Z-axis) due to directional heat extraction, causing **mechanical anisotropy** (often lower ductility in Z).
- **Grain refinement:** Laser re-melting of the previous layer creates very fine, dendritic sub-structures.

**Defect Comparison (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Lack of Fusion | Keyhole |
|---|---|---|
| **Cause** | Insufficient energy input ($E_V$ too low) | Excessive energy input ($E_V$ too high) |
| **Pore Shape** | Irregular, elongated, sharp-edged | Spherical, smooth, often with entrapped gas |
| **Pore Location** | Inter-layer and inter-track | Within melt pool interior |
| **Melt Pool Regime** | Conduction mode | Deep vapor depression (recoil pressure) |
| **Effect on Density** | Reduces density; stress concentrators | Reduces fatigue life; reduces density |
| **Remedy** | Increase $P$, decrease $v$, decrease $h$ or $t_l$ | Decrease $P$, increase $v$ |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Failing to write units** in formula-based answers (loses 0.5–1 mark per question).
> 2. **Confusing DMLS with binder jetting or polymer SLS** — clearly state the process involves **full melting of metal powder** under **inert atmosphere**.
> 3. **Skipping the schematic in 7-mark questions** — the examiner's mark split is typically: schematic (2 marks) + explanation (3 marks) + sub-points (2 marks). Always draw a labeled diagram.
> 4. **Writing "more power = better part"** — incorrect. Excess power causes keyholing. Show that there is an *optimum window*.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention support structures and stress relief** when describing the full DMLS workflow.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DMLS** = laser powder bed fusion of metals; full melting (not sintering) of metallic powders.
- Process occurs in an **inert atmosphere** (Ar or N₂) with O₂ < 50 ppm.
- **Atmospheric control** is critical for reactive metals (Ti, Al).
- The **four prime process parameters** are: **P, v, h, t_l**.
- **Volumetric Energy Density (VED):** $E_V = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t_l)$ in $\text{J/mm}^3$ — *the most tested formula in KTU exams.*
- **Optimal VED window** for 316L steel: ~50–110 J/mm³ → relative density > 99.5 %.
- **Lack of fusion porosity** = low VED (irregular pores).
- **Keyhole porosity** = high VED (spherical pores from vapor recoil).
- **Cooling rate** in DMLS: $10^4$–$10^6~\text{K/s}$ → fine martensitic/cellular microstructure.
- **Columnar grains** grow along build direction → **anisotropic mechanical properties**.
- **Support structures** are required for overhangs > 45° and act as thermal + mechanical anchors.
- **Post-processing steps:** Stress relief (~600 °C for steels) → Depowdering → Optional **HIP** (Hot Isostatic Pressing) for fatigue-critical parts.
- **DMLS vs SLM:** Technically the same physics; DMLS is an EOS trademark.
- **DMLS vs EBM:** DMLS uses laser (inert Ar) for steels/Ti/Al; EBM uses electron beam (vacuum) for Ti/CoCr.
- **Build rate equation:** $\dot{V}_B = v \cdot h \cdot t_l$ in $\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$.
- **Applications:** Aerospace brackets, medical implants (Ti-6Al-4V), conformal-cooled tooling inserts.

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-Second KTU Revision Mantra:** *"DMLS = Laser + Metal Powder + Inert Gas + Layer-by-Layer + 4 Parameters + 1 Energy Density = Fully Dense Part."* Memorize the formula, the regime boundaries, and the porosity types — and you will comfortably score full marks on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robocasting, Metal AM Processes
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET867 - METAL ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Robocasting, Metal AM Processes

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Robocasting?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Robocasting** (also known as *robotic material extrusion* or *3D plotting*) is a **solid freeform fabrication (SFF)** technique in which a computer-controlled **robotic arm fitted with a syringe/nozzle** extrudes a highly viscous, paste-like material (ceramic, metal, or composite slurry) in a layer-by-layer fashion to build a three-dimensional green part, which is subsequently subjected to **drying, debinding, and sintering** to obtain a dense functional component.

It was pioneered at **Sandia National Laboratories (USA, late 1990s)** and is officially classified under **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021** as a variant of **Material Extrusion (MEX)** — but uniquely adapted for high-viscosity pastes rather than thermoplastic filaments.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Cake Icing Robot 🎂

Imagine a robotic baker holding a piping bag filled with thick royal icing. As the robot's nozzle moves along a pre-programmed path (X–Y plane), it squeezes out a continuous bead of icing onto a tray. Once one layer is complete, the tray lowers (or the nozzle rises), and a *second* layer is piped on top of the first. Layer after layer, a 3D cake decoration emerges. Now replace icing with **metal-loaded paste** and the tray with a build plate — that's **Robocasting** for metals!

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Unlike FDM (Fused Deposition Modeling) which melts a thermoplastic filament, Robocasting extrudes a **room-temperature viscous paste** that holds its shape purely by rheological (flow) properties. The part gains strength **only after post-processing** (sintering).

---

## 1.2 Overview of Major Metal AM Processes

Metal Additive Manufacturing encompasses **seven** principal process categories recognized by **ASTM F42 Committee**. Within the scope of Module 2, we focus on the four industrially dominant ones:

| Process Family | Heat Source | Feedstock Form | Typical Accuracy | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** | Laser / Electron Beam | Fine powder (15–45 µm) | ± 0.05–0.1 mm | Complex, high-density functional parts |
| **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** | Laser / Plasma / Arc | Powder or Wire | ± 0.25–1 mm | Large parts, repair, cladding |
| **Binder Jetting (BJT)** | No heat (chemical binder) | Metal powder | ± 0.1–0.2 mm (before sinter) | Large volumes, lattice structures |
| **Material Extrusion (MEX)** — incl. Robocasting | Thermal / No heat | Paste / Filament | ± 0.2 mm | Large, low-cost parts, ceramics & metals |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> For PEMET867 Module 2, students are expected to compare these processes on the basis of **(i) feedstock form**, **(ii) energy source & density**, **(iii) build envelope**, **(iv) part accuracy & density**, **(v) post-processing needs**, and **(vi) typical applications**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module

The following physical constants and metrics recur in every Metal AM process:

- **Power density** = $P / A$ (W/mm²) — laser/E-beam focal intensity
- **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** = $E_v$ (J/mm³) — single most important parameter
- **Hatch spacing** $h$ = **0.08–0.15 mm** (typical for SLM of Ti-6Al-4V)
- **Layer thickness** $t$ = **20–50 µm** (PBF) and **0.5–2 mm** (DED)
- **Powder particle size** = **15–45 µm** (PBF), **45–150 µm** (DED)
- **Inert gas** = Argon (PBF) / Helium (EBM) at O₂ < **100 ppm**
- **Build chamber vacuum** = **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵ mbar** (EBM)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Robocasting — Process Theory

### 2.1.1 Hardware Architecture

A standard Robocasting system consists of:

1. **6-axis articulated robot arm** (or gantry XYZ stage)
2. **Syringe/piston extruder** with precision auger or pneumatic drive
3. **Deposition nozzle** (typically **100 µm – 2 mm** inner diameter)
4. **Paste reservoir** with temperature control (15–25 °C)
5. **Build platform** (often heated to 40–80 °C to aid drying)
6. **CAD slicing software** (e.g., **RoboCAD, Slic3r** adapted for paste)

### 2.1.2 The Robocasting Process Chain

The process flow is **6-stage**:

1. **Slurry/Paste Preparation** — metal powder + binder (e.g., water + methylcellulose + plasticizer) mixed into a homogeneous paste with **solid loading 50–65 vol%**.
2. **CAD Slicing** — 3D model converted to toolpaths with spacing = nozzle diameter.
3. **Extrusion (Layer-wise deposition)** — paste is forced through nozzle at controlled flow rate $Q$ (mm³/s).
4. **Drying** (in-situ or post) — solvent evaporation to prevent cracking.
5. **Debinding** (thermal, 400–600 °C) — burnout of organic binder.
6. **Sintering** (in furnace, 1200–1400 °C for steels) — densification to > **95% theoretical density**.

### 2.1.3 Critical Rheological Property — Yield Stress

For successful Robocasting, the paste must behave as a **Bingham plastic**:

$$\tau = \tau_y + \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa)
- $\tau_y$ = **yield stress** (Pa) — typically **100–1000 Pa** for Robocasting pastes
- $\mu$ = plastic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The paste must flow when $\tau > \tau_y$ (during extrusion) but stay rigid when $\tau < \tau_y$ (after deposition) — preventing slumping under its own weight.

### 2.1.4 Volumetric Flow Rate Equation

The volume of paste deposited per unit time follows:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \cdot d_n^4 \cdot \Delta P}{128 \cdot \mu \cdot L}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $d_n$ = nozzle diameter (m)
- $\Delta P$ = applied pressure (Pa)
- $L$ = nozzle length (m)

---

## 2.2 Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) — Process Theory

### 2.2.1 Selective Laser Melting (SLM) / Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS)

A **high-power fiber laser** (typically **200 W – 1 kW**, wavelength **1060–1080 nm**) selectively melts regions of a pre-spread metal powder bed (layer thickness **20–50 µm**). The chamber is flooded with **argon** to keep O₂ < **100 ppm** to prevent oxidation.

**Process cycle per layer:**

1. Recoater blade spreads a fresh powder layer.
2. Laser scans the cross-section according to sliced CAD data.
3. Melt pool solidifies → solidifies to ~99.9% density.
4. Build platform descends by **Δz = layer thickness**.
5. Repeat.

### 2.2.2 Electron Beam Melting (EBM)

Uses a **high-power electron beam (3–15 kW)** focused by electromagnetic coils inside a **vacuum chamber (10⁻⁴ mbar)**. Beam is steered at speeds up to **10⁶ mm/s** by electromagnetic deflection (no mechanical galvo mirrors).

**Key differences from SLM:**

| Parameter | SLM | EBM |
|---|---|---|
| Atmosphere | Argon | Vacuum |
| Energy transfer | Photon → Lattice vibration | Electron kinetic → heat |
| Powder preheat | None | **700–900 °C** (built-in plate heating) |
| Residual stress | High | Low (due to preheat) |
| Surface finish | Better (~10 µm Ra) | Rougher (~25 µm Ra) |
| Material range | Most metals | Limited (Ti, Al, Cu, some steels) |

---

## 2.3 Directed Energy Deposition (DED) — Process Theory

### 2.3.1 Powder-Fed Laser DED (LMD / LENS)

A **laser** creates a melt pool on a substrate while **metal powder** is injected coaxially (or laterally) via a **nozzle with carrier gas (Ar)**. The head is mounted on a **5-axis gantry or robot arm**, allowing deposition on curved/vertical surfaces.

### 2.3.2 Wire-Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM)

Uses an **electric arc (GMAW/GTAW)** as the heat source and **wire** as feedstock. Highest deposition rates (up to **10 kg/h** for steel). Produces parts with **±1% dimensional accuracy**.

### 2.3.3 Electron Beam Additive Manufacturing (EBAM)

Similar to WAAM but uses an **electron beam** in vacuum — capable of depositing **titanium** and **niobium** at high rates.

---

## 2.4 Binder Jetting — Process Theory

A **liquid binder** is selectively jetted (inkjet-style, ~ **30–80 µm droplets**) onto a metal powder bed to bond particles together. **No heat is involved during printing** — the part is then **cured, depowdered, sintered (in furnace at 1200–1400 °C)**, often with **bronze infiltration** to fill residual porosity.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units | Notes / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | Volumetric Energy Density | J/mm³ | **40–120 J/mm³** for SLM Ti-6Al-4V |
| 2 | $E_L = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | Linear Energy Density | J/mm | Used for DED |
| 3 | $E_A = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h}$ | Areal Energy Density | J/mm² | Less common |
| 4 | $\tau = \tau_y + \mu \dot{\gamma}$ | Bingham plastic rheology | Pa | Robocasting pastes |
| 5 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi d_n^4 \Delta P}{128 \mu L}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille flow | m³/s | Paste extrusion |
| 6 | $\rho_{rel} = \dfrac{\rho_{actual}}{\rho_{theoretical}}$ | Relative density | dimensionless | > **99%** for SLM, > **95%** for Robocast+ sintered |
| 7 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | Mass deposition rate | kg/s | DED / WAAM |
| 8 | $R_{build} = \dfrac{V_{part}}{t_{build}}$ | Build rate | cm³/h | Key productivity metric |
| 9 | $\eta_{dep} = \dfrac{m_{deposited}}{m_{supplied}} \times 100$ | Deposition efficiency | % | Powder catchment in DED |
| 10 | $P_{density} = \dfrac{P}{\pi r_{spot}^2}$ | Power density at focal spot | W/mm² | > **10⁶ W/mm²** for SLM |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In Q1 formula, $v$ is **scan speed (mm/s)**, not travel speed. Students often mix them up. Also remember $h$ = hatch spacing (mm), $t$ = layer thickness (mm).

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Energy Density (VED) from First Principles

### Problem Statement
Derive the expression for **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** in terms of laser power $P$, scan speed $v$, hatch spacing $h$, and layer thickness $t$.

### Step 1 — Define Energy Delivered per Unit Length

The laser beam delivers power $P$ (in Watts = J/s). As it moves at scan speed $v$ (in mm/s), the energy delivered along a line of length $L$ (mm) is:

$$E_{line} = P \cdot \frac{L}{v}$$

**Reasoning:** Time taken to traverse length $L$ is $\Delta t = L/v$. Energy = Power × Time.

### Step 2 — Define the Volume Fused per Unit Length

For a single scan track, the cross-section of the fused region (melt pool) is approximately rectangular, with:
- Width = hatch spacing $h$ (mm)
- Depth = layer thickness $t$ (mm)

So, volume fused along length $L$:

$$V_{line} = h \cdot t \cdot L$$

### Step 3 — Energy Density as Energy per Unit Volume

By definition, energy density is the ratio of total energy delivered to total volume affected:

$$E_v = \frac{E_{line}}{V_{line}} = \frac{P \cdot \dfrac{L}{v}}{h \cdot t \cdot L}$$

The length $L$ cancels (a beautiful feature — VED is independent of scan path length!):

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

This is the **master equation** for PBF process parameter design.

### Step 4 — Sanity Check with Typical SLM Values

For Ti-6Al-4V in SLM:
- $P$ = **200 W = 200 J/s**
- $v$ = **1000 mm/s**
- $h$ = **0.1 mm**
- $t$ = **0.03 mm**

Plug in:

$$E_v = \frac{200}{1000 \times 0.1 \times 0.03} = \frac{200}{3} \approx 66.67 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

This falls within the optimal range of **60–80 J/mm³** reported in literature for Ti-6Al-4V — ✓ **the formula is consistent with reality**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mass Deposition Rate for WAAM

### Step 1 — Wire Feed Rate

Let $\dot{L}_w$ = wire feed rate (m/s), $d_w$ = wire diameter (m). The volumetric wire feed rate is:

$$\dot{V}_w = \frac{\pi d_w^2}{4} \cdot \dot{L}_w$$

### Step 2 — Convert Volume to Mass

With wire material density $\rho$:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}_w = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi d_w^2}{4} \cdot \dot{L}_w$$

### Step 3 — Typical Numerical Example

For **ER70S-6 steel wire** ($\rho$ = **7850 kg/m³**), $d_w$ = **1.2 mm = 1.2 × 10⁻³ m**, $\dot{L}_w$ = **5 m/min = 0.0833 m/s**:

$$\dot{m} = 7850 \times \frac{\pi \times (1.2 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} \times 0.0833$$

$$\dot{m} = 7850 \times \frac{\pi \times 1.44 \times 10^{-6}}{4} \times 0.0833$$

$$\dot{m} = 7850 \times 1.131 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.0833$$

$$\dot{m} = 7.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s} = 2.66 \text{ kg/h}$$

This is consistent with typical low-power WAAM systems — ✓.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Hagen–Poiseuille Flow for Robocasting Paste

### Step 1 — Navier–Stokes Simplification

For steady, laminar, incompressible flow in a cylindrical nozzle of radius $R$ (radius = $d_n/2$), the Navier–Stokes equation reduces to:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{du}{dr}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}$$

### Step 2 — Integrate Twice

Integrating once with respect to $r$:

$$r \frac{du}{dr} = \frac{r^2}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dz} + C_1$$

Applying boundary condition: velocity finite at $r = 0$ → $C_1 = 0$. Integrating again:

$$u(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}(r^2 - R^2)$$

### Step 3 — Integrate Velocity over Cross-Section to get Q

$$Q = \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi R^4}{8\mu}\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)$$

Replacing $R = d_n/2$ and $-dP/dz = \Delta P / L$:

$$Q = \frac{\pi (d_n/2)^4}{8\mu} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{\pi d_n^4 \Delta P}{128 \mu L}$$

This is the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** for paste flow in Robocasting — useful for predicting extrusion pressure requirements.

### Step 4 — Verification for Typical Paste

Let $d_n$ = **0.5 mm = 5×10⁻⁴ m**, $L$ = **10 mm = 0.01 m**, $\mu$ = **100 Pa·s**, $\Delta P$ = **5×10⁵ Pa (5 bar)**:

$$Q = \frac{\pi (5 \times 10^{-4})^4 \times 5 \times 10^5}{128 \times 100 \times 0.01}$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-14} \times 5 \times 10^5}{128}$$

$$Q = \frac{9.82 \times 10^{-8}}{128} = 7.67 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q = 0.767 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

Reasonable for a fine-feature Robocasting system — ✓.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameter Windows

| Process | Laser/E-beam Power | Scan Speed | Layer Thickness | Hatch Spacing | Energy Density |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SLM (Ti-6Al-4V) | 200–400 W | 800–1500 mm/s | 30 µm | 80–120 µm | 60–80 J/mm³ |
| DMLS (Inconel 718) | 250–400 W | 700–1200 mm/s | 40 µm | 100 µm | 70–100 J/mm³ |
| EBM (Ti-6Al-4V) | 3–15 kW | 10⁵–10⁶ mm/s | 50–70 µm | 100–200 µm | 20–50 J/mm³ |
| LMD (Stainless 316L) | 1–4 kW | 5–20 mm/s | 0.5–2 mm | 1–3 mm | 50–200 J/mm³ |
| WAAM (Steel) | 3–10 kW (arc equiv.) | 5–15 mm/s | 1–3 mm | 5–10 mm | 30–100 J/mm³ |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Robocasting. Mention any two key rheological requirements of the paste used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Robocasting is a solid freeform fabrication process in which a robotic arm extrudes a highly viscous paste (metal/ceramic loaded) through a nozzle in a layer-by-layer manner to build a 3D green part, which is subsequently dried, debound, and sintered.

**Rheological Requirements (1.5 marks):**
1. **High yield stress (τy > 100 Pa):** the paste must hold its shape immediately after extrusion without slumping.
2. **Shear-thinning behavior:** viscosity drops under shear (during nozzle flow) but recovers at rest — enabling smooth extrusion + shape retention.
3. **High solid loading (50–65 vol%):** ensures low shrinkage on sintering and high final density.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Compare SLM and EBM based on (i) energy source, (ii) atmosphere, and (iii) residual stress.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | SLM | EBM |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Energy source | Fiber laser (1064 nm), 200 W–1 kW | Electron beam, 3–15 kW (vacuum) |
| (ii) Atmosphere | Argon (O₂ < 100 ppm) | Vacuum (10⁻⁴ mbar) |
| (iii) Residual stress | High (no preheat) | Low (powder bed preheated to 700–900 °C) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **Powder Bed Fusion (SLM)** process for metal additive manufacturing. Discuss the role of process parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** and explain how it influences the density and defects in SLM parts. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a):

**Schematic (2 marks):** Refer to SECTION 4.3 — the PBF functional architecture diagram shows the laser source, galvo scanner, F-theta lens, build chamber with Ar atmosphere, recoater, substrate, powder hoppers, and CNC controller.

**Process Steps (3 marks):**
1. A thin layer (20–50 µm) of metal powder is spread by the recoater blade onto the build platform.
2. A high-power fiber laser (200 W – 1 kW) is directed via galvo mirrors and focused by an F-theta lens onto the powder surface.
3. The laser selectively melts the powder according to the sliced CAD cross-section, forming a melt pool that solidifies into a dense track.
4. The build platform descends by Δz, a new powder layer is spread, and the process repeats.
5. The chamber is sealed and flooded with **argon** to keep O₂ < **100 ppm**, preventing oxidation.

**Role of Process Parameters (2 marks):**

- **Laser Power (P):** Higher P → deeper penetration, but too high causes keyholing and evaporation.
- **Scan Speed (v):** Higher v → less energy input → may cause lack-of-fusion porosity.
- **Hatch spacing (h):** Smaller h → more overlap → higher density; larger h → porosity.
- **Layer thickness (t):** Thinner t → better resolution, finer features.
- **Powder morphology:** Spherical, flowable powders ensure uniform layer spreading.

---

#### Solution to A(b):

**Derivation (4 marks):** Refer to **Section 3.1** for the complete step-by-step derivation. Key result:

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$
