**Influence on Density and Defects (3 marks):**

- **Optimum VED window (60–80 J/mm³ for Ti-6Al-4V):** Produces > 99% density, low porosity.
- **Low VED (< 40 J/mm³):** **Lack-of-fusion defects** — insufficient melting, voids between tracks/layers.
- **High VED (> 120 J/mm³):** **Keyholing** — vaporization of metal creates deep vapor cavities; also balling effect from Marangoni instability.
- **Effect on microstructure:** Higher VED → coarser grains (slower cooling); lower VED → finer grains but possible lack-of-fusion.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Robocasting process** in detail with its process chain. List any four advantages and two limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **DED, PBF, Binder Jetting, and Robocasting** based on build rate, accuracy, density, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a):

**Process Explanation (3 marks):** Robocasting is a material extrusion-based AM process where a computer-controlled robotic arm extrudes a highly viscous metal/ceramic paste through a nozzle (100 µm – 2 mm) onto a substrate, building the part layer-by-layer. The paste exhibits Bingham plastic behavior (high yield stress, shear-thinning), allowing it to retain shape after extrusion.

**Process Chain (2 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.2 (Robocasting Mermaid flowchart)**:
Slurry Prep → Paste Mixing → CAD Slicing → Paste Extrusion → Layer Drying → Debinding → Sintering → Final Part.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. Low equipment cost (no laser or vacuum chamber required).
2. Can process materials that are difficult to melt (e.g., high-melting-point ceramics).
3. Scalable to large parts with multi-axis robot arms.
4. Low material waste (paste is fully utilized).

**Limitations (0.5 marks):**
1. Final part density is lower (90–97%) compared to SLM (>99%) due to sintering shrinkage (~15–20%).
2. Limited feature resolution compared to PBF; binder burnout can introduce cracking.

---

#### Solution to B(b):

**Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Process | Build Rate | Accuracy | Density | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PBF (SLM/EBM)** | Low (~5–20 cm³/h) | High (±0.05 mm) | > 99% | Aerospace brackets, medical implants, conformal cooling channels |
| **DED (LMD/WAAM)** | High (~50–500 cm³/h) | Moderate (±0.25–1 mm) | 95–99% | Large structural parts, repair, cladding, shipbuilding |
| **Binder Jetting** | Very High (~100–1000 cm³/h) | Moderate (±0.1–0.2 mm) | 90–95% (after sinter) | Lattice structures, low-cost batches, jewelry |
| **Robocasting** | Moderate (~10–50 cm³/h) | Moderate (±0.2 mm) | 90–97% (after sinter) | Ceramic cores & shells, large metal preforms, biomedical scaffolds |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Process name confusion:** Students often write "SLM = Selective Laser **Sintering**" — this is **WRONG**. Sintering is partial melting (used in DMLS for some materials). SLM = **full melting**.
> 2. **VED units:** Most common error is forgetting to convert **W to J/s** consistency. Always keep units consistent (J/mm³ final).
> 3. **Robocasting ≠ FDM:** Do NOT claim Robocasting uses a heated nozzle — it does **NOT** melt material in-situ.
> 4. **EBM atmosphere:** Many students incorrectly state EBM uses argon — it uses **vacuum**, not inert gas.
> 5. **WAAM = wire + arc**, NOT powder. Mixing this up costs 2–3 marks easily.
> 6. **Sintering shrinkage** in Robocasting must be mentioned — examiners specifically look for it.
> 7. **Process Chain order:** Slurry → Sintering is the order. Reversing these steps loses marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2**

### 🔹 Robocasting
- ✅ Belongs to **Material Extrusion (MEX)** family per ISO/ASTM 52900.
- ✅ Requires **Bingham plastic paste** (yield stress **100–1000 Pa**).
- ✅ **No in-situ melting** — final density via **post-sintering**.
- ✅ Solid loading **50–65 vol%**, sintering shrinkage **15–20%**.
- ✅ No laser, no vacuum → **low cost**, large parts possible.
- ✅ Developed at **Sandia National Labs (1990s)**.

### 🔹 Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)
- ✅ SLM uses **fiber laser (1064 nm, 200 W–1 kW)** + **Argon atmosphere**.
- ✅ EBM uses **electron beam (3–15 kW)** + **vacuum** + powder preheat to 700–900 °C.
- ✅ Layer thickness **20–50 µm**, hatch spacing **80–200 µm**.
- ✅ Optimal VED = **60–80 J/mm³** for Ti-6Al-4V → > 99% density.
- ✅ **Lack-of-fusion** at low VED, **keyholing** at high VED.

### 🔹 Directed Energy Deposition (DED)
- ✅ **Powder-fed (LMD/LENS)** for precision; **Wire-fed (WAAM)** for large parts.
- ✅ Multi-axis capability → repair and cladding possible.
- ✅ WAAM uses **GMAW/GTAW arc**; deposition rate up to **10 kg/h**.
- ✅ Lower accuracy (±0.25–1 mm) but high build rate.

### 🔹 Binder Jetting
- ✅ **No heat during printing** — only chemical binder.
- ✅ Requires **furnace sintering (1200–1400 °C)** + possible bronze infiltration.
- ✅ Highest build rate among metal AM processes.
- ✅ Suffers from **sintering shrinkage (~15–20%)** and distortion.

### 🔹 Master Equations to Memorize
- ✅ $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ — **VED**
- ✅ $E_L = \dfrac{P}{v}$ — **LED**
- ✅ $\tau = \tau_y + \mu \dot{\gamma}$ — **Bingham plastic**
- ✅ $Q = \dfrac{\pi d_n^4 \Delta P}{128 \mu L}$ — **Hagen–Poiseuille**
- ✅ $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_w^2}{4} \cdot \dot{L}_w$ — **Wire feed deposition rate**

### 🔹 Quick Comparison Memory Trick
> **"LASER-VACUUM-ARC-BINDER-ROBOT"** — the 5 metal AM process families in Module 2!
- **L**aser-based PBF (SLM, EBM)
- **A**rc/Wire DED (WAAM, EBAM)
- **S**elective (DMLS)
- **E**xtrusion (Robocasting)
- **R**apid (Binder Jetting)
- **V**acuum / **A**rgon atmosphere distinction

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Filament
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET867 - METAL ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Filament ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Metal Additive Manufacturing (MAM)**, a **filament** is a continuous, spoolable, composite feedstock rod of precisely controlled diameter (commonly **1.75 mm** or **2.85 mm**) consisting of a high loading of **metallic powder** uniformly dispersed and bound within a **thermoplastic binder matrix** (polymer + wax + plasticizer). It serves as the consumable feedstock in **Material Extrusion (MEX)** based metal AM processes, including **Fused Filament Fabrication of Metals (FFF-M)**, **Bound Metal Deposition (BMD)**, and **Metal Fused Filament Fabrication (MF³)**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Filament is a precursor feedstock in the extrusion-based route of metal AM. The process is a hybrid of polymer FFF and traditional powder metallurgy (debinding + sintering). ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine making a **chocolate chip cookie dough log**. You cannot bake chocolate chips alone — you mix them into a dough (the **binder**) so the chips can be shaped and handled. Once shaped, the dough is removed (the dough is the **binder**, the chips are the **metal powder**), and the chips are fused together by heat (this is **sintering**). The filament is exactly this "dough log" — it lets us 3D-print complex shapes using metal powder by temporarily packaging it in an easy-to-print polymer "dough." > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction:** A metal filament is **NOT** a solid metal wire. It is a **metal–polymer composite** (also called a *green feedstock* or *feedstock rod*). Roughly **55–65 vol %** is metal powder, the rest is a sacrificial binder that is removed before sintering. ### Standard Metrics for Metal Filaments (KTU 2024 Conventions) - **Filament Diameter:** **1.75 mm** (most common), 2.85 mm (legacy). Tolerance: **± 0.05 mm**. - **Metal Loading:** **50–65 vol %** (≈ 80–92 wt %, depending on metal density). - **Moisture Content:** **< 0.05 %** (critical; moisture causes steam-pop defects during printing). - **Spool Weight:** Typically **1–2 kg**. - **Glass Transition Temperature (T_g):** 60–110 °C depending on binder system. - **Melt Flow Index (MFI):** Typically 5–20 g/10 min at printing temperature. ### Common Commercial Metal Filament Systems | Trade Name / Process | Manufacturer | Metal System | |---|---|---| | 17-4 PH, 316L, H13, A2, D2 filaments | Markforged (FDM Metal) | Tool steels & SS | | Studio System filaments | Desktop Metal (BMD) | 17-4 PH, 316L, Ti, Cu, Inconel | | Ultrafuse 316L, 17-4PH | BASF Forward AM | Stainless steels | | MetLine | Sandvik | 316L, 17-4PH | | AddiLine | Addiwise | Various | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of a Metal Filament A metal filament is a **multi-component composite system** engineered for printability, debindability, and sinterability simultaneously. Its three functional phases are: 1. **Metal Powder Phase (the "filler"):** - Typically gas-atomized spherical powder. - Particle size: **D₅₀ ≈ 5–45 μm** (often bimodal distributions to maximize packing). - High apparent and tap density (≥ 4.5 g/cm³ for steels). 2. **Polymer Binder Phase (the "vehicle"):** - Often a **multi-component binder system** (primary backbone polymer + secondary sacrificial polymer + wax). - Example binder system: **Polyamide (PA) backbone + Polyethylene (PE) / wax filler** (this is the basis of BASF Ultrafuse and Markforged systems). The PA gives strength, the PE/wax is preferentially dissolved during solvent debinding, leaving a porous skeleton for thermal debinding. 3. **Interface / Coupling Agents:** - Surfactants and dispersants (e.g., stearic acid) to prevent powder agglomeration and improve metal–polymer wetting. - Critical for uniform extrusion without "metal sharkskin" or die-lip build-up. ### 2.2 Filament Manufacturing Process (Compounding Route) The dominant industrial process is **melt-compounding followed by extrusion**: 1. **Powder Pre-treatment:** Drying metal powder (to remove adsorbed moisture) and pre-heating to match binder melt temperature. 2. **Compounding:** Powder + binder pellets are fed into a **co-rotating twin-screw extruder** (L/D ≈ 40–48) at temperatures of **170–230 °C**. Intensive mixing distributes metal homogeneously. 3. **Melt Filtration:** Molten compound passes through a screen pack to remove agglomerates. 4. **Filament Extrusion:** Compound is pushed through a precision die of diameter slightly larger than the target (to compensate for draw-down and post-extrusion swelling). 5. **Cooling & Sizing:** Air or water cooling, then **laser-based diameter measurement** with closed-loop feedback to a capstan/puller for ± 0.02 mm control. 6. **Spooling:** Wound on plastic spools, vacuum-sealed in foil bags with desiccant. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why twin-screw and not single-screw?** Twin-screw extruders provide *conveying + distributive mixing + dispersive mixing* simultaneously, which is essential to break metal agglomerates and achieve uniform metal distribution. Single-screw extruders cannot reach the shear and mixing quality required for > 50 vol % metal loading. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Use `\vert` for absolute value (not `|`) to keep markdown tables intact. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Volumetric metal loading (from wt %) | $V_m = \dfrac{W_m / \rho_m}{W_m / \rho_m + W_b / \rho_b}$ | dimensionless | $W_m + W_b = 1$ | | 2 | Weight metal loading (from vol %) | $W_m = \dfrac{V_m \cdot \rho_m}{V_m \cdot \rho_m + (1 - V_m) \cdot \rho_b}$ | dimensionless | | | 3 | Theoretical green density | $\rho_g = V_m \cdot \rho_m + (1 - V_m) \cdot \rho_b$ | g/cm³ | Rule of mixtures | | 4 | Isotropic linear scaling factor (STL compensation) | $k = (V_m \cdot \rho_m \,/\, \rho_s)^{1/3}$ | dimensionless | $\rho_s$ = sintered density | | 5 | Linear sintering shrinkage | $S = 1 - k$ | % or fraction | $S \approx 15$–$20\,\%$ typical | | 6 | Volumetric sintering shrinkage | $S_V = 1 - V_m \cdot \rho_m \,/\, \rho_s$ | % or fraction | | | 7 | Filament mass per unit length | $m_L = \rho_g \cdot A = \rho_g \cdot \pi d^2 / 4$ | g/m | $d$ = filament diameter | | 8 | Powder volume fraction in printed part | $V_m^{printed} \approx V_m$ (ideal) | — | Slight reduction due to voids | | 9 | Required STL dimension for target dimension | $L_{STL} = L_{target} / k$ | mm | Magnification command | | 10 | Volumetric part cost estimate (filament basis) | $C = m_p \cdot c_f + m_s \cdot c_s$ | ₹ | $c_f$ = filament ₹/kg, $c_s$ = sintering cost | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Filament-based metal AM is the **lowest-cost entry point into metal 3D printing** because it uses **modified polymer FFF printers** (no laser, no electron beam, no inert chamber for the printing step). It democratizes metal AM for: - **Tooling inserts** (H13, A2) for injection molding and stamping. - **Jigs and fixtures** in automotive assembly lines. - **Functional prototypes** in stainless steel and titanium for medical and aerospace. - **Low-volume production** where conventional machining is uneconomical. However, the **sintering shrinkage (15–20 %)** is a major design consideration, requiring **CAD compensation** before printing. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Metal Loading from Weight Percentage **Given:** - Weight fraction of metal in filament: $W_m$ (e.g., 0.85) - Density of metal: $\rho_m$ (e.g., 7.85 g/cm³ for steel) - Density of binder: $\rho_b$ (e.g., 1.10 g/cm³ for polyamide-wax blend) **To find:** Volume fraction $V_m$ of metal in filament. **Derivation:** Consider 1 g of filament. It contains $W_m$ grams of metal and $W_b = 1 - W_m$ grams of binder. The volumes occupied are: $$V_{\text{metal}} = \frac{W_m}{\rho_m} \quad \text{and} \quad V_{\text{binder}} = \frac{W_b}{\rho_b}$$ Total volume: $$V_{\text{total}} = \frac{W_m}{\rho_m} + \frac{W_b}{\rho_b}$$ Volume fraction of metal (by definition): $$V_m = \frac{V_{\text{metal}}}{V_{\text{total}}} = \frac{W_m / \rho_m}{W_m / \rho_m + W_b / \rho_b}$$ **Numerical Example (BASF Ultrafuse 316L):** - $W_m = 0.88$ (88 wt % metal), $\rho_m = 7.98$ g/cm³ - $W_b = 0.12$ (12 wt % binder), $\rho_b = 1.05$ g/cm³ $$V_m = \frac{0.88 / 7.98}{0.88 / 7.98 + 0.12 / 1.05} = \frac{0.1103}{0.1103 + 0.1143} = \frac{0.1103}{0.2246} = 0.491$$ So **49.1 vol %** metal loading — this is typical for stainless steel filaments. ### 3.2 Derivation: Isotropic Sintering Shrinkage **Given:** - A "green" printed part of length $L_g$ with metal volume fraction $V_m$ and metal density $\rho_m$. - After debinding, only metal powder (with some porosity) remains. - After sintering, the part has length $L_s$ and density $\rho_s$ (close to $\rho_m$ for > 95 % dense parts). **Assumption:** Shrinkage is **isotropic** (equal in all three Cartesian directions). This is true when the green part is uniform (no preferred grain orientation from printing) and sintering is diffusion-controlled. **Derivation:** The mass of metal in the green part is conserved (no metal loss): $$m_m = \rho_m \cdot V_m \cdot V_g$$ After full densification (no residual porosity), the mass equals: $$m_m = \rho_s \cdot V_s$$ Equating: $$\rho_m \cdot V_m \cdot V_g = \rho_s \cdot V_s$$ Volume ratio: $$\frac{V_s}{V_g} = \frac{V_m \cdot \rho_m}{\rho_s}$$ For isotropic shrinkage, $V \propto L^3$, so: $$\left(\frac{L_s}{L_g}\right)^3 = \frac{V_m \cdot \rho_m}{\rho_s}$$ Linear scaling factor: $$k = \frac{L_s}{L_g} = \left(\frac{V_m \cdot \rho_m}{\rho_s}\right)^{1/3}$$ **Numerical Example:** - $V_m = 0.491$ (from §3.1) - $\rho_m = 7.98$ g/cm³, $\rho_s = 7.85$ g/cm³ (97 % dense, typical for sintered 316L) $$k = \left(\frac{0.491 \times 7.98}{7.85}\right)^{1/3} = \left(0.4992\right)^{1/3} = 0.7930$$ Linear shrinkage: $$S = 1 - k = 1 - 0.7930 = 0.2070 = 20.70\,\%$$ So a **50 mm** designed dimension must be printed as: $$L_{\text{STL}} = \frac{50}{0.7930} = 63.05 \text{ mm}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation: Filament Mass per Unit Length (for Cost / Process Planning) **Given:** Filament diameter $d$, green density $\rho_g$. **Derivation:** Cross-sectional area: $$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$ Mass per unit length: $$m_L = \rho_g \cdot A = \rho_g \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$ **Numerical Example:** - $d = 1.75$ mm = 0.175 cm - $\rho_g = V_m \rho_m + (1 - V_m) \rho_b = 0.491 \times 7.98 + 0.509 \times 1.05 = 3.918 + 0.534 = 4.452$ g/cm³ $$m_L = 4.452 \times \frac{\pi (0.175)^2}{4} = 4.452 \times 0.02405 = 0.1071 \text{ g/cm} = 10.71 \text{ g/m}$$ So a 1 kg spool contains $L = 1000 / 10.71 \times 1 \approx 93.4$ m of filament — a useful figure for production planning. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Filament Selection Decision Tree | Design Requirement | Recommended Filament | Reason | |---|---|---| | Corrosion resistance (316 SS) | Ultrafuse 316L, Markforged 316L | Standard for chemical / marine | | High hardness tooling (HRC > 55) | Markforged H13, A2, D2 | Hardenable tool steels | | Lightweight / biomedical | Ti-6Al-4V (Desktop Metal) | High specific strength, biocompatible | | High temp (gas turbine) | Inconel 625 / 718 filaments | Creep & oxidation resistance | | High thermal/electrical cond. | Pure Cu filament (Cu > 95 % IACS) | Heat sinks, bus bars | | Lowest cost prototype | 17-4 PH SS | Most mature, lowest powder cost | | Magnetic application | Fe–Si (electrical steel) filaments | Soft magnetic cores | ### 3.5 Worked-Out Problem (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style) **Problem:** A steel filament has 87 wt % 316L powder ($\rho_m = 7.98$ g/cm³) and 13 wt % polymer binder ($\rho_b = 1.08$ g/cm³). After printing, the part is debound and sintered to a density of 7.70 g/cm³. Calculate: (a) the volumetric metal loading, (b) the green density, and (c) the linear shrinkage. If a final sintered dimension of 80 mm is required, what STL dimension must be designed? **Solution:** **(a) Volumetric metal loading:** $$V_m = \frac{0.87 / 7.98}{0.87 / 7.98 + 0.13 / 1.08} = \frac{0.1090}{0.1090 + 0.1204} = \frac{0.1090}{0.2294} = 0.4752$$ So **47.52 vol %** metal. **(b) Green density (rule of mixtures):** $$\rho_g = 0.4752 \times 7.98 + 0.5248 \times 1.08 = 3.792 + 0.567 = 4.359 \text{ g/cm}^3$$ **(c) Linear shrinkage:** $$k = \left(\frac{V_m \cdot \rho_m}{\rho_s}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{0.4752 \times 7.98}{7.70}\right)^{1/3} = \left(0.4925\right)^{1/3} = 0.7889$$ $$S = 1 - 0.7889 = 0.2111 = 21.11\,\%$$ **(d) Required STL dimension for 80 mm final:** $$L_{STL} = \frac{80}{0.7889} = 101.41 \text{ mm}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked for shrinkage, always show the *scaling factor k* AND the *shrinkage S* separately. Marks are awarded for the intermediate step, not only the final number. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Filament Composition — Cross-Sectional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[FILAMENT CROSS SECTION
1.75 mm diameter]:::outer B[POLYMER BINDER MATRIX
50 to 55 volume percent
Polyamide + Wax blend]:::poly C[METAL POWDER PARTICLE 1
D50 22 micrometer
Spherical gas atomized]:::metal D[METAL POWDER PARTICLE 2
D50 18 micrometer
Spherical gas atomized]:::metal E[METAL POWDER PARTICLE 3
D50 25 micrometer
Spherical gas atomized]:::metal F[INTERFACE COUPLING AGENT
Stearic acid monolayer
Improves wetting]:::intf G[MOISTURE CONTENT
less than 0.05 percent]:::warn A --> B A --> C A --> D A --> E A --> F A --> G classDef outer fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#0a1f3a,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef poly fill:#e8a23a,stroke:#a0650a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef metal fill:#9aaab8,stroke:#506070,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef intf fill:#d4a574,stroke:#8a5530,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef warn fill:#b8341a,stroke:#7a1a0a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.2 Filament Manufacturing Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Metal Powder Feedstock
D50 5 to 45 um
Spherical gas atomized]:::feed P2[Polymer Binder Pellets
PA12 + PE Wax blend
Dried 80 C 4 hours]:::feed P3[Coupling Agent
Stearic acid 0.5 wt percent]:::feed M1[Step 1 Pre Drying
80 C vacuum 4 hours
Moisture below 0.05 percent]:::proc M2[Step 2 Twin Screw Compounding
180 to 230 C
L over D 40 to 48]:::proc M3[Step 3 Melt Filtration
200 um screen pack
Removes agglomerates]:::proc M4[Step 4 Die Extrusion
Die dia 1.95 mm
Draw down to 1.75 mm]:::proc M5[Step 5 Air Cooling
20 to 40 C air
Solidification]:::proc M6[Step 6 Laser Diameter Control
Closed loop capstan
Plus minus 0.02 mm]:::proc M7[Step 7 Spooling
Vacuum foil pack
Desiccant included]:::proc Q1[Quality Control Tests]:::qc Q2[Diameter Tolerance Check]:::qc Q3[MFI Test 5 to 20 g per 10 min]:::qc Q4[Metal Loading Verification
Target 55 to 65 vol percent]:::qc P1 --> M1 P2 --> M1 P3 --> M2 M1 --> M2 M2 --> M3 M3 --> M4 M4 --> M5 M5 --> M6 M6 --> M7 M7 --> Q1 Q1 --> Q2 Q1 --> Q3 Q1 --> Q4 Q4 --> OUT[Approved Spool
1 to 2 kg
Shelf life 12 months]:::final classDef feed fill:#d4dce4,stroke:#506070,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef proc fill:#2a6fa0,stroke:#0a3a5c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef qc fill:#e8a23a,stroke:#a0650a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef final fill:#2a8a3a,stroke:#0a4a1a,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.3 Filament → Green Part → Sintered Part — Process Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR F1[FILAMENT
1.75 mm
55 to 65 vol percent metal]:::stage1 F2[GREEN PRINTED PART
Binder still present
Same size as CAD]:::stage2 F3[BROWN PART after DEBINDING
Binder removed
Porous metal skeleton]:::stage3 F4[SINTERED PART
95 to 99 percent dense
Shrunk 15 to 20 percent]:::stage4 P1[FFF Printing
200 to 250 C nozzle]:::proc1 P2[Solvent Debind
40 to 60 C bath
12 to 24 hours]:::proc2 P3[Thermal Debind
400 to 600 C
Slow ramp 1 C per min]:::proc3 P4[Sintering
1200 to 1380 C for SS
Hold 2 to 4 hours]:::proc4 F1 --> P1 --> F2 F2 --> P2 --> F3 F3 --> P3 --> F3 F3 --> P4 --> F4 classDef stage1 fill:#9aaab8,stroke:#506070,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef stage2 fill:#c0a872,stroke:#806020,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef stage3 fill:#b89060,stroke:#78481a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef stage4 fill:#2a6fa0,stroke:#0a3a5c,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef proc1 fill:#e8a23a,stroke:#a0650a,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef proc2 fill:#e8a23a,stroke:#a0650a,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef proc3 fill:#e8a23a,stroke:#a0650a,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef proc4 fill:#c42a1a,stroke:#7a1a0a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Reading Note:** Note the **two-stage debinding** in the process pipeline — this is critical. Solvent debinding extracts the soluble binder (wax/PE) first to create open porosity, then thermal debinding removes the remaining backbone polymer (PA) by pyrolysis. Skipping solvent debinding causes violent blistering and cracking of the part. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]** *Define a metal filament used in additive manufacturing. List any four commonly available metal filament systems.* **Model Answer:** A metal filament is a spoolable, composite feedstock of precisely controlled diameter (typically 1.75 mm or 2.85 mm) consisting of high-loading metal powder (50–65 vol %) uniformly dispersed in a thermoplastic binder matrix. It is used as the consumable feedstock in extrusion-based metal AM processes (FFF-M / BMD). Four commercial systems: (i) 17-4 PH SS, (ii) 316L SS, (iii) H13 tool steel, (iv) Ti-6Al-4V. *(Any four from: 17-4 PH, 316L, H13, A2, D2, Inconel 625, Inconel 718, Cu, Ti-6Al-4V, 4140, 4340 — Markforged / Desktop Metal / BASF Ultrafuse series.)* **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]** *Why is a multi-component (two-polymer + wax) binder system used in metal filaments instead of a single polymer?* **Model Answer:** A multi-component binder is used because: 1. **Solvent debinding selectivity:** A soluble component (e.g., wax/PE) can be preferentially dissolved to create interconnected porosity, allowing the backbone polymer (e.g., PA12) to escape gently during thermal debinding. 2. **Mechanical strength during printing:** The backbone polymer (PA12) provides the green strength to support overhangs and thin walls. 3. **Controlled pyrolysis:** A two-stage debind (solvent + thermal) prevents violent gas evolution that would crack the part if a single polymer were burnt out in one step. 4. **Lower thermal expansion mismatch** with metal powder during cooling. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Module-2 Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. #### ❑ Question A (14 Marks) — Manufacturing & Properties Focus **[A] (a)** Discuss in detail the **manufacturing process of metal filaments** by the twin-screw extrusion route. Explain the role of each processing stage and the critical quality control parameters. **[A] (b)** A 316L stainless steel filament contains 86 wt % metal powder ($\rho_m = 7.98$ g/cm³) and 14 wt % polymer binder ($\rho_b = 1.10$ g/cm³). After printing and sintering, the final part achieves 96 % theoretical density. Determine: (i) the volumetric metal loading of the filament, (ii) the green density, (iii) the linear sintering shrinkage, and (iv) the STL dimension required to obtain a 100 mm final dimension. **Model Solution [A] (a):** The manufacturing process of metal filaments by twin-screw extrusion consists of seven stages: 1. **Powder Pre-treatment (Drying):** Gas-atomized metal powder is dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 4 hours to drive off adsorbed moisture (target < 0.05 %). This prevents steam-pop defects during extrusion. 2. **Binder Pre-mixing:** The polymer binder pellets (typically PA12 backbone + PE/wax filler + dispersant) are dry-blended with the metal powder in a tumbling mixer to ensure homogeneous feed composition. 3. **Twin-Screw Compounding:** The dry blend is fed into a co-rotating intermeshing twin-screw extruder (L/D ≈ 40–48) at 180–230 °C. The screws provide conveying, distributive, and dispersive mixing — essential to break metal agglomerates and achieve uniform > 50 vol % metal loading without "unmixed lumps." A vacuum degassing port removes entrapped air. 4. **Melt Filtration:** The molten compound is forced through a 100–200 μm screen pack to remove unmelted polymer, hard agglomerates, and foreign particles that would otherwise clog the filament die or cause print defects. 5. **Filament Extrusion:** The filtered melt is extruded through a precision die (typical 1.90–2.00 mm bore, slightly larger than target to allow for draw-down). The throughput is metered by a gear pump for diameter stability. 6. **Cooling & Diameter Control:** The molten filament is solidified in a controlled air-cooled trough (20–40 °C). A **laser-based diameter gauge** with a closed-loop PID controller drives the capstan puller speed to maintain diameter within **± 0.02 mm**. This tolerance is critical — a 0.05 mm deviation in 1.75 mm filament causes > 10 % local extrusion error. 7. **Spooling & Packaging:** Filament is wound onto plastic spools under controlled tension (to avoid plastic deformation of the warm filament) and immediately vacuum-sealed in aluminium-foil pouches with desiccant. Shelf life: 12 months sealed. **Critical Quality Control Parameters:** - **Diameter tolerance:** ± 0.05 mm (ASTM/ISO standard). - **Melt Flow Index (MFI):** 5–20 g/10 min — too low causes nozzle clogging, too high causes over-extrusion. - **Metal loading:** Verified by TGA (thermogravimetric analysis) ash test; target ± 2 vol %. - **Moisture content:** < 0.05 % by Karl Fischer titration. - **Homogeneity:** Cross-sectional SEM-EDS at three points; no visible agglomerates > 100 μm. - **Roundness:** Ovality < 0.03 mm. - **Mechanical:** Tensile strength > 25 MPa, elongation > 5 % (to survive spooling and feeding). **Model Solution [A] (b):** **Given:** $W_m = 0.86$, $\rho_m = 7.98$ g/cm³, $W_b = 0.14$, $\rho_b = 1.10$ g/cm³, $\rho_s = 0.96 \times 7.98 = 7.66$ g/cm³. **(i) Volumetric metal loading:** $$V_m = \frac{W_m / \rho_m}{W_m / \rho_m + W_b / \rho_b} = \frac{0.86 / 7.98}{0.86 / 7.98 + 0.14 / 1.10} = \frac{0.1078}{0.1078 + 0.1273} = \frac{0.1078}{0.2351} = 0.4585$$ So **45.85 vol %**. **(ii) Green density (rule of mixtures):** $$\rho_g = V_m \rho_m + (1 - V_m) \rho_b = 0.4585 \times 7.98 + 0.5415 \times 1.10 = 3.659 + 0.596 = 4.255 \text{ g/cm}^3$$ **(iii) Linear scaling factor & shrinkage:** $$k = \left(\frac{V_m \cdot \rho_m}{\rho_s}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{0.4585 \times 7.98}{7.66}\right)^{1/3} = \left(0.4777\right)^{1/3} = 0.7809$$ $$S = 1 - 0.7809 = 0.2191 = 21.91\,\%$$ **(iv) STL dimension for 100 mm final:** $$L_{STL} = \frac{100}{0.7809} = 128.06 \text{ mm}$$ #### ❑ Question B (14 Marks) — Post-Processing & Shrinkage Focus **[B] (a)** Explain the **post-processing of filament-based metal AM parts**, covering solvent debinding, thermal debinding, and sintering. Discuss the critical process parameters and typical defect modes if any stage is mismanaged. **[B] (b)** A pure copper filament is 90 wt % Cu powder ($\rho_m = 8.96$ g/cm³) and 10 wt % binder ($\rho_b = 1.05$ g/cm³). Sintering is done in a hydrogen atmosphere and achieves 94 % theoretical density. For a part requiring a final sintered length of 60 mm, calculate: (i) vol % Cu, (ii) green density, (iii) linear shrinkage, and (iv) the corresponding STL file dimension. **Model Solution [B] (a):** Filament-based metal AM requires **three sequential post-processing stages** to convert the "green" printed part into a fully metallic functional part: **Stage 1 — Solvent Debinding (Catalytic / Immersion):** - The green part is immersed in a solvent bath (e.g., **nitric acid for catalytic debinding of POM/Acetal-based binder**, or **heptane/acetone for wax-based binder**) at 40–60 °C for 12–24 hours. - The soluble component (typically the secondary binder — wax or acetal) dissolves out, creating an **interconnected open porosity** network (≈ 30–50 % of binder volume). - *Critical parameters:* solvent temperature, bath agitation, immersion time. Under-debinding leaves residual soluble binder, causing blistering in Stage 2. Over-debinding has no major harm. - *Defect:* **Blistering & cracking** if the soluble component is not removed first, since pyrolysis of a non-porous polymer generates internal pressure. **Stage 2 — Thermal Debinding (Pyrolysis):** - The part is heated in a furnace under inert atmosphere (Ar or N₂) or in air (for stainless steels) through a slow ramp: typically **1 °C/min from 200 °C to 600 °C**, with isothermal holds at the polymer decomposition temperatures (e.g., 400 °C and 550 °C). - The remaining backbone polymer decomposes into small gas molecules (CO, CO₂, H₂O, low-MW hydrocarbons) and diffuses out through the open porosity left by Stage 1. - At the end of Stage 2, only the **"brown" part** — a fragile, porous metal skeleton (40–50 vol % metal, 50–60 vol % air gaps) — remains. - *Defect:* **Carbon residue / contamination** if heating rate is too fast or atmosphere is reducing; this ruins mechanical and corrosion properties in SS parts. **Stage 3 — Sintering:** - The brown part is heated to **70–90 % of the metal's melting temperature** (e.g., 1200–1380 °C for 316L SS, 950–1050 °C for Cu, 1250–1350 °C for 17-4 PH) for 2–4 hours in vacuum or H₂ atmosphere. - Atomic diffusion (solid-state) bonds adjacent metal particles, eliminating porosity and densifying to 95–99 % of theoretical density. - Part shrinks **15–20 %** linearly and **40–55 %** volumetrically. - *Critical parameters:* peak temperature, dwell time, ramp rate (2–5 °C/min above 600 °C), atmosphere. H₂ removes surface oxides, especially important for Cu and Ti. - *Defect:* **Distortion & warping** if sintering is too fast, **residual porosity** if temperature is too low, **melting & slumping** if temperature exceeds solidus. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often describe *only* sintering and forget the two debinding stages. Marks are split roughly 3 + 3 + 8 for solvent-debind, thermal-debind, sintering respectively. Missing the debinding stages costs at least 6 of 7 marks in part (a). **Model Solution [B] (b):** **Given:** $W_m = 0.90$, $\rho_m = 8.96$ g/cm³, $W_b = 0.10$, $\rho_b = 1.05$ g/cm³, $\rho_s = 0.94 \times 8.96 = 8.42$ g/cm³. **(i) Volumetric metal loading:** $$V_m = \frac{0.90 / 8.96}{0.90 / 8.96 + 0.10 / 1.05} = \frac{0.10045}{0.10045 + 0.09524} = \frac{0.10045}{0.19569} = 0.5133$$ So **51.33 vol %**. **(ii) Green density:** $$\rho_g = 0.5133 \times 8.96 + 0.4867 \times 1.05 = 4.599 + 0.511 = 5.110 \text{ g/cm}^3$$ **(iii) Linear shrinkage:** $$k = \left(\frac{V_m \cdot \rho_m}{\rho_s}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{0.5133 \times 8.96}{8.42}\right)^{1/3} = \left(0.5463\right)^{1/3} = 0.8162$$ $$S = 1 - 0.8162 = 0.1838 = 18.38\,\%$$ **(iv) STL dimension for 60 mm final:** $$L_{STL} = \frac{60}{0.8162} = 73.51 \text{ mm}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Student Errors (Valuation Pitfalls):** > 1. **Mixing up $W$ and $V$** in the metal-loading formula — always verify by computing both $W$ and $V$ and checking they sum correctly. > 2. **Forgetting the $V_m$ factor in shrinkage** — the formula $(ρ_g/ρ_s)^{1/3}$ is for a fully-dense green body of a *single-phase* material. For a metal–binder system, you MUST use $V_m \cdot \rho_m / \rho_s$. > 3. **Using $ρ_m$ instead of $ρ_s$** for the sintered density when the part is not 100 % dense (e.g., 96 % or 94 %). Use the *actual* sintered density. > 4. **Forgetting to convert % to fraction** in $W_m$ — using 86 instead of 0.86 gives wrong answers by a factor of 100. > 5. **Reporting only the scaling factor $k$** when the question asks for shrinkage $S$ — these are different quantities. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Filament** = metal powder (50–65 vol %) + thermoplastic binder, 1.75 mm diameter, ± 0.05 mm tolerance. - ✅ A filament is **NOT** a pure metal wire — it is a **metal–polymer composite** (green feedstock). - ✅ Manufacturing is via **twin-screw melt compounding + die extrusion + laser diameter control**. - ✅ **Multi-component binders** (PA12 + PE/Wax) are essential for two-stage debinding (solvent then thermal). - ✅ Post-processing is **3 stages**: solvent debind → thermal debind → sinter. - ✅ Sintering causes **15–20 % linear shrinkage** and **40–55 % volumetric shrinkage** — CAD must be scaled up by factor $1/k$ before printing. - ✅ Key formula: $\,V_m = \dfrac{W_m/\rho_m}{W_m/\rho_m + W_b/\rho_b}$ - ✅ Key formula: $\,k = \left(\dfrac{V_m \cdot \rho_m}{\rho_s}\right)^{1/3}\,$, $\,S = 1 - k$ - ✅ Key formula: $\,\rho_g = V_m \rho_m + (1 - V_m) \rho_b\,$ (rule of mixtures). - ✅ **Moisture** in filament < 0.05 % is non-negotiable; steam pops ruin prints. - ✅ Filament-based metal AM is the **lowest-cost entry into metal 3D printing** (modified polymer FFF printers). - ✅ Typical commercial systems: Markforged (17-4 PH, 316L, H13, A2, D2), Desktop Metal (Ti, Cu, Inconel), BASF Ultrafuse (316L, 17-4 PH), Sandvik MetLine. - ✅ Common failure modes: **diameter out-of-tolerance** → over/under extrusion; **binder residue** → blistering; **too-rapid sintering** → warping & distortion; **oxide contamination** → loss of mechanical/corrosion properties. - ✅ Always state **all three quantities** ($V_m$, $\rho_g$, $S$) in a filament-related numerical — partial answers lose marks.

topics.title: Powder and Sheet Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET867 - METAL ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Powder and Sheet Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Powder-Based Additive Manufacturing Systems

**Powder-based AM systems** are a class of metal additive manufacturing processes in which metallic powders — typically in the size range of **15 µm to 150 µm** — are used as the raw feedstock. The powder is selectively fused, melted, or sintered layer-by-layer using a high-energy heat source such as a **laser (fiber laser, Nd:YAG)**, **electron beam**, or **plasma arc**, following cross-sections derived from a 3D CAD model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The two principal Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) routes studied are:
> 1. **Selective Laser Melting (SLM) / Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS)** — uses a laser.
> 2. **Electron Beam Melting (EBM)** — uses an electron beam in a vacuum.

### 1.2 Sheet-Based Additive Manufacturing Systems

**Sheet-based (or sheet lamination) AM systems** use thin metallic foils or sheets — typically **0.05 mm to 0.5 mm** thick — as the feedstock. Sheets are stacked and bonded layer-by-layer using mechanisms such as **ultrasonic welding, brazing, adhesive bonding, or thermal cutting**. Material is removed by contour cutting (laser or knife) to define the geometry of each layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Sheet-Based Process:** **Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing (UAM)** is the dominant industrial sheet AM process for metals, developed by **Fabrisonic** (founded 1990s). It bonds sheets using ultrasonic vibrations at low temperatures (<50% of melting point).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Layered Cake Metaphor:**
> - **Powder-based AM** is like building a 3D object by sprinkling fine sand layer-by-layer, and using a focused magnifying glass (laser/electron beam) to fuse each sand layer into a solid cross-section.
> - **Sheet-based AM** is like building a 3D object by stacking sheets of paper one at a time, and using a glue-stick and scissors (ultrasonic welder + cutter) to bond and trim each sheet into shape.
>
> The "ingredient" in powder AM is granular and can take any complex 2D shape; in sheet AM, the "ingredient" is pre-formed flat stock, so geometry is defined by what you **cut away** (subtractive per layer).

## 1.4 Standard Physical & Process Metrics

- **Typical layer thickness (Powder):** 20 µm – 100 µm
- **Typical layer thickness (Sheet):** 50 µm – 500 µm
- **Powder size distribution:** D10 ≈ 15–25 µm, D50 ≈ 25–45 µm, D90 ≈ 45–150 µm
- **Laser power (SLM):** 200 W – 1 kW (fiber laser, wavelength **1070 nm**)
- **Electron beam power (EBM):** 1 kW – 15 kW
- **Build chamber atmosphere (SLM):** Inert gas (Argon or Nitrogen)
- **Build chamber atmosphere (EBM):** Vacuum (~10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵ mbar, then partial He)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Powder-Based AM Systems

### 2.1.1 Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) Systems
These systems use a **recoater blade or roller** to spread a thin layer of powder across a build platform, then a heat source selectively scans the cross-section.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. CAD model is sliced into 2D layers (typical thickness **20–100 µm**).
2. Build platform is lowered by one layer thickness.
3. **Recoater spreads powder** from the feed bed to the build bed.
4. **Heat source** (laser/electron beam) selectively scans the cross-section.
5. Powder particles fuse via melting (SLM/DMLS) or partial sintering.
6. Platform lowers, recoater spreads next layer — **process repeats**.
7. After completion, **unfused powder supports** overhangs and is recycled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is EBM done under vacuum?** Electrons travel in straight lines only in vacuum; any gas would scatter the beam. Vacuum also prevents oxidation of reactive metals like Titanium at high temperatures (~700°C bed temperature).

### 2.1.2 Blown Powder / Directed Energy Deposition (DED)
In DED, powder is injected through nozzles coaxial to the laser beam directly into the melt pool. This is the second major powder-based category.

## 2.2 Powder Characteristics — The "7 Pillars"

Powder quality governs final part density, surface roughness, and mechanical properties.

| Pillar | Description | Ideal Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Particle Size** | Mean diameter D50 | 25–45 µm (SLM), 45–105 µm (EBM) |
| **Size Distribution** | Spread D10–D90 | Narrow Gaussian preferred |
| **Flowability** | Rate at which powder flows | Hall Flow < 30 s/50 g |
| **Apparent Density** | Loose packing density | > 50% of theoretical |
| **Tap Density** | Density after tapping | > 60% of theoretical |
| **Morphology** | Shape of particles | **Spherical** (gas-atomized) |
| **Chemical Purity** | O, N contamination | O < 100 ppm, N < 50 ppm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why spherical particles?** Spherical particles provide (a) uniform powder flow, (b) high packing density, (c) consistent energy absorption, and (d) minimal satellite content.

## 2.3 Powder Production Methods

### 2.3.1 Gas Atomization (GA) — Most Common
Molten metal stream is broken into droplets by high-pressure **inert gas (Ar/N₂)** jets. Produces **spherical** particles with high purity.

### 2.3.2 Plasma Atomization (PA)
A metal wire is fed into a plasma torch; droplets form and solidify as **highly spherical** particles. Used for reactive metals like Ti, Nb.

### 2.3.3 Plasma Rotating Electrode Process (PREP)
A metal bar (electrode) rotates at high speed; an electric arc melts its tip; centrifugal force ejects **perfectly spherical** droplets. Used for high-purity Ti and aerospace alloys.

### 2.3.4 Water Atomization (WA)
Uses water jets — cheaper but produces **irregular, non-spherical** particles, unsuitable for AM. Used for press-and-sinter PM.

## 2.4 Sheet-Based Systems — Detailed Logic

### 2.4.1 Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing (UAM)
**Steps:**
1. Sheet foil (typically **Al 3003, Al 6061, Cu, Ti**) is fed over a rotating **sonotrode**.
2. The sonotrode vibrates at **20 kHz** with amplitude **10–50 µm** under normal force (1–3 kN).
3. Ultrasonic vibrations + pressure break oxide layers and cause **metallic bonding via diffusion** at the interface.
4. Layer thickness of new sheet bonded = **foils of 100–150 µm**.
5. After each layer, a **CNC milling cutter** removes excess material to define the layer geometry.

### 2.4.2 Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM)
A sheet (often paper, polymer, or metal-coated paper) is bonded to the previous layer using **adhesive + heated roller**. A laser cuts the cross-section. Less common for pure metals.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Apparent Density** | $\rho_{app} = \dfrac{m_{powder}}{V_{container}}$ | Powder quality check |
| **Tap Density** | $\rho_{tap} = \dfrac{m_{powder}}{V_{tapped}}$ | After standardized tapping |
| **Carr Index (Compressibility)** | $CI = \dfrac{\rho_{tap} - \rho_{app}}{\rho_{tap}} \times 100$ | Flowability indicator (CI < 15 = good flow) |
| **Hausner Ratio** | $HR = \dfrac{\rho_{tap}}{\rho_{app}}$ | HR < 1.25 = good flowability |
| **Hatch Spacing Relation** | $H = k \cdot D_{spot}$ | Hatching strategy in SLM (k ≈ 0.6–0.8) |
| **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | P=power(W), v=scan speed(mm/s), h=hatch spacing(mm), t=layer thickness(mm). Units: **J/mm³** |
| **Specific Energy Consumption** | $E_s = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot w \cdot \rho}$ | For DED processes |
| **Powder Packing Factor** | $\phi = \dfrac{\rho_{app}}{\rho_{true}}$ | Ideally 0.55–0.65 for AM powders |
| **Hall Flow Rate** | $t_{flow}$ for 50 g of powder | < 30 s/50 g (ASTM B213) |
| **Number of layers (Sheet AM)** | $n = \dfrac{H_{part}}{t_{sheet}}$ | H_part = part height, t_sheet = sheet thickness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** is the single most important process parameter in PBF. If $E_v$ is too low → lack of fusion porosity. If too high → keyhole porosity or vaporization. Typical sweet spot for Ti-6Al-4V SLM: **$E_v \approx 60–120$ J/mm³**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace** uses SLM-printed Ti-6Al-4V brackets and Inconel 718 turbine parts.
- **Medical implants** (orthopedic) use EBM Ti-6Al-4V with controlled porosity for bone ingrowth.
- **UAM** is used to embed **fiber-optic sensors, cooling channels, and dissimilar metal joints** (e.g., Al-Cu hybrid parts) for the defense and aerospace industries.
- **Automotive** uses binder jetting (a powder-AM derivative) for prototype sand moulds and large series production of structural parts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Energy Density (VED) for SLM

The Volumetric Energy Density (VED) is the energy delivered per unit volume of powder bed. We derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Energy delivered by the laser in time t:**
$$ E = P \cdot t $$
where $P$ = laser power (W) and $t$ = time (s).

**Step 2 — Time spent by the laser scanning length L:**
$$ t = \dfrac{L}{v} $$
where $v$ = scan speed (mm/s) and $L$ = scan length (mm).

**Step 3 — Volume of material melted per scan:**
For a single laser scan, the melt track is a rectangle:
- width = hatch spacing $h$ (mm)
- depth = layer thickness $t_l$ (mm)

So the cross-sectional area is $A = h \cdot t_l$ (mm²), and the melted volume per unit length is $A \cdot 1 = h \cdot t_l$ (mm³ per mm). For a full scan length $L$:
$$ V_{melt} = L \cdot h \cdot t_l $$

**Step 4 — Volumetric Energy Density (J per mm³):**
$$ E_v = \dfrac{E}{V_{melt}} = \dfrac{P \cdot t}{L \cdot h \cdot t_l} $$

Substituting $t = L/v$:
$$ E_v = \dfrac{P \cdot (L/v)}{L \cdot h \cdot t_l} = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t_l} $$

$$\boxed{E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t_l}}$$

**Units check:** $\dfrac{W}{(mm/s)(mm)(mm)} = \dfrac{J/s}{mm^3/s} = \dfrac{J}{mm^3}$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading:** $E_v$ is in **J/mm³**. A higher $E_v$ means more energy per unit volume → more melting → denser part (up to a point).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — VED Calculation

**Problem:** In an SLM process, the laser power is **400 W**, scan speed is **1200 mm/s**, hatch spacing is **0.1 mm**, and layer thickness is **0.04 mm**. Calculate the Volumetric Energy Density and comment on whether it falls in the optimal Ti-6Al-4V range (60–120 J/mm³).

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
$$ P = 400\,\text{W}, \quad v = 1200\,\text{mm/s}, \quad h = 0.1\,\text{mm}, \quad t_l = 0.04\,\text{mm} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the VED formula:**
$$ E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t_l} = \dfrac{400}{1200 \times 0.1 \times 0.04} $$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator:**
$$ 1200 \times 0.1 = 120 $$
$$ 120 \times 0.04 = 4.8 $$

**Step 4 — Final result:**
$$ E_v = \dfrac{400}{4.8} = 83.33\,\text{J/mm}^3 $$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
$$ 60 \leq 83.33 \leq 120 \Rightarrow \text{Within optimal Ti-6Al-4V SLM range} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The selected process parameters will likely produce a **dense (>99.5%) Ti-6Al-4V part** with minimal lack-of-fusion or keyhole defects.

## 3.3 Derivation: Number of Layers in Sheet-Based AM

In UAM/LOM systems, the build is fabricated by stacking sheets of known thickness.

**Given:**
- Part height: $H$ (mm)
- Sheet thickness: $t_s$ (mm)

The number of layers required is simply the part height divided by the sheet thickness:
$$ n = \dfrac{H}{t_s} $$

**Worked Example:** Fabricate a **25 mm tall** Al 3003 part using UAM with **0.1 mm** Al foils.
$$ n = \dfrac{25}{0.1} = 250\,\text{layers} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Sheet-based AM requires **5–10× more layers** than powder-based AM for the same part height, which means longer build times but lower post-processing needs.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Powder vs Sheet Systems

| Parameter | Powder-Based (SLM) | Powder-Based (EBM) | Sheet-Based (UAM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Feedstock size** | 15–150 µm | 45–105 µm | 50–500 µm foils |
| **Heat source** | Fiber laser (1070 nm) | Electron beam | Ultrasonic (20 kHz) |
| **Atmosphere** | Inert gas (Ar) | Vacuum + He | Ambient air |
| **Bed temperature** | 80–200°C | 600–750°C | Room temperature |
| **Typical layer** | 20–50 µm | 50–100 µm | 100–500 µm |
| **Build rate** | Medium | High | Low |
| **Surface finish** | Ra 5–20 µm | Ra 20–40 µm | Ra 1–10 µm (after milling) |
| **Residual stress** | High | Low | Very low |
| **Multi-material** | Limited | Limited | **Excellent** (dissimilar metals) |
| **Embedded sensors** | Difficult | Difficult | **Easy** |
| **Vacuum required?** | No | **Yes** | No |
| **Material range** | Steels, Ti, Al, Ni | Ti, refractory metals | Al, Cu, Ti, SS |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Powder Bed Fusion (SLM) System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Model STL File] --> B[Slicing Software Layer Generation]
    B --> C[Build Job Controller]
    C --> D[Recoater Blade Powder Spreader]
    D --> E[Powder Feed Bed Source]
    D --> F[Build Platform Z Axis]
    F --> G[Fiber Laser 1070 nm 200W to 1kW]
    G --> H[Galvanometer Scanner XY Deflection]
    H --> I[Scan Cross Section and Melt Powder]
    I --> J{Fusion Complete}
    J -- No --> C
    J -- Yes --> K[Lower Build Platform by Layer Thickness]
    K --> D
    K --> L[All Layers Done]
    L --> M[Unfused Powder Removal Sieving]
    L --> N[Heat Treatment Stress Relief]
    N --> O[Final Part Machining Inspection]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Reading:** The block diagram above maps the **functional data and material flow** of an SLM system. The **recoater block** retrieves powder from the feed bed, the **laser + galvanometer block** delivers energy to selective regions, and the **Z-axis block** progressively lowers the part.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Electron Beam Melting (EBM) System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CAD STL Slicer] --> B[EB Control Unit]
    B --> C[Vacuum Chamber 10 to the minus 4 mbar]
    C --> D[He Backfill to 10 to the minus 2 mbar]
    B --> E[Tungsten Filament Electron Gun]
    E --> F[Electromagnetic Lenses Focus Beam]
    F --> G[Deflection Coils XY Scan]
    G --> H[Powder Bed Ti 6Al 4V Bed Temp 700C]
    H --> I[Melt Pool Formation and Solidification]
    I --> J{All Layers Complete}
    J -- No --> K[Rake Powder Spreader]
    K --> H
    J -- Yes --> L[Cool Down Powder Recovery]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing (UAM) System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD File] --> B[CNC G Code Generation]
    B --> C[Build Platform]
    C --> D[Sheet Foil Feed Roller]
    D --> E[Sonotrode Ultrasonic 20 kHz Amplitude 10 to 50 um]
    E --> F[Normal Force 1 to 3 kN]
    F --> G[Foil Foil Metallic Bonding Diffusion]
    G --> H{All Sheets Bonded}
    H -- No --> D
    H -- Yes --> I[Layer Geometry Defined]
    I --> J[CNC Milling Cutter Subtractive Layer Finish]
    J --> K{All Layers Done}
    K -- No --> D
    K -- Yes --> L[Final Part Dissimilar Joints or Embedded Sensors]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why three diagrams?** KTU often asks to "draw and explain" the schematic. The block-level functional architecture is the most **examination-safe** representation, since exact machine drawings are protected IP. These three diagrams cover the full syllabus scope of Powder and Sheet Systems.

## 4.4 Process Comparison Flow — Powder vs Sheet

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AM Feedstock Type] --> B[Powder]
    A --> C[Sheet]
    B --> B1[Powder Bed Fusion SLM or DMLS]
    B --> B2[Electron Beam Melting EBM]
    B --> B3[Binder Jetting BJT]
    B --> B4[Directed Energy Deposition DED]
    C --> C1[Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing UAM]
    C --> C2[Laminated Object Manufacturing LOM]
    C --> C3[Adhesive Bonding and Laser Cut LOM variant]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) and list any four key process parameters.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Question)]**

**Model Answer:**
Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) is a metal additive manufacturing process in which a thin layer of metallic powder is spread across a build platform and selectively fused using a high-energy heat source (laser or electron beam) according to the cross-section generated from a sliced 3D CAD model.

The four key process parameters are:
1. **Laser / Electron beam power (P)**
2. **Scan speed (v)**
3. **Hatch spacing (h)**
4. **Layer thickness (t_l)**
5. *(Bonus) Atmosphere composition and build plate temperature.*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not write only "PBF uses a powder and a laser." You must **list the parameters explicitly** because a 3-mark question requires depth. Missing the parameters will cost 1 mark.

---

### Q2. What is Volumetric Energy Density (VED)? State its formula and SI units.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Question)]**

**Model Answer:**
Volumetric Energy Density (VED) is defined as the amount of energy delivered by the heat source per unit volume of the powder bed during a single scan. It is the most important process parameter governing part density and defect formation in PBF.

$$ E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t_l} $$

where:
- $P$ = laser power (W)
- $v$ = scan speed (mm/s)
- $h$ = hatch spacing (mm)
- $t_l$ = layer thickness (mm)

**SI Units:** J/mm³ (or equivalently MJ/m³)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** and provide an **internal choice**. Each choice is typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Below are two complete alternative questions for the same module.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the working principle of **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)**. Discuss the role of the recoater, galvanometer scanner, and inert gas atmosphere.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Selective Laser Melting (SLM), also called Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS) in commercial terminology, is a Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) process that uses a high-power fiber laser (typically 200 W–1 kW, wavelength 1070 nm) to fully melt metallic powders in an inert atmosphere.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
```
CAD model → Slicer → Build Controller → Recoater → Powder Bed
                                                     ↓
       Recoater ← ← ← Lower Platform ← ← Laser + Galvanometer (selective melting)
                                                     ↓
                                             Melt Pool → Solidified Layer
```

**Working Steps (3 marks):**
1. The CAD model is sliced into 2D layers (typically 20–50 µm thick).
2. A **recoater blade** spreads a thin, uniform layer of powder (D50 ≈ 30–45 µm) from the feed bed across the build platform.
3. A **fiber laser beam**, deflected by a **galvanometer scanner** (rotating mirrors at 10–50 m/s), selectively scans the 2D cross-section.
4. Powder particles **fully melt** at temperatures above the liquidus, forming a continuous melt pool that solidifies to a dense track.
5. After one layer is complete, the build platform **lowers by one layer thickness**, the recoater spreads the next layer, and the process repeats.
6. The build chamber is continuously purged with **high-purity Argon (O₂ < 100 ppm)** to prevent oxidation of reactive metals.

**Role of Key Components (1 mark):**
- **Recoater:** Ensures uniform powder layer thickness; critical for surface finish and inter-layer bonding.
- **Galvanometer scanner:** Provides high-speed, accurate laser positioning; scan strategy (zigzag, chessboard, contour) affects residual stress.
- **Inert gas (Ar/N₂):** Prevents oxidation, removes spatter and condensate ("smoke") from the laser path.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A SLM machine is used to fabricate Ti-6Al-4V parts. Given: Laser Power P = 285 W, Scan Speed v = 950 mm/s, Hatch Spacing h = 0.12 mm, Layer Thickness t_l = 0.03 mm. Calculate (i) Volumetric Energy Density, and (ii) comment on the defect mode if P is increased to 500 W while keeping all other parameters the same.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$$ P_1 = 285\,\text{W}, \quad v = 950\,\text{mm/s}, \quad h = 0.12\,\text{mm}, \quad t_l = 0.03\,\text{mm} $$

**(i) VED Calculation (3 marks):**
$$ E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t_l} = \dfrac{285}{950 \times 0.12 \times 0.03} $$

Step 1 — Denominator:
$$ 950 \times 0.12 = 114 $$
$$ 114 \times 0.03 = 3.42 $$

Step 2 — VED:
$$ E_v = \dfrac{285}{3.42} = 83.33\,\text{J/mm}^3 $$

Step 3 — Interpretation: $E_v = 83.33$ J/mm³ lies within the **optimal Ti-6Al-4V SLM range (60–120 J/mm³)**. Expected relative density > 99.5%.

**(ii) Defect Analysis (4 marks):**

New power $P_2 = 500$ W. New VED:
$$ E_{v,new} = \dfrac{500}{3.42} = 146.2\,\text{J/mm}^3 $$

Since $146.2 > 120$ J/mm³, the energy density is **excessively high**, leading to:

- **Keyhole porosity** — excessive energy vaporizes the metal; recoil pressure creates a deep vapor cavity that collapses and traps gas bubbles.
- **Spattering** — violent melt-pool ejection causes balling and surface defects.
- **Element loss** — preferential vaporization of Al in Ti-6Al-4V (Al vapor pressure is high) → change in alloy chemistry, embrittlement.
- **Residual stress and distortion** — very high thermal gradients.

**Conclusion:** Reducing scan speed or increasing hatch spacing would be better alternatives to lower the VED back to the optimum range.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often skip the **"comment"** part in (ii) and only compute numbers. This costs 3–4 marks. Always tie the numerical result to the **physical defect mechanism** (lack of fusion vs keyhole) for full credit.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of **Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing (UAM)**. List its advantages and limitations compared to powder-based systems.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** UAM is a **sheet-based, solid-state AM process** developed and commercialized by **Fabrisonic** (USA) in the 1990s. It bonds thin metal foils using **ultrasonic vibrations** at temperatures well below the melting point, combined with periodic CNC milling for geometric definition.

**Working Principle with Block Diagram (3 marks):**
```
Foil spool → Sonotrode (20 kHz, 10–50 µm amplitude, 1–3 kN normal force)
            ↓
       Foil-Foil metallic bond (oxide disruption + diffusion)
            ↓
       Layer-by-layer build (100–150 µm per layer)
            ↓
       CNC milling cutter (defines layer geometry, removes excess)
```

**Detailed Steps (1 mark):**
1. A metal foil (Al, Cu, Ti, SS) of 0.1–0.15 mm thickness is fed over the build area.
2. The **sonotrode** (vibrating horn) presses the new foil against the previous layer with **1–3 kN** normal force.
3. The sonotrode vibrates at **20 kHz** with amplitude **10–50 µm**.
4. Ultrasonic vibrations break surface oxide layers; clean metal-to-metal contact enables **solid-state diffusion bonding** at the interface.
5. After each bond, a **CNC milling cutter** removes excess material to define the layer's 2D shape.
6. Process repeats until part is complete.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- No melting → **no residual stress**, no solidification cracks.
- Can join **dissimilar metals** (e.g., Al–Cu, Al–Ti) without brittle intermetallics.
- Allows **embedded sensors, optical fibres, and cooling channels**.
- Low-temperature operation → no phase transformations, retains base-metal properties.
- Excellent surface finish (Ra < 10 µm after milling).

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Low build rate (only 100–150 µm per layer vs 20–50 µm at much larger area for SLM).
- Limited to **soft, ductile metals** (Al, Cu); not suitable for high-strength steels or refractory metals.
- Maximum part size is constrained by the sonotrode footprint and CNC envelope.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** and **Electron Beam Melting (EBM)** in terms of (i) heat source and atmosphere, (ii) bed temperature, (iii) build speed and surface finish, and (iv) typical materials.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Question)]**

**Model Solution:**

| Comparison Parameter | SLM | EBM |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Heat source & atmosphere** | Fiber laser (1070 nm, 200 W–1 kW); inert gas (Ar or N₂) at slight overpressure | Electron beam (1–15 kW, accelerating voltage 60 kV); vacuum (10⁻⁴ mbar) backfilled with He to ~10⁻² mbar |
| **(ii) Bed temperature** | 80–200 °C (preheat to reduce distortion) | 600–750 °C (high preheat prevents powder smoke and supports) |
| **(iii) Build speed & surface finish** | Lower scan speed; finer surface finish (Ra 5–20 µm); finer features possible | Higher scan speed (up to 10⁶ mm/s); coarser surface (Ra 20–40 µm); thicker layers (50–100 µm) |
| **(iv) Typical materials** | Stainless steel 316L, AlSi10Mg, Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel 718, maraging steel | Ti-6Al-4V (dominant), TiAl, CoCr, pure Ti; reactive and refractory metals preferred |

**Additional Discussion (2 marks):**
- **Cost:** SLM machines are cheaper; EBM requires high-vacuum systems.
- **Residual stress:** SLM parts have high residual stress → require stress-relief heat treatment. EBM parts have **low residual stress** due to high bed temperature (in-situ annealing).
- **Powder:** SLM uses finer powder (15–45 µm); EBM uses coarser powder (45–105 µm).
- **Energy efficiency:** EBM is more energy-efficient (no gas consumption); SLM consumes large volumes of Ar.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write SLM and EBM are "the same thing" with a different heat source. This is **incorrect**. The atmosphere, bed temperature, scan speed, and resulting microstructure differ fundamentally. Always prepare a **comparison table** — it scores full marks in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Powder and Sheet Systems**

- **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** = spreading powder + selective energy source (laser/e-beam) + layer-by-layer melting.
- **SLM (DMLS)** = laser + Ar atmosphere + fine powder (15–45 µm) + lower build rate + finer surface.
- **EBM** = electron beam + vacuum/He + coarse powder (45–105 µm) + high bed temp (700°C) + high build rate + reactive metals.
- **Volumetric Energy Density (VED):** $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t_l}$ — too low = lack of fusion, too high = keyhole.
- **Ti-6Al-4V SLM sweet spot:** 60–120 J/mm³.
- **7 Powder Pillars:** Size, distribution, flowability, apparent density, tap density, **spherical morphology**, purity (O, N < 100 ppm).
- **Carr Index (CI) < 15** and **Hausner Ratio (HR) < 1.25** indicate good flowability.
- **Spherical powder** is essential for AM → produced by **gas atomization, plasma atomization, or PREP** (NOT water atomization).
- **UAM (Sheet AM)** = 20 kHz ultrasonic + 1–3 kN normal force + low temperature + CNC milling per layer.
- **UAM key strengths:** dissimilar metals, embedded sensors, low residual stress, fine surface finish.
- **UAM key weakness:** limited to soft metals (Al, Cu) and slow build rate.
- **Layer thickness comparison:** SLM (20–50 µm) < EBM (50–100 µm) < UAM (100–500 µm).
- **Number of layers (sheet AM):** $n = \dfrac{H_{part}}{t_{sheet}}$.
- **Powder vs Sheet geometry:** Powder defines part by **fusing selectively**; Sheet defines part by **cutting away excess**.
- **EBM requires vacuum** because electrons scatter in gas → beam defocuses and loses energy.
- **Atmosphere functions in SLM:** prevents oxidation + removes spatter/smoke from optical path.
- **Powder reuse:** Unfused powder from SLM/EBM can be sieved and reused, but oxygen content must be monitored (limit to 2–5 reuse cycles for reactive alloys).

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Topic Notes — Powder and Sheet Systems (PEMET867 / Module 2)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metal AM as an Extension of Welding Techniques (GTAW/GMAW)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET867 - METAL ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Metal AM as an Extension of Welding Techniques (GTAW/GMAW)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM)** is a Directed Energy Deposition (DED) variant of metal additive manufacturing in which a conventional electric arc — generated between a continuously fed metallic wire electrode and the substrate — is used as the heat source to melt and deposit material layer-by-layer, building near-net-shape metallic components directly from a 3D CAD model.

When the arc is produced using a **non-consumable tungsten electrode** shielded by an inert gas, the process is classified as **GTAW-based WAAM** (Gas Tungsten Arc Welding, also called TIG-WAAM or Plasma-WAAM). When a **continuously fed consumable wire electrode** acts as both the electrode and the filler material under an external shielding gas, it is called **GMAW-based WAAM** (Gas Metal Arc Welding, also called MIG-WAAM).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** WAAM is treated as a *direct technological descendant* of arc welding because the same power source, torch kinematics, and metallurgical principles are reused. The only conceptual addition is **closed-loop path planning** on top of a 3D sliced geometry.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The 3D Pen Welder"

Imagine a **chalk artist** drawing a white figure on a blackboard. Each chalk stroke leaves a thin, solid line. Now imagine the artist does *not* lift the chalk after one stroke but continues drawing **layer upon layer**, going over the same region again and again until the chalk height becomes a 3D shape. 

In WAAM:
- The **chalk** = the filler wire (consumable electrode).
- The **hand** = the welding torch mounted on a CNC gantry or robotic arm.
- The **board** = the metallic substrate.
- The **force pushing chalk out** = the arc energy that melts the wire instantly.
- The **artist's path** = the G-code / NC toolpath sliced from the 3D CAD model.

Each pass of the torch adds one *bead*, and stacked beads become a *wall*, *rib*, or *blade* — exactly the kind of large structural parts (1–10 m scale) the aerospace, marine, and defence industries need.

### 1.3 Key Metrics You Must Memorise

> [!NOTE]
> - **Deposition Rate (DR)** for WAAM: **typically 50–500 g/min** — about **10× to 100× faster** than powder-bed SLM.
> - **Deposition Efficiency (η):** **GTAW ≈ 0.7 (70%)** , **GMAW ≈ 0.6–0.9 (60–90%)** depending on transfer mode.
> - **Typical Layer Height (h):** **0.8 mm – 3.0 mm** (much thicker than laser SLM's 0.02–0.05 mm).
> - **Wall Width (w):** **3 mm – 12 mm** depending on current and travel speed.
> - **Heat Input (HI):** measured in **kJ/mm**; standard welding limits for steels stay between **0.5 – 1.5 kJ/mm**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Welding Can Be "Extended" into AM

Conventional welding is a *joining* process — two parts become one. Additive manufacturing requires *building* — nothing becomes something. The transition happens through three engineering shifts:

1. **Path Planning replaces Manual Operator Skill:** A welding operator normally relies on visual cues. In WAAM, a robotic/CNC system executes a pre-computed trajectory generated by **slicing software** (e.g., cross-sectional slicing of an STL file).
2. **Layered Deposition replaces Single-Pass Joint:** Successive beads are stacked with controlled **inter-layer cooling / dwell time (t_d)** to maintain dimensional accuracy and limit the heat-affected zone (HAZ).
3. **Closed-Loop Process Monitoring replaces Manual Inspection:** Arc voltage, current, and infrared thermal imaging feed back into the controller, mimicking the "feel" an experienced welder has but with sub-millisecond response.

### 2.2 The Two Parent Processes in Detail

#### 2.2.1 GTAW-Based WAAM (TIG-WAAM)

- **Electrode:** Non-consumable **tungsten** (W + 2% ThO₂ or W + 2% La₂O₃).
- **Filler:** Separate wire fed **cold** into the front of the molten pool from a side nozzle.
- **Arc type:** Constant-current DCEN (Direct Current Electrode Negative).
- **Transfer:** **Dip transfer** or **pulsed arc** — high precision, very clean beads.
- **Typical current:** 100–250 A; **Voltage:** 10–18 V.
- **Strengths:** Excellent for **titanium (Ti-6Al-4V)**, **nickel superalloys (Inconel 718)**, aluminium; low spatter; high metallurgical quality.
- **Weakness:** Slower deposition rate than GMAW.

#### 2.2.2 GMAW-Based WAAM (MIG-WAAM)

- **Electrode:** **Consumable** wire (Ø 0.8–1.6 mm) acting as both filler and arc carrier.
- **Shielding gas:** Ar, Ar/CO₂, Ar/O₂ mixtures depending on transfer mode.
- **Arc type:** Constant-voltage DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive).
- **Transfer modes relevant to WAAM:**
  - **Short-circuit transfer** — low heat, thin walls, low deposition.
  - **Dip / Globular transfer** — medium deposition, periodic spatter.
  - **Spray / Pulsed-spray transfer** — high deposition, smoothest beads (preferred for WAAM).
- **Typical current:** 150–400 A; **Voltage:** 18–32 V.
- **Strengths:** Very high deposition rate (up to **5 kg/h**), robust, easy to automate with off-the-shelf MIG sources.
- **Weakness:** More spatter; coarser surface; needs post-machining.

### 2.3 Process Parameters Governing WAAM Quality

| Parameter | Symbol | GTAW-WAAM Typical | GMAW-WAAM Typical | Effect on Build |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Welding current | $I$ | 100–250 A | 150–400 A | ↑ I → wider, deeper beads |
| Arc voltage | $V$ | 10–18 V | 18–32 V | ↑ V → wider, flatter bead |
| Travel speed | $v$ | 3–10 mm/s | 5–15 mm/s | ↑ v → thinner, taller bead |
| Wire feed rate | $W_f$ | 0.5–2 m/min | 2–12 m/min | ↑ $W_f$ → ↑ deposition |
| Dwell time | $t_d$ | 30–180 s | 30–180 s | ↓ residual heat, ↑ accuracy |
| Shielding gas flow | $Q$ | 8–15 L/min | 12–25 L/min | Protects from oxidation |
| Contact-tip-to-work distance | $CTWD$ | 3–5 mm | 10–20 mm | Affects arc stability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** Aerospace landing-gear brackets, ship propellers, missile fins, and titanium structural space-frame nodes are now being prototyped in WAAM because deposition rates are high enough to be *economically viable* for **large** parts (> 0.5 m³) where laser powder-bed systems would take hundreds of hours.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these five equations — every numerical WAAM problem in KTU exams reduces to one or more of them.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat Input | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | $\eta$ = thermal efficiency factor |
| 2 | Deposition Rate (mass) | $\dot{m} = \rho_w \cdot A_w \cdot v_f$ | kg/s | $\rho_w$ = wire density |
| 3 | Wire Cross-section Area | $A_w = \dfrac{\pi d_w^{\,2}}{4}$ | m² | $d_w$ = wire diameter |
| 4 | Bead Volume per Unit Length | $V_b = h \cdot w \cdot L$ | mm³ | h = layer height, w = bead width |
| 5 | Layer Height from Mass Balance | $h = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot \Delta t}{\rho_d \cdot w \cdot L}$ | mm | $\rho_d$ = deposit density |
| 6 | Linear Energy Density | $LED = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v}$ | J/mm | Often called "arc energy" |
| 7 | Power Input | $P = V \cdot I$ | W | DC arc power |

> [!WARNING]
> Do **not** confuse *Deposition Rate (kg/h)* with *Deposition Velocity (mm/s)*. The first is a **mass flow**, the second is a **translational speed of the torch**.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility Snapshot

- **Aerospace (Airbus, Boeing tier-1 suppliers):** Ti-6Al-4V structural brackets via TIG-WAAM.
- **Marine (Damenshipyard, Naval Group):** Aluminium and steel propellers up to Ø 1.5 m.
- **Defence (DRDO labs, BARC):** Missile motor casings, gun barrels in maraging steel.
- **Civil:** Reinforcement cages, decorative façade elements.
- **Energy:** Large-diameter pipe spool pieces for offshore platforms.

The same arc physics that governs a welded pipe joint is therefore used, but **without the pipe** — only the molten filler remains.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Input (HI) Equation

The **heat input** quantifies the energy delivered per unit length of weld, which directly controls the size of the HAZ, the cooling rate, and the resulting microstructure.

**Starting principle:** Electrical arc power delivered to the workpiece.

$$P = V \cdot I$$

where $V$ is the arc voltage (in volts, V) and $I$ is the welding current (in amperes, A). The unit of $P$ is watts (W), i.e., joules per second.

**Step 2 — Account for Thermal Efficiency ($\eta$):** Not all electrical power enters the workpiece. Some is lost as convection, radiation from the arc column, and spatter ejection. A dimensionless efficiency factor $\eta$ (< 1) is introduced.

$$P_{useful} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I$$

For arc welding processes typical textbook values are:
- **GTAW:** $\eta \approx 0.60 - 0.70$
- **GMAW:** $\eta \approx 0.70 - 0.85$
- **SAW:** $\eta \approx 0.90 - 0.99$

**Step 3 — Convert from Power to Energy per Unit Length:** If the torch travels at speed $v$ (in mm/s), the time to traverse 1 mm of weld is $\Delta t = 1/v$ seconds. Therefore energy per mm is:

$$HI = P_{useful} \cdot \Delta t = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{1}{v}$$

with the unit J/mm. To express in kJ/mm (more convenient for steel structures), divide by 1000:

$$\boxed{HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \; \text{[kJ/mm]}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** $1\ \text{kJ} = 1000\ \text{J}$, hence the divisor of 1000. If you keep $v$ in **m/min** instead of mm/s, then $HI$ comes out directly in kJ/mm because of unit cancellation.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass Deposition Rate from Wire Feed

The wire is fed at a linear speed $v_f$ (in m/min or mm/s). In one second, a length of wire equal to $v_f$ enters the molten pool.

**Step 1 — Volume of wire consumed per second:**

$$V_{wire/s} = A_w \cdot v_f = \dfrac{\pi d_w^{\,2}}{4} \cdot v_f$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by wire density $\rho_w$ to obtain mass:**

$$\dot{m} = \rho_w \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_w^{\,2}}{4} \cdot v_f$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kg/h (typical industrial metric):**

$$\dot{m}_{kg/h} = \dot{m}_{kg/s} \cdot 3600$$

> Worked numerical example (frequently asked in KTU):
> - Wire diameter $d_w = 1.2$ mm = 0.0012 m
> - Wire density $\rho_w = 7850$ kg/m³ (steel ER70S-6)
> - Feed rate $v_f = 4$ m/min = 4/60 m/s = 0.0667 m/s
> 
> Substituting:
> 
> $$A_w = \dfrac{\pi (0.0012)^2}{4} = 1.131 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2$$
> 
> $$\dot{m} = 7850 \cdot 1.131 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.0667 \approx 5.92 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{kg/s}$$
> 
> $$\dot{m}_{kg/h} \approx 5.92 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 3600 \approx 2.13\ \text{kg/h}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Layer Height from Mass Balance

Consider a single weld bead deposited over a length $L$ with width $w$ and height $h$. The volume is:

$$V_{bead} = h \cdot w \cdot L$$

The mass of that bead must equal the mass of wire consumed during the dwell time $\Delta t$ at deposition rate $\dot m$:

$$\rho_d \cdot h \cdot w \cdot L = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta t$$

Solving for layer height:

$$\boxed{h = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot \Delta t}{\rho_d \cdot w \cdot L}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** This equation is *the* fundamental design constraint in WAAM slicing software. If you specify a target layer height (say $h = 1.5$ mm), the software back-calculates the wire-feed rate, dwell time, or path length required.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Linear Energy Density and Cooling Rate

The **cooling rate** ($dT/dt$) of a weld is approximated by the **3-D Rosenthal equation**, simplified for thick plates:

$$\dfrac{dT}{dt} \approx \dfrac{2\pi k \cdot (T_c - T_0)^2}{HI}$$

where $k$ is thermal conductivity and $T_c - T_0$ is the difference between the cooling-cessation temperature and ambient. KTU problems usually stop at defining **linear energy density**:

$$LED = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v}$$

with units J/mm. Note that $LED = HI \cdot 1000 / \eta$ (without the efficiency and kJ unit).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — GTAW vs GMAW WAAM

| Decision Criterion | GTAW-WAAM | GMAW-WAAM |
|---|---|---|
| Material compatibility | Ti, Ni alloys, Al, stainless | Carbon steel, low-alloy steel, Al, Cu |
| Deposition rate | Low (0.5–2 kg/h) | High (2–5 kg/h) |
| Bead surface roughness | **Ra 0.4–1.0 µm** (as-deposited) | Ra 1.5–3.5 µm |
| Spatter | Negligible | Moderate (especially globular) |
| Equipment cost | Higher (HF start, gas lens) | Lower (commercial MIG source) |
| Wall thickness tolerance | ±0.2 mm | ±0.5 mm |
| Post-processing | Minimal | Significant machining |
| Operator skill needed (manual) | High | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 WAAM System Block Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CAD["CAD MODEL INPUT"] --> SLICER["SLICING SOFTWARE LAYER GENERATION"]
    SLICER --> PATH["NC TOOLPATH GENERATION"]
    PATH --> CONTROLLER["CNC OR ROBOT CONTROLLER"]
    CONTROLLER --> TORCH["WELDING TORCH MOUNTED ON GANTRY"]
    PS["WELDING POWER SOURCE GTAW OR GMAW"] --> TORCH
    WF["WIRE FEED UNIT"] --> TORCH
    GAS["SHIELDING GAS CYLINDER ARGON OR AR MIX"] --> TORCH
    TORCH --> POOL["MOLTEN WELD POOL ON SUBSTRATE"]
    POOL --> LAYER["DEPOSITED BEAD LAYER"]
    LAYER --> NEXT["SUBSTRATE OR PREVIOUS LAYER"]
    SENSE["ARC SENSORS VOLTAGE CURRENT IR CAMERA"] --> FEEDBACK["FEEDBACK SIGNAL"]
    FEEDBACK --> CONTROLLER
    NEXT --> SLICER
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Closed-Loop Layer Addition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["START NEW LAYER N"] --> S2["POSITION TORCH AT LAYER ORIGIN"]
    S2 --> S3["IGNITE ARC CONTACT OR HIGH FREQUENCY"]
    S3 --> S4["TRAVERSE PATH WHILE FEEDING WIRE"]
    S4 --> S5["MONITOR ARC STABILITY AND POOL TEMPERATURE"]
    S5 --> S6{"LAYER COMPLETE"}
    S6 -- NO --> S4
    S6 -- YES --> S7["RAISE TORCH BY DELTA H LAYER HEIGHT"]
    S7 --> S8["DWELL TIME FOR INTER LAYER COOLING"]
    S8 --> S9{"FINAL LAYER REACHED"}
    S9 -- NO --> S1
    S9 -- YES --> S10["PART COMPLETE MOVE TO POST PROCESSING"]
```

### 4.3 Heat Flow During WAAM Deposition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ARC["ELECTRIC ARC HEAT SOURCE"] --> MELT["MELT WIRE AND SUBSTRATE SURFACE"]
    MELT --> POOL["MOLTEN POOL"]
    POOL --> CONV["CONVECTIVE LOSS TO SHIELDING GAS"]
    POOL --> RAD["RADIATIVE LOSS TO ENVIRONMENT"]
    POOL --> COND["CONDUCTIVE LOSS INTO SUBSTRATE AND PREVIOUS LAYER"]
    COND --> SUB["SUBSTRATE BASE PLATE"]
    COND --> PREV["PREVIOUSLY DEPOSITED LAYERS HAZ"]
    POOL --> SOLID["SOLIDIFYING BEAD AS TORCH ADVANCES"]
```

### 4.4 Cross-Section Comparison — Single Bead vs Multi-Layer Wall

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CROSS["CROSS SECTION VIEW OF A WAAM WALL"]
        L1["LAYER 1 BOTTOM"] --> L2["LAYER 2"] --> L3["LAYER 3"] --> L4["LAYER 4 TOP"]
        L1 --- W1["WALL WIDTH W"]
        L4 --- H1["TOTAL HEIGHT H N TIMES LAYER HEIGHT"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The cross-section above is a *schematic side view*. In an actual WAAM wall you would observe a **layer-band microstructure**, periodic **inter-layer dark bands** (where dwell cooling causes partial re-austenitisation), and anisotropic mechanical properties — a frequently tested KTU concept.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM). How is it classified as an extension of conventional welding?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
