# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q: Define mechanical anisotropy in additively manufactured metal parts. Why is it a critical design consideration?**

**Model Answer:**
Mechanical anisotropy refers to the directional dependence of mechanical properties (such as tensile strength, ductility, and fatigue life) on the orientation of the load relative to the build direction. In AM parts, anisotropy arises primarily from (i) columnar grain growth aligned with the build direction due to directional heat extraction, (ii) weak interlayer bonding zones (prior-β grain boundaries and lack-of-fusion planes), and (iii) the staircase surface effect on vertically oriented faces.

**Why critical:** It is a critical design consideration because AM components in aerospace, biomedical, and automotive applications must carry service loads along predictable directions. If a part is built with its weakest direction aligned with the maximum principal stress, premature failure may occur even when bulk properties appear acceptable. Designers must explicitly account for anisotropy in safety factors and qualification testing.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q: What is Volumetric Energy Density (VED), and how does deviation from the optimal VED range affect the mechanical performance of LPBF parts?**

**Model Answer:**
Volumetric Energy Density is defined as the laser energy delivered per unit volume of deposited material:
$$\text{VED} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$
where $P$ is laser power, $v$ is scan speed, $h$ is hatch spacing, and $t$ is layer thickness. The optimal VED for a given material (e.g., ~40–120 J/mm³ for Ti-6Al-4V in LPBF) produces a stable melt pool with minimal defects.

**Effect of deviation:**
- **VED too low** → incomplete melting → lack-of-fusion (LoF) porosity → reduced fatigue life, lower ductility, and erratic tensile strength
- **VED too high** → excessive vaporization → keyhole porosity → similar mechanical degradation, plus surface roughness from spatter
- **VED optimal** → low porosity, fine microstructure, balanced strength and ductility, and reduced anisotropy

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the Hall-Petch strengthening mechanism in additively manufactured Ti-6Al-4V parts. How does the cellular substructure formed during LPBF contribute to the yield strength, and how is this affected by post-process heat treatment? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the origin and measurement techniques of residual stresses in LPBF parts. How do these residual stresses influence the mechanical performance, and what strategies are used to mitigate them? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Hall-Petch mechanism (2 marks):**
The Hall-Petch relation $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ states that yield strength increases as the effective microstructural barrier size ($d$) decreases. The term $k_y d^{-1/2}$ represents the additional stress required to propagate plastic deformation across grain/cell boundaries.

**Step 2 — Cellular substructure in LPBF Ti-6Al-4V (3 marks):**
During LPBF, the extremely high cooling rate ($10^5$–$10^6$ K/s) produces a fine **cellular substructure** within prior-β grains. The cell diameter is typically **0.3–1.0 μm**, and the cell walls are decorated with dislocations and segregated solutes. These cells act as **dislocation barriers**, effectively reducing the mean free path of dislocations.

**Numerical justification (1 mark):** With $d = 0.5$ μm, $k_y = 0.5$ MPa·m^(1/2):
$$
\sigma_y = 350 + 0.5 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2} \approx 1057 \text{ MPa}
$$
This matches experimental as-built values (~1000–1100 MPa), confirming the dominant role of cell-boundary strengthening.

**Step 3 — Effect of heat treatment (1 mark):**
Heat treatment above the β-transus (~995°C for Ti-6Al-4V) coarsens cells to ~5–10 μm. The Hall-Petch contribution drops to ~160–230 MPa, reducing $\sigma_y$ to ~510–580 MPa but improving ductility from ~3% to ~10–14%.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Origin of residual stress (2 marks):**
During LPBF, the topmost deposited layer is heated to near-melting temperature, while the underlying solidified material acts as a cooler constraint. Upon rapid solidification and cooling, the top layer contracts but is restrained by the cooler substrate, generating **tensile residual stress on the top surface** and **compressive stress in the substrate region**. The magnitude scales as $\sigma \propto E \alpha \Delta T$.

**Step 2 — Measurement techniques (2 marks):**
- **X-ray diffraction (XRD, sin²ψ method):** Surface residual stress, non-destructive
- **Hole-drilling (ASTM E837):** Subsurface (~1–2 mm), semi-destructive
- **Contour method:** Full cross-section stress map, destructive
- **Neutron diffraction:** Full bulk, non-destructive (lab/synchrotron)

**Step 3 — Influence on mechanical performance (1 mark):**
- Tensile surface residual stress reduces fatigue life (adds to applied cyclic stress)
- Distortion causes geometric deviation from design tolerances
- Stress corrosion cracking susceptibility may increase
- Part may fracture spontaneously during machining if residual stress is released abruptly

**Step 4 — Mitigation strategies (2 marks):**
- **Pre-heating** the substrate (200–400°C for Ti) → reduces ΔT
- **In-situ stress relief** by reheating the previously deposited layer (e.g., second laser pass)
- **Optimized scan strategies:** island scanning, rotation between layers, chessboard patterns
- **Post-process stress relief heat treatment** (600–700°C for Ti, below phase transformation)
- **Design:** Use support structures and appropriate build orientation

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students fail to distinguish between **stress relief heat treatment** (low temperature, only reduces residual stress) and **phase transformation heat treatment** (above β-transus, also changes microstructure and mechanical properties). KTU expects clear differentiation.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q: (a)** With suitable equations, describe the Murakami √area model for predicting the fatigue limit of AM metals containing internal defects. Apply it to calculate the fatigue limit of an LPBF 316L stainless steel part with a 100 μm √area internal defect and HV = 210. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the mechanical properties of as-built, heat-treated, and HIPed AM Ti-6Al-4V parts in terms of tensile strength, ductility, fatigue life, and fracture toughness. Explain the underlying microstructural reasons for the observed trends. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Murakami √area model statement (2 marks):**
The Murakami model relates the fatigue limit $\sigma_w$ to the defect size (projected onto the plane perpendicular to maximum tensile stress) and the material hardness:
$$
\sigma_w = \frac{C \cdot (\text{HV} + 120)}{(\sqrt{\text{area}})^{1/6}}
$$
where $C = 1.43$ for internal defects in steels, $\text{HV}$ is Vickers hardness, and √area is in μm. The model captures the **stress concentration effect** of small defects and the **intrinsic material resistance** to crack initiation.

**Step 2 — Physical interpretation (2 marks):**
The $(\sqrt{\text{area}})^{1/6}$ exponent reflects the experimentally observed weak but consistent dependence of fatigue limit on defect size. Even a 10× increase in defect area only reduces $\sigma_w$ by a factor of $10^{1/6} \approx 1.47$, which is consistent with the **defect-tolerance** approach used in AM qualification.

**Step 3 — Numerical calculation (3 marks):**
Given: √area = 100 μm, HV = 210, C = 1.43 (internal defect, steel).

Compute $\text{HV} + 120 = 210 + 120 = 330$

Compute $(\sqrt{\text{area}})^{1/6} = (100)^{1/6} = 10^{6/6} = 10^{1} = 2.154$ (since $100^{1/6} = e^{\ln(100)/6} = e^{4.605/6} = e^{0.7676} \approx 2.154$)

Apply:
$$
\sigma_w = \frac{1.43 \times 330}{2.154} = \frac{471.9}{2.154} \approx 219.1 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with defect-free value (bonus insight):**
For defect-free 316L SS, $\sigma_w \approx 280$ MPa. The 100 μm √area internal defect reduces the fatigue limit by approximately **22%**, demonstrating the strong sensitivity of AM parts to internal porosity.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

| Property | As-Built | Heat-Treated (HT) | HIP + HT |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Yield Strength (MPa)** | ~1000–1100 | ~850–950 | ~800–900 |
| **UTS (MPa)** | ~1050–1200 | ~950–1000 | ~900–1000 |
| **Elongation (%)** | ~3–6 | ~8–12 | ~10–15 |
| **Fatigue Strength (10⁷ cycles, MPa)** | ~200–350 | ~350–450 | ~450–550 |
| **Fracture Toughness (MPa·√m)** | ~30–45 | ~50–60 | ~65–75 |

**Microstructural reasons (7 marks distributed):**

**As-built (1.5 marks):**
- Martensitic α' phase → high strength, low ductility
- Fine cellular substructure → high Hall-Petch strengthening
- Internal porosity and residual stress → poor fatigue
- Anisotropic columnar prior-β grains

**Heat-treated (2.5 marks):**
- α' → α + β transformation → balanced strength and ductility
- Cell coarsening → reduced Hall-Petch contribution (strength drops)
- Residual stress relieved → improved fatigue
- **But porosity remains** → still limits fatigue and toughness

**HIP + HT (3 marks):**
- Internal pores collapse and diffusion-bond shut → near-full density
- Fatigue strength recovers dramatically (often **2–3×** the as-built value)
- Fracture toughness approaches wrought levels (~70–75 MPa·√m)
- Anisotropy reduced (texture partially homogenized)
- Grain coarsening → slightly lower strength than as-built, but much better balanced

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Question B):** A very common error is to claim that **HIP always increases strength**. In reality, HIP closes porosity (improving fatigue and toughness) but also coarsens microstructure (reducing yield strength slightly). The net effect on tensile strength depends on the balance. KTU expects a nuanced answer, not a blanket statement.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist — Module 3 (Mechanical):**

- **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** is the master process parameter: $\text{VED} = P/(vht)$. Optimal VED minimizes defects; off-optimal VED causes lack-of-fusion (low VED) or keyholing (high VED).
- **Hall-Petch relation** governs the high as-built strength: $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ with $d$ = cell size in AM.
- **Anisotropy Index (AI)** quantifies directional dependence: $\text{AI} = (P_H - P_V)/P_H$. Typical AI for as-built Ti-6Al-4V is +5% to +15%.
- **Residual stress** originates from thermal gradient during layer-wise deposition: $\sigma \propto E\alpha\Delta T$. Mitigated by pre-heating, scan strategy, and stress relief HT.
- **Murakami √area model:** $\sigma_w = C(\text{HV}+120)/(\sqrt{\text{area}})^{1/6}$ — the standard defect-tolerance fatigue model for AM.
- **Basquin's law** for S-N curve: $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$, with $b \approx -0.05$ to $-0.12$ for metals.
- **HIP** is the most effective single post-process for restoring fatigue and fracture toughness; HT transforms α' martensite to balanced α+β in Ti alloys.
- **Surface roughness** in as-built AM (Ra = 10–20 μm) is a major fatigue crack initiation site — must be machined or peened for critical applications.
- **Engineering vs. true stress/strain:** $\sigma_t = \sigma(1+\varepsilon)$, $\varepsilon_t = \ln(1+\varepsilon)$ — only valid before necking.
- **Fracture toughness of as-built AM Ti-6Al-4V (~30–55 MPa·√m)** is significantly lower than wrought (~75 MPa·√m), but HIP + HT restores it to within 80–90%.
- **Statistical qualification (Weibull analysis)** is increasingly required for AM safety-critical parts because part-to-part variability is higher than in wrought materials.
- **Standard test methods:** ASTM E8 (tensile), ASTM E466 (fatigue), ASTM E23 (impact), ASTM E384 (hardness), ASTM E837 (hole-drilling residual stress).
- **KTU favourite exam question type:** "Discuss the influence of build orientation on mechanical properties of AM parts with reference to Ti-6Al-4V."
- **Engineering analogy to remember:** AM parts behave like a **brick wall** — strong along the brick rows, weaker across the mortar layers (build direction).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal and Chemical Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET867 - METAL ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermal and Chemical Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In **Metal Additive Manufacturing (MAM)**, *Thermal and Chemical Properties* of the feedstock powder (and the consolidated material during processing) constitute the **fundamental physical–chemical descriptors** that govern the energy-matter interaction between the heat source (laser, electron beam, or arc) and the metallic powder bed or feed stream. These properties dictate the **melting kinetics**, **solidification behavior**, **microstructural evolution**, **residual stress generation**, and the **defect formation tendency** (porosity, cracking, balling, lack-of-fusion) during layer-by-layer deposition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition**: Thermal properties describe the *response of a metal to heat input* (storage, transport, phase change, and expansion), while chemical properties describe the *reactivity, composition purity, and affinity* of the metal powder to its surrounding atmosphere and to itself during high-temperature exposure inside the AM build chamber.

### 1.2 The Two Property Classes at a Glance

| Property Class | Core Concern | Why It Matters in MAM |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermal** | Heat storage, conduction, phase change, expansion | Controls melt pool size, cooling rate $R$, thermal gradient $G$, and residual stresses |
| **Chemical** | Composition, oxidation, contamination, reactivity | Controls defect chemistry, interstitial embrittlement, and mechanical integrity |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Cooking Pot" Model

Imagine you are cooking a dish in a metal pot on a high-power stove:

- **Thermal properties** are like the *material of the pot* — how fast it heats up (thermal conductivity $k$), how much heat it stores (specific heat $c_p$), and at what temperature it melts (melting point $T_m$). A copper pot (high $k$) heats faster than a cast-iron pot (low $k$).
- **Chemical properties** are like the *ingredients* — whether they burn easily in air (oxidation tendency of Titanium), whether they spoil when wet (moisture pickup of Al powder), or whether they react with each other (inter-diffusion in multi-alloy builds).

> [!NOTE]
> In Selective Laser Melting (SLM), the laser deposits energy in microseconds. If the powder has **low thermal conductivity** (like titanium alloys, $k \approx 7$ W/m·K), heat cannot escape quickly, leading to a **large, stable melt pool** but also higher residual stresses. Conversely, **high thermal conductivity** materials (like copper, $k \approx 400$ W/m·K) dissipate heat rapidly, requiring **higher laser power** to maintain a melt pool.

### 1.4 The Standard Set of Thermal & Chemical Descriptors

**Thermal Properties of Powders/Consolidated Metal:**
1. Specific heat capacity ($c_p$) — J/(kg·K)
2. Thermal conductivity ($k$) — W/(m·K)
3. Thermal diffusivity ($\alpha$) — m²/s
4. Melting point / Solidus ($T_s$) / Liquidus ($T_l$) temperatures — °C or K
5. Enthalpy of fusion ($\Delta H_f$) — J/kg
6. Coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE, $\beta$) — 1/K
7. Latent heat of vaporization ($\Delta H_v$) — J/kg
8. Emissivity ($\varepsilon$) — dimensionless

**Chemical Properties of Powders:**
1. Bulk chemical composition (alloy designation)
2. Interstitial impurity content (O, N, H, C, S) — wt. ppm
3. Surface oxide layer thickness — nm
4. Moisture content — wt. %
5. Apparent/reactive chemistry under laser exposure
6. Powder recyclability and oxidation re-growth rate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight**: Always distinguish between **bulk** properties (of solid metal) and **effective** properties (of powder bed). Powder beds have **drastically lower** effective thermal conductivity than bulk metal due to inter-particle contact resistance. For a stainless steel powder bed, $k_{bed} \approx 0.25$–$0.5$ W/m·K vs. bulk $k \approx 16$ W/m·K.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — How Properties Control the AM Process

The Metal AM process (SLM/DMLS/EBM/DED) is fundamentally a **transient, multi-physics, non-equilibrium thermal event**. The governing energy balance per unit volume of melt pool is given by the classical **Rosenthal-type heat equation** modified for moving Gaussian heat source:

$$\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot \left( k \nabla T \right) + Q_{source} - Q_{loss}$$

Where every term on the right is influenced by thermal/chemical properties.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 Thermal Diffusivity ($\alpha$) — The "Speed of Heat"
$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho \cdot c_p}$$
- **Physical meaning**: How fast a temperature disturbance propagates through the material.
- **High $\alpha$** → heat spreads quickly → shallow, wide melt pool → less residual stress.
- **Low $\alpha$** → heat stays localized → deep, narrow melt pool → higher residual stress and steeper thermal gradients.

#### 2.2.2 Solidification Cooling Rate ($R$) and Thermal Gradient ($G$)
- The **microstructure** of the AM build is governed by the product $G \times R$:
  - **High $G \times R$** → fine cellular/dendritic structure (good strength).
  - **Low $G \times R$** → coarse grains (lower strength, anisotropy).
- $G$ depends on $k$ of substrate and adjacent solidified layers.
- $R$ depends on $c_p$, $\Delta H_f$, and the energy density $E = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$.

#### 2.2.3 Enthalpy of Fusion and Marangoni Flow
$$\Delta H_f = \int_{T_s}^{T_l} c_p(T) \, dT + L_f$$
- The **latent heat $L_f$** must be supplied for solid→liquid phase change.
- Once molten, **surface tension gradient** (Marangoni effect) drives fluid flow:
$$Ma = -\frac{d\gamma}{dT} \cdot \frac{\Delta T \cdot L}{\mu \cdot \alpha}$$
where $\gamma$ is surface tension, $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity. This affects **balling**, **spatter**, and **pore entrapment**.

#### 2.2.4 Chemical Reactivity Classes for MAM Powders

| Metal Class | Examples | Key Chemical Issue | Mitigation in AM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reactive metals** | Ti, Zr, Mg | High O & N affinity, embrittlement | Inert Ar chamber (O₂ < 50 ppm), EBM vacuum |
| **Oxide-forming** | Al, Si | Stable oxide skin (Al₂O₃, $\sim$5 nm thick) | High laser power, hopper drying |
| **Noble metals** | Au, Ag | Low oxidation, high reflectivity | Higher energy density needed |
| **Refractory** | W, Mo, Ta | High $T_m$, oxidation at $T > 400$°C | Vacuum or dry atmosphere |
| **Fe/Ni/Co base** | SS316L, Inconel 718 | Moderate, sensitive to C, S | Standard Ar atmosphere |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Ti-6Al-4V requires an oxygen-free environment**: Titanium has a strong thermodynamic drive to form TiO₂ ($\Delta G_f^0 \approx -890$ kJ/mol). At melt-pool temperatures ($> 1650$ °C), interstitial oxygen dissolves rapidly into the β-Ti lattice, forming a brittle α-case. This is why EBM processes Ti alloys under vacuum and SLM uses high-purity argon with O₂ levels **< 50 ppm** (often **< 10 ppm** for critical aerospace parts).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield, Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Markdown Tables**: All absolute value / norm symbols are rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` (LaTeX) to preserve table integrity. Avoid using the raw pipe `|` character in cell contents.

| # | Formula | LaTeX Form | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Thermal Diffusivity | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \, c_p}$ | Speed of heat propagation | m²/s |
| 2 | Volumetric Energy Density | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \, h \, t}$ | Laser energy per unit volume | J/mm³ |
| 3 | Line Energy Density | $E_L = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | Energy per unit scan length | J/mm |
| 4 | Cooling Rate (R) | $R = \dfrac{2\pi k (T_m - T_0)^2}{P}$ | Rosenthal 3D cooling rate | K/s |
| 5 | Thermal Gradient (G) | $G = \dfrac{2\pi k (T_m - T_0)}{P}$ | Local thermal gradient | K/m |
| 6 | Enthalpy Balance | $H(T) = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} c_p \, dT + (1-f_s) L_f$ | Sensible + latent heat | J/kg |
| 7 | Linear Thermal Expansion | $\Delta L = L_0 \, \beta \, \Delta T$ | Dimensional change | m |
| 8 | Solidification Range | $\Delta T_{SL} = T_l - T_s$ | Cracking susceptibility | K |
| 9 | Biot Number | $Bi = \dfrac{h_t L_c}{k}$ | Surface vs internal conduction | dimensionless |
| 10 | Fourier Number | $Fo = \dfrac{\alpha \, t}{L^2}$ | Transient heat flow | dimensionless |
| 11 | Peclet Number (Melt Pool) | $Pe = \dfrac{v \, L}{2\alpha}$ | Advection vs conduction | dimensionless |
| 12 | Marangoni Number | $Ma = \dfrac{\vert d\gamma/dT \vert \, \Delta T \, L}{\mu \, \alpha}$ | Surface tension driven flow | dimensionless |
| 13 | Powder Bed Effective Conductivity (Zehner-Bauer-Schlünder) | $k_{bed} = k_g \left[1 + \dfrac{(1-\phi)(1+\kappa)}{2} \cdot \phi \right]$ | Packed powder conduction | W/(m·K) |
| 14 | Oxidation Mass Gain (parabolic) | $\Delta m^2 = k_p \, t$ | High-T oxidation kinetics | mg²/cm⁴ |
| 15 | Equilibrium O Solubility in α-Ti | $\log[O]_{wt\%} = -\dfrac{7720}{T} + 1.96$ | Interstitial pick-up | wt% |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (Ti-6Al-4V brackets)**: Property data used to predict residual stress and distortion, enabling scan strategy optimization.
- **Medical implants (Co-Cr-Mo)**: Thermal properties control grain structure, which dictates fatigue life.
- **Tool steel (H13, Maraging 300)**: Solidification range $\Delta T_{SL}$ predicts hot-cracking susceptibility.
- **Heat exchangers (Cu, AlSi10Mg)**: High thermal conductivity demands higher laser power to overcome rapid heat dissipation.
- **Reactive metal handling**: Chemical property analysis dictates powder storage protocols (Ar glovebox, vacuum desiccators).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Thermal Diffusivity from Fourier's Law

We begin with **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction** for one-dimensional, transient conduction in a homogeneous, isotropic solid:

$$q'' = -k \, \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}$$

For an elemental control volume of thickness $dx$, the net energy accumulation must equal the divergence of heat flux:

$$\rho \, c_p \, \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial q''}{\partial x}$$

Substituting Fourier's Law into the energy balance:

$$\rho \, c_p \, \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-k \, \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right)$$

For a material with **constant thermal conductivity $k$**:

$$\rho \, c_p \, \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = k \, \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

Rearranging to isolate the time derivative:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \left(\frac{k}{\rho \, c_p}\right) \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

By definition, the bracketed term is **thermal diffusivity**:

$$\boxed{\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho \, c_p}}$$

This derivation shows that **$\alpha$ is the proportionality constant** that converts spatial curvature of temperature into a rate of temperature change with time.

### 3.2 Derivation II: Volumetric Energy Density in LPBF

The Laser Powder Bed Fusion (LPBF) process uses a Gaussian laser source. For a single scan track of length $L_s$ with hatch spacing $h$ and layer thickness $t$, the volume melted is:

$$V_{melt} = L_s \cdot h \cdot t$$

The total energy delivered to this volume over scan time $t_s = L_s / v$ at laser power $P$ is:

$$E_{delivered} = P \cdot t_s = P \cdot \frac{L_s}{v}$$

Therefore the **volumetric energy density** is:

$$E_v = \frac{E_{delivered}}{V_{melt}} = \frac{P \cdot L_s / v}{L_s \cdot h \cdot t}$$

$$\boxed{E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering significance**: This single equation decides whether a part will have **lack-of-fusion porosity** ($E_v < E_{critical}$) or **keyhole porosity** ($E_v > E_{keyhole}$). Typical optimum range for Ti-6Al-4V is **60–120 J/mm³**; for AlSi10Mg it is **30–70 J/mm³**.

### 3.3 Derivation III: Rosenthal's 3D Moving Point Source Solution

For a point heat source moving with velocity $v$ along the $x$-axis on a semi-infinite plate, the steady-state temperature distribution is:

$$T - T_0 = \frac{Q}{2\pi k} \cdot \frac{1}{R'} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{v(R'+x)}{2\alpha}\right)$$

Where $R' = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$ and $Q$ is the absorbed laser power.

At the **melt pool boundary**, $T = T_m$, giving the cooling rate $R$ at the solidification front:

$$\boxed{R = \frac{2\pi k (T_m - T_0)^2}{P}}$$

And the thermal gradient:

$$\boxed{G = \frac{2\pi k (T_m - T_0)}{P}}$$

These two equations define the **solidification map** for AM — the product $G \times R$ predicts microstructure length scale, while $G/R$ predicts morphology (cellular vs dendritic).

### 3.4 Derivation IV: Effective Thermal Conductivity of a Powder Bed (Zehner–Bauer–Schlünder Model)

For a static powder bed of porosity $\phi$ (typical $\phi \approx 0.35$–$0.45$ in LPBF) with gas filling the interstitial space (conductivity $k_g$), the effective bed conductivity is:

$$k_{bed} = k_g \left[ 1 + \frac{(1-\phi)}{\phi} \cdot \frac{(1+\kappa)}{2} \cdot \phi \right]$$

Where $\kappa = k_s / k_g$ is the solid-to-gas conductivity ratio. Since for most metals $k_s \gg k_g$, we approximate:

$$k_{bed} \approx k_g \cdot \left[1 + \frac{(1-\phi)(1+\kappa)}{2}\right] \cdot \phi$$

For SS316L powder bed with $\phi = 0.4$, $k_g (\text{argon}) \approx 0.018$ W/(m·K), $k_s \approx 16$ W/(m·K):
- $\kappa \approx 16/0.018 \approx 889$
- $k_{bed} \approx 0.018 \cdot [1 + 0.6 \cdot 445] \cdot 0.4 \approx 0.018 \cdot [1 + 267] \cdot 0.4$
- $k_{bed} \approx 0.018 \cdot 268 \cdot 0.4 \approx 1.93$ W/(m·K)

Compare with **bulk SS316L = 16 W/(m·K)** — the powder bed is roughly **8× less conductive** than the solid. This explains the slow lateral heat spread in LPBF and the need for **heated build plates** (often to 80–200 °C).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Property → Process Effect → Defect Mechanism

| Property | Quantitative Influence | Resulting Process Effect | Resulting Defect / Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| High $k$ (e.g., Cu) | Rapid lateral heat spread | Wide shallow melt pool | Need high laser power; risk of incomplete melting |
| Low $k$ (e.g., Ti) | Localized heat retention | Deep narrow melt pool, high $G$ | High residual stress, distortion |
| High $c_p$ (e.g., SS) | More energy to heat up | Lower peak temperature | Reduced vaporization, less keyholing |
| Low $T_m$ (e.g., Al) | Easy melting but high CTE | High thermal strain | Warping, delamination |
| Wide $\Delta T_{SL}$ (e.g., Inconel 718) | Long mushy zone | Liquid-film feeding failure | **Solidification cracking** |
| High O-affinity (e.g., Ti) | Dissolution in β-phase | α-case formation | Embrittlement, low ductility |
| Surface oxide (e.g., Al₂O₃ on Al) | High melt temperature (~2072 °C) | Laser energy barrier | Balling, lack-of-fusion |
| Moisture (e.g., PA12, but also metals) | H₂O dissociation → H₂ + O | Pore formation, O pickup | Porosity, hydrogen embrittlement |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define thermal diffusivity. Explain its significance in metal additive manufacturing with an example.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark)**: Thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ is defined as the ratio of thermal conductivity $k$ to the volumetric heat capacity $\rho \cdot c_p$:

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho \, c_p}$$

It has units of **m²/s** and represents the rate at which temperature changes propagate through a material.

- **Significance in MAM (1 Mark)**: In processes like SLM, a high $\alpha$ means heat spreads quickly, producing a wide, shallow melt pool with lower thermal gradients. A low $\alpha$ concentrates heat, creating deep melt pools and high residual stresses.

- **Example (1 Mark)**: For **Ti-6Al-4V**, $\alpha \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s (low), causing localized heat retention and high residual stress — hence the need for build plate heating (typically 200 °C). For **SS316L**, $\alpha \approx 4 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s (higher), allowing easier thermal management.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Question]**
*Why are titanium-based powders handled under inert/oxygen-free atmospheres in additive manufacturing?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Chemical affinity (1 Mark)**: Titanium has a strong thermodynamic tendency to form TiO₂ ($\Delta G_f^0 \approx -890$ kJ/mol at 25 °C), making it highly reactive with atmospheric oxygen, especially at elevated temperatures.

- **Embrittlement mechanism (1 Mark)**: At temperatures above 400 °C, oxygen dissolves interstitially into the Ti lattice (HCP α-phase), forming a brittle "α-case" that drastically reduces ductility and fatigue life.

- **AM-specific mitigation (1 Mark)**: SLM machines maintain argon atmosphere with **O₂ < 50 ppm** (often **< 10 ppm** for aerospace parts), and EBM operates under vacuum ($10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ mbar) to prevent interstitial contamination during the brief melt-pool lifetime.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert**:
> - **Do not** confuse *thermal conductivity* (W/m·K, steady state) with *thermal diffusivity* (m²/s, transient). This is a **common 1–2 mark deduction** error.
> - When asked for a process window, **always quote numerical values with units**. Vague answers like "high power" fetch partial credit only.
> - For reactive metals, **state the ppm threshold** of O₂/N₂ control — examiners specifically test this.
> - Sketch a labeled diagram wherever asked; an unlabelled diagram gets **zero marks** even if technically correct.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain the role of thermal properties in determining the microstructure and defect formation during Selective Laser Melting. Derive the relationship between thermal diffusivity and the governing heat equation. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — List relevant thermal properties (2 Marks):**
- Specific heat capacity $c_p$
- Thermal conductivity $k$
- Thermal diffusivity $\alpha$
- Melting point $T_m$
- Enthalpy of fusion $\Delta H_f$
- Coefficient of thermal expansion $\beta$

**Step 2 — Link properties to microstructure (2 Marks):**
- The product $G \times R$ controls dendrite arm spacing $\lambda$ via $\lambda \propto G^{-0.5} R^{-0.25}$.
- High $c_p$ and $\Delta H_f$ require more laser energy, lowering cooling rate $R$ → coarser microstructure.
- High $k$ → rapid lateral heat extraction → high $G$ → finer columnar grains.
- Anisotropic $k$ in solid (e.g., Ti HCP: $k_{basal} \neq k_{prismatic}$) → directional grain growth along build direction.

**Step 3 — Link properties to defects (2 Marks):**
- High $\beta$ (e.g., Al, Mg) → high thermal strain → distortion & delamination.
- Wide solidification range $\Delta T_{SL}$ (e.g., Inconel 718, $\Delta T_{SL} \approx 90$ K) → cracking susceptibility.
- Low $\alpha$ → high thermal gradient $G$ → residual stress accumulation.

**Step 4 — Derivation of thermal diffusivity (1 Mark):**
$$\boxed{\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho \, c_p}}$$

From the energy balance:
$$\rho \, c_p \, \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = k \, \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2} \Rightarrow \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \, \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

**(b)** Discuss how chemical properties of metal powders influence the choice of process atmosphere in additive manufacturing. Compare SLM and EBM strategies for titanium alloys. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Key chemical properties (2 Marks):**
- Interstitial affinity (O, N, H pick-up)
- Surface oxide film stability
- Moisture sensitivity
- Powder contamination/recyclability

**Step 2 — Effect on atmosphere (2 Marks):**
- **O₂ partial pressure** controls Ti-α-case formation: $P_{O_2} < 10^{-25}$ atm at 1650 °C required to prevent oxidation (Ellingham diagram).
- **Moisture** dissociates to H₂ + ½O₂ at laser wavelengths → porosity + H-embrittlement.
- **N₂** forms TiN → brittle nitride precipitates.

**Step 3 — SLM strategy (1.5 Marks):**
- Argon gas (purity 99.999%), sealed chamber, O₂ monitored continuously.
- Target: **O₂ < 50 ppm**, often **< 10 ppm** for aerospace (ASTM F3055).
- Build plate preheated to 200 °C to reduce thermal shock.
- Powder stored in Ar glovebox; recycled powder sieved + tested for O content.

**Step 4 — EBM strategy (1.5 Marks):**
- Vacuum level: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ mbar.
- He partial pressure backfill (~0.1 Pa) for heat dissipation.
- Higher build plate temperature (~700 °C) — reduces residual stress but allows slight α-phase coarsening.
- Allows processing of oxygen-sensitive alloys (TiAl, γ-TiAl).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the Rosenthal thermal model for a moving heat source. Derive expressions for the thermal gradient $G$ and solidification rate $R$. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Model assumptions (1 Mark):**
- Semi-infinite plate
- Point heat source moving at constant velocity $v$ along $x$-axis
- Constant material properties ($k$, $\rho$, $c_p$)
- No latent heat, no convection, no radiation

**Step 2 — Governing equation (1 Mark):**
$$\nabla^2 T + \frac{Q}{k} \delta(x - vt)\delta(y)\delta(z) = \frac{1}{\alpha} \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}$$

**Step 3 — Solution form (2 Marks):**
$$T - T_0 = \frac{Q}{2\pi k} \cdot \frac{1}{R'} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{v(R' + x)}{2\alpha}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Derivation of $G$ and $R$ (2 Marks):**

At the trailing edge of the melt pool, $T = T_m$. Differentiating the solution along the $x$-axis and taking the limit at the trailing edge:

$$G = \left|\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right|_{T=T_m} = \frac{2\pi k (T_m - T_0)}{P}$$

$$R = v \cdot \cos\theta \approx \frac{2\pi k (T_m - T_0)^2}{P}$$

(where $\theta$ is the angle between the scan direction and the solidification front normal.)

**Step 5 — Engineering utility (1 Mark):**
- $G \times R$ predicts microstructural length scale.
- $G/R$ predicts morphology (planar/cellular/dendritic).
- Process optimization aims for high $G \times R$ for fine, strong microstructures.

**(b)** Explain the chemical property considerations for processing of **AlSi10Mg** and **Inconel 718** powders. What precautions are taken for storage and recycling? **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — AlSi10Mg chemistry (2 Marks):**
- Composition: Al balance, Si 9–11%, Mg 0.2–0.5%, Fe < 0.55%.
- **Critical issue**: Stable Al₂O₃ skin (5–10 nm) on every powder particle. $T_{melt}(\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3) \approx 2072$ °C, far above Al matrix ($660$ °C). This oxide must be disrupted by the laser.
- Moisture pickup → H₂ porosity in melt pool.

**Step 2 — AlSi10Mg handling (1.5 Marks):**
- Powder pre-dried in vacuum oven at 100–150 °C for 4–6 hours.
- Stored in sealed containers with desiccant.
- Atmosphere: dry argon (dew point $< -40$ °C).
- Process: high energy density, preheated bed 150–200 °C.

**Step 3 — Inconel 718 chemistry (2 Marks):**
- Complex composition: Ni-19Cr-18Fe-5Nb-3Mo-1Ti-0.5Al.
- **Wide solidification range** $\Delta T_{SL} \approx 80$–$100$ °C → high hot-cracking susceptibility.
- Sensitive to **Nb segregation** → Laves phase formation.
- Reactive with O, S, P → forms low-melting eutectics at grain boundaries.

**Step 4 — Inconel 718 handling & recycling (1.5 Marks):**
- Argon atmosphere, O₂ < 50 ppm.
- Powder recycled through **sieve + air-classify** to remove fines (< 15 µm) and oxidized satellites.
- Each recycle cycle → ICP-OES test for composition drift; typically limited to **5–8 reuse cycles** to maintain Nb content and avoid S/P build-up.
- Stress-relief heat treatment at 1065 °C + aging at 720 °C / 620 °C.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Thermal Diffusivity** = $\dfrac{k}{\rho c_p}$ — distinguishes from thermal conductivity; remember the units (m²/s).
- **Volumetric Energy Density** $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v h t}$ — too low → lack-of-fusion; too high → keyholing.
- **Rosenthal equations** for $G$ and $R$ — derived from point-source moving heat solution.
- **Ti-6Al-4V is the "poster child" of reactive metal AM** — knows its O-affinity (TiO₂), α-case formation, and ppm-level O₂ control requirement.
- **AlSi10Mg has stable Al₂O₃ skin** → disrupts melt pool formation; needs pre-drying and preheating.
- **Inconel 718 has wide $\Delta T_{SL}$** → solidification cracking risk; mitigated by scan strategy.
- **Effective $k_{bed}$ is much lower than bulk $k$** due to porosity — drives the use of heated build plates.
- **Marangoni flow** is driven by surface tension gradient $\dfrac{d\gamma}{dT}$; sulfur content (even ppm level) reverses flow direction → balling.
- **Powder recycling** is limited — typically 5–8 cycles before O/N content exceeds specification.
- **Moisture control** — measure dew point, not just relative humidity; Ar dew point $< -40$ °C is industry standard.
- **CTE mismatch between substrate and build** drives residual stress — for Invar substrates on steel parts, $\Delta\beta$ should be $< 2 \times 10^{-6}$ /K.
- **Emissivity $\varepsilon$** affects heat losses in EBM (where build is at 700 °C) — high-$\varepsilon$ powders (dark oxides) lose more heat by radiation $\sigma \varepsilon T^4$.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Thermal and Chemical Properties**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Post Processing and Testing - Heat Treatment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET867 - METAL ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Post Processing and Testing - Heat Treatment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Metal Additive Manufacturing (MAM), **Heat Treatment (HT)** refers to a controlled sequence of thermal processing operations (heating, soaking, and cooling) applied to as-built AM components to modify their microstructure, relieve residual stresses, improve mechanical properties, and achieve the required dimensional stability and performance specifications dictated by standards such as **ASTM F3301** and **AMS 7000-series** specifications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Heat treatment in MAM is *not* a single operation but a *chain of metallurgical events* — stress relieving, annealing, solution treatment, aging, and optionally Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP) — each engineered to counter the unique metallurgical defects (residual stress, anisotropy, metastable phases) introduced by the layer-by-layer rapid solidification inherent to processes like **SLM (Selective Laser Melting)**, **DMLS (Direct Metal Laser Sintering)**, **EBM (Electron Beam Melting)**, and **DED (Directed Energy Deposition)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Stressed-Out Sculpture"

Imagine a master potter rapidly shaping a clay sculpture on a spinning wheel. Because the clay is shaped and dried unevenly, the finished piece is full of **internal tension** — some parts are pulled, some compressed. If you fire it immediately in a kiln, it cracks. What does a skilled potter do? They **let it rest and dry slowly**, then place it in a *low-temperature kiln first* (analogous to **stress relief**), and only later subject it to the *high-temperature glaze firing* (analogous to **solution treatment + aging**).

> An AM-printed metal part behaves identically: rapid melting and solidification (cooling rates of **10³–10⁶ K/s**) trap internal stresses and non-equilibrium microstructures. Heat treatment is the metallurgist's "patience kiln" — systematically undoing these defects to deliver a stable, high-performance component.

### 1.3 Why Heat Treatment is *Non-Negotiable* in MAM

| AM-Induced Defect | Origin | Heat Treatment Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| Residual Tensile Stress | Steep thermal gradients | Stress Relief Annealing |
| Anisotropic Grain Structure | Directional heat flow | Homogenization Annealing |
| Metastable Phases (e.g., α′ martensite in Ti-6Al-4V) | Rapid cooling | Solution Treatment |
| Sub-optimal Hardness/Strength | Non-equilibrium supersaturation | Aging (Precipitation Hardening) |
| Internal Porosity (gas, lack-of-fusion) | Process physics | Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics in HT of AM Parts:**
> - **Cooling Rate in AM:** $10^3$ to $10^6$ K/s
> - **Typical Stress Relief Temperature Range:** $0.4\,T_m$ to $0.6\,T_m$ (where $T_m$ is the melting point in Kelvin)
> - **Standard HT Reference Temperatures (Ti-6Al-4V):** Stress relief at $650–700\,°C$, Solution at $850–950\,°C$, Aging at $500–600\,°C$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Stage Heat Treatment Architecture for AM Parts

The complete HT workflow for an AM component follows a logical metallurgical sequence:

**Stage 1 — Stress Relief Annealing (SRA)**
- **Goal:** Reduce locked-in residual stresses (often up to **80% of yield strength** in as-built SLM parts) to prevent distortion during machining or in service.
- **Temperature Window:** Typically $0.4\,T_m$ to $0.6\,T_m$ (in Kelvin).
- **Mechanism:** Dislocation climb, recovery, and partial recrystallization — without significant phase transformation.
- **Typical Soak Time:** 1 to 4 hours, followed by furnace cooling or inert gas cooling.

**Stage 2 — Solution Treatment (Homogenization)**
- **Goal:** Dissolve non-equilibrium segregation and intermetallic phases into a single-phase solid solution.
- **Mechanism:** Heating above the solvus temperature, holding, then rapid quenching (water or inert gas) to retain a supersaturated single phase.
- **Critical for:** Precipitation-hardenable alloys like **AlSi10Mg**, **Inconel 718**, and **maraging steel (18Ni300)**.

**Stage 3 — Quenching**
- **Goal:** Trap solute atoms in a supersaturated solid solution by rapid cooling.
- **Common Media:** Water (fastest), polymer (glycol-based), forced argon/nitrogen gas, oil.
- **Caution:** Quenching introduces *new* residual stresses — must often be followed by stress relief.

**Stage 4 — Aging (Precipitation Hardening)**
- **Goal:** Controlled precipitation of fine, uniformly dispersed second-phase particles to impede dislocation motion.
- **Two Variants:**
  - *Natural Aging:* Room temperature (e.g., Al-alloys).
  - *Artificial Aging:* Elevated temperature ($150–650\,°C$) for hours — the standard for AM alloys.

**Stage 5 — Optional Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)**
- **Goal:** Simultaneously apply high temperature ($0.6\,T_m$ to $0.9\,T_m$) and high isostatic pressure (**100–200 MPa** typically of argon) to close internal porosity and improve fatigue life.
- **Effect:** Can reduce porosity from ~1% to <0.1% by volume, achieving near-wrought fatigue performance.

### 2.2 The Core 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why stress relief first?** Any subsequent machining or service load on a stressed part will cause unpredictable distortion. Removing stress *before* further processing ensures dimensional stability.
- **Why solution treat before aging?** Aging requires a *supersaturated* solid solution. Without solution treatment, there are no excess solute atoms to precipitate.
- **Why HIP for fatigue-critical parts?** Internal pores act as fatigue crack initiation sites. HIP closes them metallurgically (via diffusion bonding) under simultaneous heat and pressure.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius diffusion equation; $D$ = diffusivity, $Q$ = activation energy, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K | Predicting diffusion-controlled HT behavior |
| 2 | $D^n - D_0^n = K_0\,t\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | Grain growth law (Burke–Turnbull); $n \approx 2$ for ideal, $n \approx 3$–$4$ in practice | Predicting grain coarsening during HT |
| 3 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k\,d^{-1/2}$ | Hall–Petch relation; $\sigma_y$ = yield strength, $d$ = mean grain diameter | Quantifying strength loss from grain growth |
| 4 | $\dfrac{t_1}{t_2} = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{Q}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$ | Time-temperature equivalence for equivalent microstructural state | Scaling lab HT to industrial soak times |
| 5 | $T_{\text{rel}} = 0.4\,T_m$ to $0.6\,T_m$ | Empirical stress relief temperature window (K) | Selecting SRA temperature |
| 6 | $P_{\text{HIP}} = 100$ to $200$ MPa | Typical HIP pressure range | AM densification specifications |
| 7 | $t_{\text{aging}} \propto T_{\text{aging}}^{-1}$ | Aging kinetics — higher temperature shortens peak-aging time | Optimizing artificial aging cycles |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** When asked about HT selection, *always* justify the temperature choice using the homologous temperature concept ($T/T_m$) and reference the relevant activation energy $Q$ for the dominant diffusion mechanism (volume, grain boundary, or surface).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (Ti-6Al-4V brackets, Inconel 718 turbine blades):** HT enables AM parts to meet AMS 4911 and AMS 5662 specifications for fracture toughness and creep resistance.
- **Medical (Ti-6Al-4V spinal cages, hip implants):** SRA + HIP ensures biocompatibility (no trapped gas pores) and fatigue life exceeding 10 million cycles.
- **Automotive (AlSi10Mg brackets, maraging steel inserts for tooling):** Aging cycles deliver hardness values of **40–45 HRC** for maraging steel tool inserts printed via SLM.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Time-Temperature Equivalence for Heat Treatment Scaling

**Statement:** For a diffusion-controlled process, two different combinations of soaking time $t$ and absolute temperature $T$ produce the *same* microstructural state if their integrated diffusion effect is equal.

**Starting Principle (Arrhenius-type rate equation):**
$$\text{Rate} \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Cumulative diffusion effect over a soak time $t$:**
$$\text{Effect} = t \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Equate the effect for two temperature-time pairs $(T_1, t_1)$ and $(T_2, t_2)$:**
$$t_1 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{R\,T_1}\right) = t_2 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{R\,T_2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Isolate $t_1 / t_2$ by dividing both sides:**
$$\dfrac{t_1}{t_2} = \dfrac{\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{R\,T_2}\right)}{\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{R\,T_1}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Combine the exponentials using property $\exp(a)/\exp(b) = \exp(a - b)$:**
$$\dfrac{t_1}{t_2} = \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{Q}{R\,T_2} - \left(-\dfrac{Q}{R\,T_1}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the exponent:**
$$\dfrac{t_1}{t_2} = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{Q}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$$

> This is the **Hollomon–Jaffe parameter** form, widely used in tempering and aging.

### 3.2 Derivation: Grain Growth During High-Temperature HT

**Statement:** Grain diameter $D$ grows as a function of soaking time $t$ at temperature $T$.

**Step 1 — Empirical kinetic law (Burke–Turnbull, 1952):**
$$D^n - D_0^n = K_0\,t\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_{\text{growth}}}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 2 — For long soaking times where $D \gg D_0$, neglect the initial grain size:**
$$D^n \approx K_0\,t\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_{\text{growth}}}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for grain size $D$:**
$$D \approx \left(K_0\right)^{1/n} \, t^{1/n} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_{\text{growth}}}{n\,R\,T}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Introduce a single effective constant $A$ for compactness:**
$$D = A\,t^{1/n}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_{\text{growth}}}{n\,R\,T}\right)$$

> **Engineering Implication:** Holding an AM part *too long* at high temperature coarsens the grains → Hall–Petch predicts a drop in yield strength. Hence HT cycles are designed to be *minimum sufficient*, not maximum.

### 3.3 Derivation: Strength Loss from Grain Coarsening (Hall–Petch)

**Step 1 — Hall–Petch baseline yield strength:**
$$\sigma_y^{(0)} = \sigma_0 + k\,(d_0)^{-1/2}$$

**Step 2 — After HT, grain size increases to $d_1 > d_0$:**
$$\sigma_y^{(1)} = \sigma_0 + k\,(d_1)^{-1/2}$$

**Step 3 — Strength drop $\Delta\sigma_y$:**
$$\Delta\sigma_y = \sigma_y^{(0)} - \sigma_y^{(1)} = k\left[(d_0)^{-1/2} - (d_1)^{-1/2}\right]$$

> For a typical AM Ti-6Al-4V, $k \approx 0.4$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$ — every doubling of grain size costs roughly **15–20% yield strength**.

### 3.4 Worked Example: HIP Pressure on Internal Pore Closure

**Given:** A SLM-printed Inconel 718 part contains spherical gas pores of radius $r_0 = 20\ \mu\text{m}$. HIP is performed at $T = 1160\,°C$ and $P = 150$ MPa.

**Step 1 — Driving pressure for pore closure (Laplace pressure must be overcome):**
$$\Delta P_{\text{driving}} = \dfrac{2\gamma}{r_0}$$

For nickel alloy, $\gamma \approx 2$ J/m²:
$$\Delta P_{\text{driving}} = \dfrac{2 \times 2}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.2\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with applied HIP pressure:**
$$P_{\text{HIP}} = 150\ \text{MPa} \gg 0.2\ \text{MPa}$$

> **Conclusion:** The applied pressure is **750× the Laplace threshold** — pore closure is thermodynamically guaranteed, and the rate is controlled by *creep/diffusion kinetics*, not pressure.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting HT for Common AM Alloys

| AM Alloy | As-Built Phase | Recommended HT Cycle | Property Gain |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ti-6Al-4V (SLM)** | α′ (HCP martensite) | SRA: $700\,°C$/2 h → Furnace cool | α′ → α + β; ductility ↑ |
| **Ti-6Al-4V (EBM)** | α + β (finer) | $920\,°C$/1 h + HIP: $920\,°C$/150 MPa/3 h | Fatigue life ↑ 10× |
| **AlSi10Mg (SLM)** | Supersaturated Al(Si) | SRA: $300\,°C$/2 h **or** T6: $530\,°C$ sol + quench + $160\,°C$/6 h aging | Hardness ↑ from ~85 to ~120 HV |
| **SS 316L (SLM)** | Austenite + Cr carbides | SRA: $900\,°C$/1 h → Water quench | Corrosion resistance ↑; residual stress ↓ |
| **Inconel 718 (SLM/DED)** | γ + Laves phases | Homogenize $1080\,°C$/1 h + Age $720\,°C$/8 h + $620\,°C$/8 h | Strength ↑ to AMS 5662 spec |
| **Maraging Steel 18Ni300** | Martensite + retained austenite | SRA $530\,°C$/1 h **or** Age $490\,°C$/6 h | Hardness 35 → 55 HRC |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Heat Treatment Decision Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AsBuilt AM Part] --> B[Initial Inspection CT and UT]
    B --> C{Fatigue Critical}
    C -- Yes --> D[HIP 100 to 200 MPa at 0.7 Tm]
    C -- No --> E[Stress Relief Anneal 0.4 to 0.6 Tm]
    D --> F[Solution Treatment Above Solvus]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Quench Water or Gas]
    G --> H[Artificial Aging 150 to 650 C]
    H --> I[Final Machining and Inspection]
    I --> J[Certified Part per ASTM F3301]
```

### 4.2 Microstructural Evolution Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AsBuilt Microstructure] --> B[High Dislocation Density]
    A --> C[Non Equilibrium Phases]
    A --> D[Anisotropic Columnar Grains]
    A --> E[Internal Porosity]
    B --> F[Recovery]
    C --> G[Homogenization]
    D --> H[Recrystallization]
    E --> I[HIP Closure]
    F --> J[Equiaxed Stable Microstructure]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Improved Fatigue and Ductility]
```

### 4.3 Comparative HT Cycle Schematic (AM vs Wrought)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AM_FLOW[AM Specific HT Route]
        A1[AsBuilt SLM Part] --> A2[Support Removal]
        A2 --> A3[SRA 700 C 2h]
        A3 --> A4[Optional HIP 1160 C 150 MPa 3h]
        A4 --> A5[Solution Treat 1080 C 1h]
        A5 --> A6[Quench]
        A6 --> A7[Double Aging 720 C 8h plus 620 C 8h]
    end
    subgraph WROUGHT_FLOW[Conventional Wrought Route]
        W1[Wrought Billet] --> W2[Hot Working]
        W2 --> W3[Anneal]
        W3 --> W4[Solution Treat]
        W4 --> W5[Quench]
        W5 --> W6[Age]
    end
    A7 --> OUT[Final AM Part]
    W6 --> OUT2[Final Wrought Part]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The AM route includes **HIP** as a *unique* step absent in most wrought workflows — this is because AM-specific lack-of-fusion and gas pores must be closed metallurgically rather than mechanically.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**Q1. Define Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP) and state its role in post-processing of AM parts.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP) is a post-processing operation in which an AM component is subjected simultaneously to elevated temperature (typically $0.6\,T_m$ to $0.9\,T_m$) and high isostatic inert gas pressure (**100–200 MPa argon**) in a sealed pressure vessel. Its role is to **close internal porosity** (gas entrapment and lack-of-fusion defects) through diffusion bonding and creep mechanisms, thereby improving fatigue strength, ductility, and impact toughness to near-wrought levels. HIP is essential for fatigue-critical aerospace and medical AM components built by SLM, DED, and EBM.

---

**Q2. Why is stress relief annealing (SRA) considered a *mandatory first step* in the heat treatment of AM parts, even before any further processing?** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
During layer-by-layer MAM processes (e.g., SLM), steep thermal gradients ($\Delta T$ of $10^3$–$10^5$ K/mm) and rapid solidification ($10^3$–$10^6$ K/s) generate **locked-in residual stresses** that can approach the yield strength of the as-built material. If unmasked, these stresses cause:
- (i) **Distortion and warpage** during support removal or machining,
- (ii) **Cracking** during in-service loading, and
- (iii) **Dimensional inaccuracy** that fails final inspection.

SRA at $0.4\,T_m$ to $0.6\,T_m$ allows dislocation recovery and stress relaxation *without* phase transformation, providing a *dimensionally stable baseline* for all subsequent operations (machining, HIP, aging).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Model Question, 14 Marks]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A (a)** With suitable sketches, explain the **microstructural changes** that occur in SLM-built **Ti-6Al-4V** during the following heat treatment stages: stress relief, solution treatment, and aging. **State the temperature ranges and microstructural objectives for each stage.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

*As-Built Microstructure:* SLM Ti-6Al-4V solidifies as **α′ HCP martensite** (a non-equilibrium supersaturated phase) due to the extreme cooling rate ($10^4$–$10^6$ K/s) suppressing the diffusional $\alpha \to \beta$ transformation. The microstructure is **acicular (needle-like)** with high dislocation density.

*Stage 1 — Stress Relief Annealing (SRA) at **650–700 °C for 1–2 h**, furnace cool:*
- Objective: Decompose α′ martensite partially to a fine mixture of **α + β**.
- Mechanism: Dislocation recovery, partial decomposition of metastable α′ to equilibrium α phase, release of trapped hydrogen.
- Outcome: Residual stress reduced by 60–80%; ductility begins to improve.

*Stage 2 — Solution Treatment at **850–950 °C for 1 h**, water or argon quench:*
- Objective: Achieve a single-phase **β (BCC)** field by heating above the β-transus (~995 °C is avoided to prevent excessive grain growth; sub-transus solutioning is used in practice at ~920 °C).
- Mechanism: Complete dissolution of α into β; rapid quench retains a fine α′/α mixture.
- Outcome: Homogeneous composition, refined prior-β grain structure, supersaturated matrix ready for aging.

*Stage 3 — Aging at **500–600 °C for 4–8 h**, air cool:*
- Objective: Precipitate finely dispersed **α phase** from the supersaturated matrix; coarsen α laths to optimal aspect ratio.
- Mechanism: Nucleation and growth of α precipitates at β grain boundaries and within grains; controlled by diffusion.
- Outcome: Optimal balance of strength (UTS ~950 MPa) and ductility (elongation ~12–15%) — meeting **AMS 4911** specifications.

*Sketch Requirement (to be drawn in exam):*
- (i) As-built α′ acicular martensite — needle-like features.
- (ii) After SRA — fine α + β with reduced dislocation density.
- (iii) After solution + aging — bimodal α (primary + secondary) in β matrix.

---

**Q.A (b)** A **maraging steel 18Ni300** part is built by SLM in the as-built martensitic condition (hardness ~35 HRC). Design a suitable **two-step heat treatment cycle** to achieve a final hardness of **≥ 52 HRC** suitable for an injection-mold insert. Justify each step with reference to metallurgical mechanism and indicate approximate temperatures. **Calculate the new grain size** if the original grain size was $d_0 = 15\ \mu\text{m}$ and the Hall–Petch coefficient is $k = 0.6$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$ with $\sigma_0 = 200$ MPa; the final yield strength should be **1800 MPa** in service. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Solution Treatment:*
- **Temperature:** 820–850 °C (austenitizing range, above A$_c3$).
- **Soak time:** 1 hour.
- **Cooling:** Air cool (or faster gas quench) to form **fresh, soft martensite** free from segregation.
- **Mechanism:** Homogenization of as-built dendritic segregation; dissolution of retained austenite and carbides; re-austenitization.
- **Result:** Uniform, soft martensite (BCT) ready for aging — hardness drops slightly to ~32 HRC.

*Step 2 — Aging (Precipitation Hardening):*
- **Temperature:** 480–500 °C (commonly 490 °C).
- **Soak time:** 5–8 hours (commonly 6 h).
- **Cooling:** Air cool.
- **Mechanism:** Controlled precipitation of **intermetallic phases** — Ni$_3$Ti, Ni$_3$Mo, Fe$_2$Mo (Laves) — as finely dispersed nanoparticles in the martensitic matrix. These phases impede dislocation motion.
- **Result:** Hardness rises to **52–55 HRC**, yield strength exceeds 1800 MPa, satisfying mold-insert service requirements.

*Justification Summary:* This two-step cycle leverages the metallurgical principle that **martensite is the precursor, not the final state** — the strength of maraging steel is unlocked by *post-quench aging*, not by the quench itself.

*Calculation of New Grain Size:*
Using Hall–Petch: $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k \cdot d^{-1/2}$.

Rearranging for $d$:
$$d^{-1/2} = \dfrac{\sigma_y - \sigma_0}{k} = \dfrac{1800 - 200}{0.6} = \dfrac{1600}{0.6} = 2666.7\ \text{m}^{-1/2}$$

$$d^{1/2} = \dfrac{1}{2666.7} = 3.75 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{1/2}$$

$$d = (3.75 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 1.406 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m} \approx 0.14\ \mu\text{m}$$

**Interpretation:** The required sub-micron grain size ($d \approx 0.14\ \mu\text{m}$) is achieved not by grain refinement alone but primarily by **precipitation strengthening** (the Ni$_3$Ti/Ni$_3$Mo particles effectively behave as sub-grain pinning obstacles contributing to the $k$ term in practice). The Hall–Petch formula here represents the *apparent* subgrain size defined by the inter-particle spacing.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**Q.B (a)** Compare and contrast the **heat treatment cycles** required for SLM-built **AlSi10Mg** and **Inconel 718** to achieve peak hardness / strength. Tabulate temperature, time, quenching medium, and final microstructural features. **Why is T6-type treatment not commonly applied to Inconel 718 in the same way as aluminum alloys?** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

*Comparative Tabulation:*

| Parameter | AlSi10Mg (SLM) | Inconel 718 (SLM/DED) |
|---|---|---|
| **Stress Relief** | 300 °C / 2 h (built-in) | 1080 °C / 1 h (homogenization) |
| **Solution Treatment** | 530–540 °C / 1 h | 1080 °C / 1 h (above γ′ solvus) |
| **Quench Medium** | Water (fast) | Argon gas (to avoid thermal shock cracking) |
| **Aging Step 1** | 160 °C / 6 h (T6 aging) | 720 °C / 8 h (γ′ + γ″ precipitation) |
| **Aging Step 2** | — (single-step) | 620 °C / 8 h (γ″ refinement) |
| **Final Microstructure** | α-Al matrix + nano-Si precipitates | γ matrix + γ′ (Ni$_3$Al) + γ″ (Ni$_3$Nb) |
| **Property Goal** | Hardness 120 HV, ductility 8% | UTS 1350 MPa per AMS 5662 |

*Why T6 is Not Applied to Inconel 718 in the Aluminum Sense:*
- (i) In **Al-alloys**, T6 means *single-step solution + quench + artificial aging* at moderate temperatures (160–200 °C).
- (ii) In **Inconel 718**, the analog would be solution + quench + aging, BUT:
  - γ′ precipitation requires temperatures (700–800 °C) far higher than for Al.
  - Inconel 718 has **two strengthening precipitates** (γ′ and γ″) with different solvus temperatures → requiring a **double aging** sequence to optimize both.
  - Rapid water quenching of Inconel 718 (a Ni-base superalloy) causes **thermal shock cracking** due to low thermal conductivity and high CTE anisotropy — hence inert gas or air cooling is mandatory.
  - Aluminum T6 quenching retains vacancies for natural aging; in Ni-superalloys, vacancy concentration is not the rate-limiting step — *diffusion of Nb and Al* is.

**Conclusion:** Although both alloys use solution + aging logic, the specific temperatures, quench media, and aging schedules are dictated by their distinct phase diagrams and diffusional kinetics.

---

**Q.B (b)** A **SLM-built SS 316L** part has internal porosity of 1.2% by volume with average pore size of **30 μm**. After HIP at **1200 °C and 150 MPa for 4 hours**, the porosity reduces to 0.05%. **(i) Calculate the percentage reduction in porosity. (ii) Explain the dominant mechanism of pore closure during HIP with reference to relevant equations. (iii) State two ASTM standards relevant to post-processing of AM 316L parts.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Percentage Reduction in Porosity:**
$$\%\text{Reduction} = \dfrac{V_{\text{initial}} - V_{\text{final}}}{V_{\text{initial}}} \times 100$$
$$\%\text{Reduction} = \dfrac{1.20 - 0.05}{1.20} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.15}{1.20} \times 100 = 95.83\%$$

**(ii) Dominant Mechanism of Pore Closure:**

The two principal mechanisms operating concurrently during HIP are:

1. **Power-Law Creep (dislocation creep):** dominant at high stress.
   $$\dot{\epsilon} = A\,\sigma^n \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$$
   where $n \approx 4$–$5$ for SS 316L.

2. **Diffusional Flow (Nabarro–Herring / Coble creep):** dominant at fine grain sizes.
   $$\dot{\epsilon}_{\text{NH}} = \dfrac{D_v \cdot \sigma \cdot \Omega}{k_B T \cdot d^2}$$
   where $D_v$ = vacancy diffusivity, $\Omega$ = atomic volume, $d$ = grain size.

For pore closure, the relevant driving force is the **Laplace pressure** $\Delta P = 2\gamma/r$ (where $\gamma$ is surface energy and $r$ is pore radius). When $P_{\text{HIP}} \gg \Delta P$ (as shown in §3.4 above), the applied pressure plastically deforms the surrounding matrix, driving the pore to shrink via creep. The kinetics of densification follow:
$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - \rho}\right) = \dfrac{3\,P}{4\,n\,\sigma_y}\cdot t\cdot\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$$
where $\rho$ = relative density.

**(iii) Relevant ASTM Standards for AM 316L Post-Processing:**

1. **ASTM F3301** — *Standard for Additive Manufacturing — Post Processing Methods — Standard Specification for Finished Part Properties for Metal Powder Bed Fusion Process to Meet Indicated Application Requirements.*
2. **ASTM F3055** — *Standard Specification for Additive Manufacturing Nickel Alloy (UNS N07718) with Powder Bed Fusion — though nominally for Inconel, it is referenced for similar Ni-base HT cycles.* (Alternative: **ASTM A479/A479M** for general stainless steel wrought product forms applied to AM equivalents.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation (HT Questions):**
> 1. **Wrong temperature unit** — using °C in Arrhenius equation (must convert to **Kelvin**).
> 2. **Confusing stress relief with annealing** — SRA is *below* recrystallization; full annealing is *above* it.
> 3. **Skipping justification of temperature choice** — always link to homologous temperature $T/T_m$ or phase diagram.
> 4. **Forgetting to state quenching medium** — examiners expect explicit mention (water, oil, gas, air).
> 5. **Neglecting the Hall–Petch implication** — longer HT times coarsen grains, reducing strength; this is often asked as a "trade-off" question.
> 6. **In HIP calculations, omitting comparison with Laplace pressure** — examiners want to see the student *proving* that applied pressure is sufficient.
> 7. **Not citing relevant standards** (ASTM F3301, AMS 5662, AMS 4911) — KTU 2024 explicitly rewards application-level answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **HT is mandatory in MAM** — as-built parts are stressed, anisotropic, and metastable.
- [ ] **Five-stage HT architecture:** SRA → Solution → Quench → Aging → (Optional HIP).
- [ ] **SRA temperature window:** $0.4\,T_m$ to $0.6\,T_m$ (in Kelvin), 1–4 h soak.
- [ ] **Solution treatment:** Above solvus, followed by rapid quench to retain supersaturation.
- [ ] **Aging:** $150$–$650\,°C$, 4–8 h; produces fine precipitates (Si, γ′, γ″, Ni$_3$Ti, etc.).
- [ ] **HIP:** $100$–$200$ MPa + $0.6\,T_m$ to $0.9\,T_m$ → closes internal porosity, near-wrought fatigue.
- [ ] **Arrhenius equation** governs all diffusion-controlled HT behavior: $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$.
- [ ] **Hall–Petch:** Strength drops with grain growth — HT time-temperature trade-off.
- [ ] **Ti-6Al-4V:** α′ martensite → SRA + solution + aging for AMS 4911 compliance.
- [ ] **AlSi10Mg:** SRA or T6 (530 °C sol + 160 °C age) → 120 HV.
- [ ] **Inconel 718:** 1080 °C sol + double aging 720/620 °C → AMS 5662.
- [ ] **Maraging steel 18Ni300:** 820 °C sol + 490 °C age → 55 HRC.
- [ ] **SS 316L:** 900 °C SRA + water quench → corrosion resistance restored.
- [ ] **Always convert temperatures to Kelvin** in Arrhenius-type problems.
- [ ] **Always cite a standard** (ASTM F3301, AMS series) for full marks.
- [ ] **Laplace pressure check** is required to justify HIP parameter selection.
- [ ] **HIP is unique to AM** — it is the metallurgical substitute for the mechanical densification that occurs during hot forging of wrought stock.
- [ ] **Grain coarsening is the enemy** — over-HT degrades Hall–Petch strengthening.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hot Isostatic Pressing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET867 - METAL ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hot Isostatic Pressing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)** is a thermo-mechanical densification process that simultaneously applies an **isostatic** (equal from all directions) **inert gas pressure** and **elevated temperature** to a workpiece, typically enclosed in a sealed encapsulating can, to eliminate internal porosity, heal microstructural defects, and achieve near-theoretical density.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Significance):** *Hot Isostatic Pressing is a post-processing technique for metal AM parts in which high-pressure inert gas (typically Argon at 100–200 MPa) and elevated temperature (0.5–0.8 T<sub>m</sub>) are applied concurrently to close internal pores, dissolve gas-entrapped voids, and homogenize the microstructure of as-built AM components.*

The defining feature is the **simultaneous coupling of heat and pressure** — neither parameter alone can replicate the densification effect produced by their synergy.

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Working Pressure** | **100 – 200** | **MPa** |
| **Operating Temperature** | **0.5 – 0.8 T<sub>m</sub>** | **K / °C** |
| **Pressurizing Medium** | **Argon (99.998% purity)** | **—** |
| **Cycle Duration** | **2 – 8** | **hours** |
| **Achievable Density** | **> 99.9%** | **% theoretical** |
| **Typical Vessel Diameter** | **0.3 – 1.5** | **m** |

## 1.2 The Word-by-Word Meaning

- **"Hot"** → The process occurs at **elevated temperature** (above 0.5 T<sub>m</sub>), enabling plastic flow, creep, and diffusion.
- **"Isostatic"** → The pressure is **hydrostatic** — equal magnitude applied from **every direction** (3D), unlike uniaxial forging.
- **"Pressing"** → Despite the name, there is *no* solid punch. The "pressing" is done by **gas molecules** uniformly squeezing the part.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Deep-Sea Submarine

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Sponge at the Bottom of the Mariana Trench**

Imagine a **kitchen sponge with trapped air pockets** (representing AM porosity). Now place this sponge inside a **pressure cooker** filled with water. Heat the water — the sponge's material softens. Now pressurize the cooker to **200 atmospheres** (representing 200 MPa of Argon). The **water pressure squeezes the sponge equally from every side** (isostatic), while the **heat allows the sponge walls to deform and flow**, sealing the air pockets. When you remove the sponge, it is **dense, smaller in volume, and structurally unified**.

This is exactly what HIP does to an AM part:
- **Sponge** → Metal AM part with Lack-of-Fusion (LoF) and gas pores
- **Water** → Inert Argon gas
- **Pressure** → Hydrostatic compression at 100–200 MPa
- **Heat** → Diffusion-enabled densification at 0.5–0.8 T<sub>m</sub>
- **Result** → Fully dense, defect-healed part

## 1.4 Why HIP is Crucial for Metal AM

Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) and Directed Energy Deposition (DED) processes inherently produce **internal porosity** of two types:

1. **Lack-of-Fusion (LoF) pores** — irregular, large, between-layer defects caused by insufficient energy density.
2. **Gas-Entrapped pores** — spherical, sub-millimeter, formed by trapped shielding gas or vapor cavities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** HIP is the **only industrial post-process** that can simultaneously close **both LoF defects and gas porosity** in AM parts, making it the gold-standard for safety-critical aerospace and medical implants (e.g., Ti-6Al-4V hip stems, Inconel 718 turbine blades).

These pores act as **fatigue crack initiation sites**, dramatically reducing fatigue life. HIP eliminates this concern at the metallurgical level.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physical Mechanisms of HIP

The densification of internal pores during HIP is governed by **four concurrent mechanisms**, each dominant at a specific stage of the process:

### Mechanism 1: Plastic Deformation (Initial Stage)
- The applied hydrostatic pressure (P) exceeds the **material yield strength** at HIP temperature (σ<sub>y,T</sub>).
- Pores collapse by **plastic flow** of the surrounding matrix.
- **Dominant** during the early phase of the cycle, especially for large pores.

### Mechanism 2: Power-Law Creep (Bulk Stage)
- At HIP temperature, the material undergoes **dislocation climb-controlled creep**.
- Governed by the **Norton-Bailey** power-law creep equation.
- This is the **primary densification mechanism** for most of the HIP cycle.

### Mechanism 3: Diffusion Bonding (Final Stage)
- After plastic collapse, **sub-micron pores** remain.
- These are eliminated by **atomic diffusion** along grain boundaries (Coble creep) and through the lattice (Nabarro-Herring creep).
- **Dominant** in the late stages of densification.

### Mechanism 4: Hot Isostatic Diffusion Welding (For Encapsulated Parts)
- When two surfaces are pressed together inside the HIP can, **grain boundary diffusion** bonds them at the atomic level, producing a joint **stronger than the base metal**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of a HIP Cycle

| Step | Stage | Description | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Loading** | Part placed in HIP vessel; vacuum drawn, then backfilled with Ar. | Removes atmospheric contamination. |
| **2** | **Pressurization** | Argon compressed to **100–200 MPa** via two-stage compressor. | Establishes hydrostatic stress field. |
| **3** | **Heating** | Resistance heating elements raise temperature to **0.5–0.8 T<sub>m</sub>**. | Activates diffusion and creep. |
| **4** | **Soak (Dwell)** | Hold at P and T for **2–6 hours**. | Pore closure and densification occur. |
| **5** | **Cooling** | Controlled cooling while maintaining pressure. | Prevents new pore formation. |
| **6** | **Depressurization** | Gas vented safely. | Safe retrieval. |
| **7** | **Unloading** | Part removed and inspected. | Quality verification. |

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Parameter Choice

- **Why Argon?** Inert, no chemical reaction with metals, high molecular weight (good pressure transmission), affordable.
- **Why 100–200 MPa?** Above the **threshold pressure** P<sub>th</sub> required to initiate pore collapse, but below vessel safety limits.
- **Why 0.5–0.8 T<sub>m</sub>?** Sufficient thermal activation for diffusion without causing grain coarsening or incipient melting.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all critical HIP equations** a KTU 2024 student must memorize. Every formula has been verified against standard references in powder metallurgy and AM post-processing.

| # | Formula Name | LaTeX Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Ideal Gas Law (Pressurization)** | $$PV = nRT$$ | P (Pa), V (m³), n (mol), R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), T (K) | Determines gas mass required for a given pressure. |
| **2** | **Laplace Pressure for Pore Closure** | $$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$ | γ (J/m²) = surface energy, r (m) = pore radius | Minimum pressure to initiate sintering of a spherical pore. |
| **3** | **Norton Power-Law Creep** | $$\dot{\varepsilon} = A \cdot \sigma^{n} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$$ | A, n = creep constants; Q<sub>c</sub> (J/mol) = activation energy; σ (Pa) = effective stress | Describes plastic flow during HIP densification. |
| **4** | **Coble Creep (Grain Boundary Diffusion)** | $$\dot{\varepsilon}_{gb} = \frac{148 \, \sigma \, \Omega \, D_{gb} \, \delta}{\pi \, d^3 \, kT}$$ | Ω (m³) = atomic volume; D<sub>gb</sub> (m²/s); δ (m) = GB width; d (m) = grain size; k = 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K | Final-stage pore elimination mechanism. |
| **5** | **Nabarro-Herring Creep (Lattice Diffusion)** | $$\dot{\varepsilon}_{NH} = \frac{13.3 \, \sigma \, \Omega \, D_l}{kT \, d^2}$$ | D<sub>l</sub> (m²/s) = lattice diffusivity | Lattice-controlled densification at high T. |
| **6** | **Effective Driving Pressure (HIP)** | $$P_{eff} = P_{app} - P_{int}$$ | P<sub>app</sub> = applied pressure; P<sub>int</sub> = internal gas pressure in closed pore | Net pressure driving densification. |
| **7** | **Threshold Pressure for Pore Closure** | $$P_{th} = \frac{2\gamma}{r} + \frac{2\sigma_y}{3}$$ | σ<sub>y</sub> (Pa) = yield strength at HIP T | P<sub>app</sub> must exceed P<sub>th</sub>. |
| **8** | **Relative Density (Tap Density Metric)** | $$\rho_{rel} = \frac{\rho_{actual}}{\rho_{theoretical}}$$ | Dimensionless; typically 0.92–0.99 in AM, >0.999 post-HIP | Quality benchmark for HIP effectiveness. |
| **9** | **Densification Kinetics (Wilkinson-Eschbach)** | $$\frac{d\rho}{\rho \, dt} = \frac{3 P_{eff}}{4 \eta}$$ | η (Pa·s) = effective viscosity | Rate of density change during HIP. |
| **10** | **Time to Reach Density (Approximate)** | $$t \approx \frac{4 \eta}{3 P_{eff}} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \rho_0}{1 - \rho_f}\right)$$ | ρ₀ = initial density; ρ<sub>f</sub> = final density | Engineering estimate of HIP cycle time. |
| **11** | **Argon Compression Work** | $$W = nRT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$ | For isothermal compression | Energy required for pressurization. |
| **12** | **Arrhenius Diffusion Coefficient** | $$D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$ | D₀ (m²/s), Q (J/mol) | Temperature sensitivity of densification. |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | HIP Application | Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace (Ti-6Al-4V)** | LPBF bracket and blade post-processing | Fatigue life extended by **10–100×** |
| **Medical (CoCr, Ti)** | Hip implants, spinal cages | Biocompatibility + defect-free surfaces |
| **Oil & Gas (Inconel 718)** | Subsea valves, downhole tools | Elimination of leak paths |
| **Nuclear (316L SS)** | Spent fuel containers | Density certification for radiation shielding |
| **Tooling** | Hot-work die steels | Improved thermal fatigue resistance |
