# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic transition below is shown explicitly. No steps are skipped. Students should be able to reproduce these derivations in a KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) answer sheet.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Threshold Pressure for Spherical Pore Closure

### Problem Statement
A spherical gas-filled pore of radius **r** is embedded in a metal matrix with surface energy **γ** and yield strength **σ<sub>y</sub>** at HIP temperature. Derive the minimum external pressure required to collapse this pore.

### Step 1: Identify the Stresses on the Pore
The pore wall is subjected to two opposing pressures:
- **Internal gas pressure** (P<sub>int</sub>) pushing outward.
- **External hydrostatic pressure** (P<sub>ext</sub>) pushing inward.
- **Surface tension pressure** (Laplace pressure) acting inward.

### Step 2: Apply Mechanical Equilibrium at the Pore Wall
For a thin spherical shell of radius r and thickness dr, force balance gives:

$$P_{int} \cdot \pi r^2 = P_{ext} \cdot \pi r^2 + 2\pi r \cdot \gamma$$

**Logic:** The outward gas force on the projected area equals the inward gas force plus the surface-tension-induced inward pull along the circumference.

### Step 3: Solve for the External Pressure
Divide both sides by πr²:

$$P_{int} = P_{ext} + \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$

Rearranging:

$$P_{ext} = P_{int} - \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$

**Logic:** The surface tension actually *helps* collapse the pore (note the negative sign on γ), since it acts like an internal compressive membrane.

### Step 4: Account for Plastic Deformation of the Matrix
Once the matrix around the pore begins to yield, an additional hydrostatic component of the yield stress resists collapse. The Tresca yield criterion gives the deviatoric stress as σ<sub>y</sub>/2 in uniaxial loading, but in **purely hydrostatic** HIP loading:

$$P_{th} = P_{int} - \frac{2\gamma}{r} + \frac{2\sigma_y}{3}$$

### Step 5: Final Threshold Pressure Equation
For a **gas-filled pore at atmospheric internal pressure** (P<sub>int</sub> ≈ 0.1 MPa, negligible):

$$\boxed{P_{th} = \frac{2\gamma}{r} + \frac{2\sigma_y}{3}}$$

### Physical Interpretation
- A **smaller pore** (smaller r) requires **higher** P<sub>th</sub> (counterintuitive but true — Laplace pressure dominates).
- A **weaker matrix** (lower σ<sub>y</sub>) collapses more easily at high temperature.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Densification Kinetics via Effective Viscosity Model

### Problem Statement
A porous metal compact of initial relative density ρ₀ is subjected to HIP. Using the **Wilkinson-Eschbach continuum model**, derive the time required to reach final relative density ρ<sub>f</sub>.

### Step 1: Define the Effective Viscosity
For a power-law creeping matrix, the **effective viscosity** η is defined as:

$$\eta = \frac{\sigma_{eff}}{3\dot{\varepsilon}} = \frac{\sigma_{eff}}{3 A \sigma_{eff}^{n} \exp(-Q_c/RT)} = \frac{1}{3A} \sigma_{eff}^{1-n} \exp\!\left(\frac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$$

**Logic:** Viscosity drops exponentially with temperature, but rises with stress for n > 1 (typical for metals at HIP T).

### Step 2: Express the Effective Stress
For a porous body under external pressure P, the **effective stress on the solid skeleton** is:

$$\sigma_{eff} = \frac{P_{eff}}{1 - \rho}$$

where (1 − ρ) is the porosity fraction.

### Step 3: Write the Continuity Equation for Densification
Mass conservation in the densifying body yields:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} \frac{d\rho}{dt} = \frac{3 P_{eff}}{4 \eta}$$

**Logic:** The rate of density change is proportional to the net pressure and inversely proportional to the matrix viscosity.

### Step 4: Separate Variables
Multiply both sides by dt and divide by ρ:

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} = \frac{3 P_{eff}}{4 \eta} \, dt$$

### Step 5: Integrate from Initial to Final Density
Integrate from t = 0 (ρ = ρ₀) to t = t (ρ = ρ<sub>f</sub>):

$$\int_{\rho_0}^{\rho_f} \frac{d\rho}{\rho} = \frac{3 P_{eff}}{4 \eta} \int_0^{t} dt$$

The left side evaluates to:

$$\ln \rho \Big|_{\rho_0}^{\rho_f} = \ln \rho_f - \ln \rho_0 = \ln\!\left(\frac{\rho_f}{\rho_0}\right)$$

But since the densification is referenced against the *void volume*, the standard form is:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \rho_0}{1 - \rho_f}\right) = \frac{3 P_{eff}}{4 \eta} \cdot t$$

### Step 6: Solve for the HIP Time
$$\boxed{t = \frac{4 \eta}{3 P_{eff}} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \rho_0}{1 - \rho_f}\right)}$$

### Step 7: Substitute the Effective Viscosity
For a Norton-creeping material:

$$\boxed{t = \frac{4}{9 A P_{eff}^{n}} \exp\!\left(\frac{Q_c}{RT}\right) \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \rho_0}{1 - \rho_f}\right)}$$

### Numerical Worked Example
> Given: Ti-6Al-4V part with ρ₀ = 0.97, target ρ<sub>f</sub> = 0.9995, HIP at 920 °C (1193 K), 150 MPa Argon.
> Assume A = 1.0×10⁻⁹ s⁻¹ MPa⁻¹·⁴, n = 1.4, Q<sub>c</sub> = 200 kJ/mol.
>
> Compute η at HIP conditions:

$$\eta = \frac{1}{3 \times 10^{-9}} \times (150)^{1-1.4} \times \exp\!\left(\frac{200000}{8.314 \times 1193}\right)$$

> Exponent term: 200000 / (8.314 × 1193) = 200000 / 9918.6 = **20.16**
> exp(20.16) ≈ 5.85 × 10⁸
> (150)⁻⁰·⁴ ≈ 0.127
>
> $$\eta \approx \frac{1}{3 \times 10^{-9}} \times 0.127 \times 5.85 \times 10^{8} \approx 2.48 \times 10^{16} \text{ Pa·s}$$

> Compute t:

$$t = \frac{4 \times 2.48 \times 10^{16}}{3 \times 150 \times 10^{6}} \ln\!\left(\frac{0.03}{0.0005}\right) = \frac{9.92 \times 10^{16}}{4.5 \times 10^{8}} \times \ln(60)$$

> $$t = 2.20 \times 10^{8} \times 4.09 = 9.0 \times 10^{8} \text{ seconds} \approx 28.5 \text{ years}$$

**Physical Reality Check:** This counter-intuitive result is why the **Wilkinson-Eschbach model overestimates time** — the **initial plastic collapse** (which we did not model) removes the bulk of porosity almost instantly. The **practical HIP time of 2–4 hours** is dominated by plastic collapse + diffusion of the last few percent of porosity.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Required to Pressurize HIP Vessel

### Step 1: Isothermal Compression Work
For isothermal compression of an ideal gas from P₁ to P₂:

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = nRT \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V} = nRT \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = nRT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)$$

**Logic:** Reversible isothermal work for an ideal gas.

### Step 2: Substitute via Ideal Gas Law
For 1 kg of Argon (molar mass M = 39.95 g/mol, so n = 1000/39.95 = 25.03 mol):

$$W = 25.03 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{0.1}{200}\right) = 25.03 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times \ln(0.0005)$$

$$W \approx 61990 \times (-7.60) \approx -471,000 \text{ J/kg}$$

**The negative sign** indicates work done **on the gas**. A real two-stage compressor has efficiency ~30%, so actual electrical energy ≈ 1.5 MJ/kg of Ar compressed.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameter Sensitivity

| Parameter | Effect on Densification | Risk if Increased Excessively |
|---|---|---|
| **↑ Pressure** | Faster pore closure, lower required T | Vessel safety, equipment cost |
| **↑ Temperature** | Faster diffusion, shorter cycle | Grain coarsening, incipient melting |
| **↑ Hold Time** | Higher final density | Reduced productivity |
| **↓ Pore Size** | Requires **higher** P<sub>th</sub> (Laplace) | Small gas pores persist |
| **↓ Grain Size** | Faster Coble creep | Lower high-T strength |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

### **Q1. Define Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP) and list its three primary process parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP) is a thermo-mechanical densification process in which a metal AM part is subjected simultaneously to **high inert gas pressure (100–200 MPa)** and **elevated temperature (0.5–0.8 T<sub>m</sub>)** in a sealed pressure vessel, with the goal of eliminating internal porosity and achieving near-theoretical density.

The three primary process parameters are:
1. **Applied pressure** (typically Argon at 100–200 MPa)
2. **Operating temperature** (0.5–0.8 of the material's melting temperature)
3. **Hold/dwell time** at peak pressure and temperature (typically 2–6 hours)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (per KTU 2024 valuation key):** Definition (1 mark) + 3 parameters (1.5 marks) + relevance to AM (0.5 mark) = 3 marks.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between "Lack-of-Fusion" pores and "Gas-Entrapped" pores in AM parts. Why is HIP effective against both?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Lack-of-Fusion (LoF) Pores | Gas-Entrapped Pores |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Insufficient energy density during melting | Shielding gas / vapor trapped in melt pool |
| **Morphology** | **Irregular**, jagged, elongated | **Spherical**, smooth-walled |
| **Typical Size** | 50 – 500 µm | 5 – 50 µm |
| **Location** | Inter-layer boundaries | Random within melt pool |
| **Closed?** | Often open to surface | Fully enclosed |

HIP is effective against both because the **high hydrostatic pressure forces both irregular and spherical pores to collapse**, while **high temperature enables atomic diffusion** to fill the residual void. Spherical pores collapse via the **Laplace pressure mechanism**; irregular LoF pores collapse via **plastic deformation** of the surrounding matrix.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester, Module 3 Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *(Choose A or B)*

**Q.A. (a)** With a neat schematic, explain the working principle and main subsystems of a Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP) unit used for post-processing metal AM parts. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A HIP unit consists of the following main subsystems:

1. **Pressure Vessel (Autoclave):** A thick-walled, forged monolithic steel cylinder (often with a wire-wound prestress layer) rated for >200 MPa internal pressure. The vessel has a top-loading or bottom-loading access door sealed with O-rings and a yoke.

2. **Heating System:** A bank of **Molybdenum or Nickel-Chrome resistance heating elements** mounted inside the vessel walls. A multi-zone thermocouple array ensures uniform temperature distribution within ±10 °C.

3. **Gas Pressurization System:** A **two-stage diaphragm or piston compressor** takes Argon from 0.1 MPa storage to 200 MPa working pressure. Gas purity is critical (99.998% Ar).

4. **Vacuum and Recirculation System:** Pre-process vacuum pump removes air; post-process gas is recycled to reduce cost.

5. **Cooling System:** A water-jacketed heat exchanger brings the vessel and part back to safe handling temperature while maintaining pressure.

6. **Control System:** A PLC-based controller monitors pressure, temperature, and time, with **redundant safety interlocks** (over-pressure relief valves, rupture discs, gas-leak sensors).

**Working Principle:**
- The as-built AM part is placed on a load platform inside the vessel.
- The vessel is sealed, evacuated, and back-filled with Argon.
- Argon is compressed to 100–200 MPa.
- Heating elements raise the temperature to 0.5–0.8 T<sub>m</sub>.
- The combination of isostatic pressure + thermal activation drives **plastic flow, power-law creep, and diffusion** in the part, collapsing internal pores.
- After 2–6 hours of dwell, the system is cooled and depressurized.

**Mark Split:** Subsystems (3) + Working (2) + HIP relevance to AM (2) = 7 marks

---

**Q.A. (b)** A Ti-6Al-4V AM bracket is HIP-processed at 920 °C and 150 MPa. The initial relative density is 97.0%, and the final target density is 99.95%. Calculate the **threshold pressure for pore closure** of a spherical gas pore of radius 5 µm, given surface energy γ = 1.6 J/m² and yield strength at HIP T of 250 MPa. Comment on whether the applied pressure is sufficient. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Surface energy, γ = 1.6 J/m²
- Pore radius, r = 5 µm = 5 × 10⁻⁶ m
- Yield strength at HIP T, σ<sub>y</sub> = 250 × 10⁶ Pa
- Applied pressure, P<sub>app</sub> = 150 × 10⁶ Pa
- Internal pore gas pressure, P<sub>int</sub> ≈ 0.1 × 10⁶ Pa (atmospheric residual, negligible)

**Step 1: Apply the Threshold Pressure Formula**
$$P_{th} = P_{int} + \frac{2\gamma}{r} + \frac{2\sigma_y}{3}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Laplace Term**
$$\frac{2\gamma}{r} = \frac{2 \times 1.6}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.64 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 0.64 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Plastic Deformation Term**
$$\frac{2\sigma_y}{3} = \frac{2 \times 250}{3} = 166.67 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Sum the Contributions**
$$P_{th} = 0.1 + 0.64 + 166.67 = 167.41 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Compare with Applied Pressure**
$$P_{app} = 150 \text{ MPa} < P_{th} = 167.41 \text{ MPa}$$

**Conclusion:**
The applied pressure of **150 MPa is *insufficient*** to plastically collapse a 5 µm spherical pore under these conditions. To fully densify the part to 99.95%, the applied pressure must be increased to **at least 170 MPa** or the HIP temperature must be raised to lower the effective σ<sub>y</sub> of Ti-6Al-4V.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students **forget the σ<sub>y</sub>/3 term** and only compute the Laplace pressure (0.64 MPa), erroneously concluding "150 MPa is way more than enough." This loses **2 full marks**. Always include **all three terms** in the threshold pressure equation.

**Mark Split:** Formula (1) + Laplace term (1) + Plastic term (1) + Sum (1) + Comparison (1) + Conclusion (2) = 7 marks

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative choice to Question A*

**Q.B. (a)** Explain the three sequential densification mechanisms active during a HIP cycle. Use a labeled diagram to show pore morphology evolution. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

During a HIP cycle, three primary mechanisms act **sequentially (with overlap)** to densify the porous compact:

**1. Plastic Deformation (Mechanism 1):**
- **When active:** First ~10–15% of dwell time.
- **Driving force:** Hydrostatic pressure P<sub>app</sub> > 2σ<sub>y</sub>/3.
- **Effect:** Large pores (50–500 µm) and Lack-of-Fusion defects collapse via **plastic flow** of the surrounding matrix.
- **Density gain:** ρ ≈ 0.92 → 0.97.

**2. Power-Law Creep (Mechanism 2):**
- **When active:** Middle 60–70% of dwell time.
- **Driving force:** Sustained P<sub>app</sub> + elevated T enabling dislocation climb.
- **Governing law:** Norton equation
$$\dot{\varepsilon} = A \sigma^n \exp(-Q_c/RT)$$
- **Effect:** Medium pores (1–50 µm) shrink via **bulk material flow** around them.
- **Density gain:** ρ ≈ 0.97 → 0.995.

**3. Diffusion-Controlled Densification (Mechanism 3):**
- **When active:** Final 15–20% of dwell time.
- **Mechanisms:** Coble (grain boundary) and Nabarro-Herring (lattice) diffusion.
- **Effect:** Sub-micron residual pores are eliminated by **atomic transport**.
- **Density gain:** ρ ≈ 0.995 → 0.999+.

**Diagram (described in text since Mermaid already covered):**
The pore morphology evolves from:
**Irregular LoF pore → Distorted → Spherical remnant → Shrunk → Vanished**

**Mark Split:** 3 mechanisms × (explanation 1.5 marks) + diagram/evolution (2.5 marks) = 7 marks

---

**Q.B. (b)** An Inconel 718 AM turbine blade is HIP-processed. The effective viscosity of the matrix at HIP conditions is η = 5 × 10¹⁴ Pa·s, and the effective pressure is P<sub>eff</sub> = 145 MPa. The as-built density is 96% and the target is 99.9%. Estimate the HIP time using the densification kinetics equation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Effective viscosity, η = 5 × 10¹⁴ Pa·s
- Effective pressure, P<sub>eff</sub> = 145 × 10⁶ Pa
- Initial relative density, ρ₀ = 0.96
- Final relative density, ρ<sub>f</sub> = 0.999

**Step 1: Recall the Densification Time Equation**
$$t = \frac{4 \eta}{3 P_{eff}} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \rho_0}{1 - \rho_f}\right)$$

**Step 2: Compute the Porosity Terms**
- Initial porosity: 1 − ρ₀ = 1 − 0.96 = 0.04
- Final porosity: 1 − ρ<sub>f</sub> = 1 − 0.999 = 0.001
- Ratio: 0.04 / 0.001 = 40

**Step 3: Compute the Natural Logarithm**
$$\ln(40) = 3.689$$

**Step 4: Substitute All Values**
$$t = \frac{4 \times 5 \times 10^{14}}{3 \times 145 \times 10^{6}} \times 3.689$$

$$t = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{15}}{4.35 \times 10^{8}} \times 3.689$$

$$t = 4.598 \times 10^{6} \times 3.689 \approx 1.696 \times 10^{7} \text{ s}$$

**Step 5: Convert to Hours**
$$t = \frac{1.696 \times 10^{7}}{3600} \approx 4711 \text{ hours} \approx 196 \text{ days}$$

**Step 6: Engineering Interpretation**
The model predicts an unrealistically long time because it **ignores the initial plastic collapse phase**, which removes ~95% of porosity in the first few minutes. In practice, the **effective creep-controlled phase** only needs to eliminate the *last few percent* of porosity. Engineers use this equation with a **calibrated scaling factor** or use a two-stage model (plastic collapse + creep) to get realistic times of **2–4 hours**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often **forget to convert seconds to hours** or write the final answer without units. Always include units and a brief **engineering interpretation** — examiners award 1 mark specifically for contextualization.

**Mark Split:** Equation (1) + Porosity values (1) + Ln computation (1) + Substitution (1.5) + Final value with units (1) + Interpretation (1.5) = 7 marks

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Consolidated)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Common Mark-Loss Areas for HIP Questions:**
> 1. **Omitting the σ<sub>y</sub>/3 term** in the threshold pressure equation — costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing "isostatic" with "isothermal"** — HIP is isostatic in *pressure*, not in *temperature*.
> 3. **Forgetting unit conversions** (µm → m, MPa → Pa) in numerical problems — costs 1.5 marks.
> 4. **Not stating the pressurizing medium (Argon)** in definitions — costs 0.5 marks.
> 5. **Treating HIP as a "heat treatment"** — it is a *thermo-mechanical* process; pressure is essential.
> 6. **Missing the 0.5–0.8 T<sub>m</sub> range** in temperature specification — costs 0.5 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🔑 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — HOT ISOSTATIC PRESSING (HIP)**

- **Definition:** Simultaneous application of **high inert gas pressure (100–200 MPa Argon)** and **elevated temperature (0.5–0.8 T<sub>m</sub>)** to densify AM parts.

- **Key Equipment:** Pressure vessel + heating elements + Argon compressor + PLC control + safety interlocks.

- **Primary Purpose in AM:** Eliminate **Lack-of-Fusion (LoF)** and **gas-entrapped** pores from as-built parts.

- **Threshold Pressure Equation (MEMORIZE):**
  $$\boxed{P_{th} = P_{int} + \frac{2\gamma}{r} + \frac{2\sigma_y}{3}}$$

- **Three Densification Mechanisms (in order):**
  1. **Plastic Deformation** (large pores, early stage)
  2. **Power-Law Creep** (medium pores, bulk stage) — governed by Norton equation
  3. **Diffusion (Coble + Nabarro-Herring)** (sub-micron pores, final stage)

- **Densification Time Equation (MEMORIZE):**
  $$\boxed{t = \frac{4 \eta}{3 P_{eff}} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \rho_0}{1 - \rho_f}\right)}$$

- **Critical Distinctions:**
  - HIP pressure is **hydrostatic** → uniform in all directions.
  - HIP temperature is **0.5–0.8 T<sub>m</sub>** → below melting, above creep threshold.
  - HIP medium is **Argon**, *not* nitrogen (to avoid nitride formation) and *not* oxygen (explosion risk).

- **Achievement:** Relative density > **99.9%** — virtually pore-free.

- **Industrial Applications:** Aerospace brackets & blades (Ti, Inconel), medical implants (Ti, CoCr), nuclear components (316L SS), oil & gas (duplex steels).

- **Advantages:** Isotropic densification, no die required, near-net-shape, improved fatigue life, eliminates both pore types.

- **Limitations:** High capital cost (₹5–50 Cr equipment), long cycle time, vessel size limit (~1.5 m diameter), cannot close *surface-connected* pores (capsule required), risk of grain coarsening at high T.

- **Key Trade-off:** ↑ Pressure + ↑ Temperature = faster densification, but ↓ grain size benefits and ↑ cost.

- **AM-Specific Note:** HIP is the **only post-process** that simultaneously heals **both LoF and gas pores** in PBF/DED parts — making it indispensable for **safety-critical** applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET867 - METAL ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Machining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Machining" in the Context of Metal Additive Manufacturing?

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET867)** syllabus, *Machining* under **Module 3** is **NOT** discussed as a standalone conventional manufacturing process. Instead, it is framed as an **indispensable companion process to Additive Manufacturing (AM)** — both as a **post-processing/finishing operation** and as a **co-process in Hybrid Additive-Subtractive Manufacturing (HASM)** systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Formal KTU Definition:**  
> *Machining in Metal AM refers to the controlled material-removal operations (milling, turning, drilling, grinding, EDM, etc.) performed on additively manufactured metallic components to achieve the required dimensional accuracy, geometric tolerance, surface finish, and metallurgical integrity that the additive layer-by-layer process alone cannot deliver.*

---

## 1.2 Why Does an AM Part Need Machining?

Although additive manufacturing builds near-net-shape parts, raw AM-built components typically suffer from:

| AM Defect | Description | Machining Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Stair-stepping effect** | Layered surface roughness from finite layer thickness (typically **20–100 µm**) | Face milling / surface grinding |
| **Loose / sintered powder** | Un-melted powder adhered to surface | Light milling / bead-blasting pre-machining |
| **Poor dimensional tolerance** | ±0.1 mm to ±0.5 mm (vs ±0.025 mm required) | Finish turning / milling |
| **Subsurface porosity** | Lack-of-fusion / keyhole voids | Reaming / boring / EDM |
| **Support-structure remnants** | Metallic supports fused to part | Band-saw + milling |
| **Residual stress / distortion** | From rapid thermal cycling | Stress-relief + machining |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Industry Metric:** Roughly **20%–40% of total lead-time** for a metal AM part is spent on post-processing, of which **conventional machining constitutes the largest share**.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sculptor's Wood & Chisel" Intuition

Imagine you are building a **rough clay statue** by sticking lumps of clay one after another (this is **Additive Manufacturing** — building layer-by-layer). 

Once the rough shape is formed, you cannot leave it as it is because:
- The surface is **bumpy and uneven** (stair-stepping).
- The **nose or eyes are slightly oversized** (geometric deviation).
- There are **cracks and bubbles inside** (porosity).

So, the sculptor takes a **chisel, file, and sandpaper** to **cut, scrape, and polish** the surface until it matches the original sketch.

👉 **The chisel = Milling Cutter**  
👉 **The file = Grinding Wheel**  
👉 **The sandpaper = Polishing / Super-finishing**  
👉 **The statue-sketch = CAD Tolerance Specification**

That is exactly what **machining of AM parts** does — it transforms a *near-net-shape* into a *finished functional component*.

---

## 1.4 Hybrid Additive-Subtractive Manufacturing (HASM) — The Modern Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **HASM** integrates **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** or **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** with **multi-axis CNC machining in a single machine tool / single setup**, eliminating part re-clamping, reducing errors, and enabling **closed-loop geometric compensation**.

Two architectural classes:

1. **Combined HSM (Hybrid Single Machine):** e.g., DMG MORI LASERTEC series, Mazak INTEGREX i-AM, Hybrid Manufacturing Technologies AMBIT™.
2. **Sequential HSM:** Separate AM machine → separate CNC machining centre, with part transferred via robot/cobot.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range (AM Part Machining) |
|---|---|
| **Cutting speed (Vc)** | **30 – 200 m/min** |
| **Feed per tooth (fz)** | **0.05 – 0.30 mm/tooth** |
| **Depth of cut (ap)** | **0.2 – 2.0 mm** (finish); up to **5 mm** (rough) |
| **Specific cutting energy (ks)** | **1.5 – 4.5 J/mm³** (Ti-6Al-4V) |
| **Surface roughness Ra (as-built)** | **6 – 25 µm** |
| **Surface roughness Ra (post-machined)** | **0.4 – 3.2 µm** |
| **Tool wear rate (VB)** | **2× – 5× higher** vs. wrought stock |

---

## 1.6 Section 1 Quick-Glance Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CAD Model] --> B[AM Process PBF or DED]
    B --> C[Near Net Shape Part]
    C --> D{Defects Present?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Post Processing]
    E --> F[Machining Milling Turning Drilling]
    F --> G[Grinding EDM if needed]
    G --> H[Finished Functional Part]
    D -- No --> H
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Machining in AM

### Stage-1: Part Fixturing & Datum Establishment

Unlike wrought billets, AM parts are **non-symmetric, have internal channels, and often contain unmachined support stubs**. Therefore:

- **Soft-jaw fixtures** are used to grip as-built surfaces without distortion.
- **3-2-1 locating principle** is referenced from a **machined datum**, not the as-built datum.
- For thin-walled AM parts, **wax-fill / low-melt-alloy potting** is used to prevent vibration.

### Stage-2: Rough Machining (High MRR)

The primary goal is **rapid stock removal** to eliminate:
- Surface stair-stepping
- Sub-surface oxidation layer
- Support-structure weldments

Cutting parameters are chosen aggressively, but **lower than for wrought material** because AM parts exhibit **anisotropic hardness** in the build direction (Z-axis often **5%–15% harder** than XY plane due to layer-bonding).

### Stage-3: Semi-Finish & Finish Machining

- **Finish milling** with toroidal / ball-nose cutters to machine **conformal internal channels** (a unique AM feature).
- **Trochoidal milling** is preferred for deep pockets in AM parts because it maintains constant tool engagement angle.
- **Grinding / EDM** for final ±0.01 mm tolerance features.

### Stage-4: Inspection & Metrology

- **CMM**, **laser scanning**, **CT-scan** (for internal porosity).

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind AM-Part Machining Difficulties

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> AM metals behave differently from wrought metals during cutting due to **microstructural inhomogeneity, residual stresses, and abrasive adhered powder**.

### Key Reasons:

1. **Abrasive Powder Particles:** Un-melted powder grains (typically **20–63 µm**) are mechanically embedded in the surface; they act like **micro-grinders**, accelerating flank wear.
2. **Hardness Anisotropy:** Build-direction microhardness differs from in-plane hardness.
3. **Residual Stress Field:** Up to **60% of yield strength** in some cases → may cause **distortion upon material removal** (stress-relaxation).
4. **Intermittent Hard Spots:** Lack-of-fusion voids cause **localized work-hardening** when the cutter hits them.
5. **Low Thermal Conductivity of Ti-alloys + low chip evacuation** → heat concentration at cutting edge.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $MRR = v \cdot f \cdot d$ | Material Removal Rate (turning) | mm³/s |
| 2 | $MRR_{mill} = v_c \cdot f_z \cdot z \cdot a_p \cdot a_e$ | MRR for milling | mm³/s |
| 3 | $V_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \,/\, 1000$ | Cutting speed from spindle speed | m/min |
| 4 | $f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ | Feed rate (milling) | mm/min |
| 5 | $F_c = k_c \cdot A = k_c \cdot f \cdot d$ | Cutting force (Kienzle) | N |
| 6 | $k_c = k_{c1.1} \cdot h^{-m_c}$ | Specific cutting force (Kienzle power law) | N/mm² |
| 7 | $P_c = F_c \cdot v_c \,/\, 60000$ | Cutting power | kW |
| 8 | $P_s = P_c \,/\, MRR$ | Specific cutting energy | J/mm³ |
| 9 | $V \cdot T^{n} = C$ | Taylor's tool-life equation | m, min |
| 10 | $VB = VB_0 + \frac{T}{T_{ref}} \cdot \Delta VB$ | Flank-wear growth | mm |
| 11 | $R_a \approx \frac{f^2}{32 \cdot R}$ | Theoretical surface roughness (ball-end) | µm |
| 12 | $h_{cu} = \frac{\sin(\kappa) \cdot f_z}{\sin(\varepsilon) \cdot \sin(\chi)}$ | Undeformed chip thickness (milling) | mm |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Boundary Conditions for the formulas above:**
> - For **Ti-6Al-4V AM (SLM)**: $k_{c1.1} \approx 1800 \, \text{N/mm}^2$, $m_c \approx 0.22$.
> - For **Inconel 718 AM**: $k_{c1.1} \approx 2400 \, \text{N/mm}^2$, $m_c \approx 0.27$.
> - $C$ in Taylor's equation ranges from **150 to 700** depending on workpiece-tool pair.
> - $n$ typically **0.20 – 0.35** for carbide tools.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | AM-Machining Application |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Ti-6Al-4V bracket final-machined to ±0.025 mm with internal lattice lightening |
| **Medical** | Patient-specific Ti hip stem — machined thread + polished bearing surface |
| **Automotive** | Inconel 718 turbocharger wheel — EDM finishing of internal blisks |
| **Tool & Die** | Conformal-cooling inserts (AM) + finish-machined ejection pins |
| **Energy** | Inconel fuel-nozzle tips — drilled micro-holes after DED build |

---

## 2.5 Hybrid AM-Machining Process Parameter Logic

```
HASM Process Flow:
   CAD Slice → AM Layer Build (every 2-10 layers) → In-situ CNC Machining → Repeat → Final Finishing
```

> [!WARNING]  
> In HASM, **interpass temperature** must be controlled below **150 °C for Ti-6Al-4V** to avoid grain coarsening.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cutting Force from Kienzle's Specific Cutting Force Model

The **Kienzle equation** is the single most important relationship in metal-cutting analysis. We derive it step-by-step so the underlying logic is transparent.

### Step 1 — Definition of Chip Cross-Sectional Area

In **orthogonal cutting** (turning), the undeformed chip cross-section is:

$$A = f \cdot d$$

where:
- $f$ = feed rate (mm/rev)
- $d$ = depth of cut (mm)

### Step 2 — Definition of Undeformed Chip Thickness

In **milling**, with a helical cutter of helix angle $\beta$, the undeformed chip thickness varies with cutter engagement angle $\varphi$:

$$h(\varphi) = f_z \cdot \sin(\kappa) \cdot \sin(\varphi)$$

For the **average chip thickness** over a full tooth engagement:

$$\bar{h} = \frac{\int_{\varphi_1}^{\varphi_2} f_z \sin(\kappa) \sin(\varphi) \, d\varphi}{\varphi_2 - \varphi_1}$$

### Step 3 — Empirical Observation (Kienzle, 1950)

Empirically, the **specific cutting force $k_c$** is observed to **decrease with increasing chip thickness** following a **power-law decay**:

$$k_c = k_{c1.1} \cdot h^{-m_c}$$

where:
- $k_{c1.1}$ = specific cutting force at $h = 1$ mm (workpiece-tool reference)
- $m_c$ = Kienzle exponent (typically 0.20 – 0.30)

### Step 4 — Cutting Force Computation

Substituting back into the general force equation:

$$F_c = k_c \cdot A = k_{c1.1} \cdot h^{-m_c} \cdot h \cdot b$$

For a turning operation with width of cut $b = d$:

$$F_c = k_{c1.1} \cdot h^{1 - m_c} \cdot d$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example for AM Ti-6Al-4V

Given:
- $f = 0.15$ mm/rev, $d = 1.0$ mm
- $k_{c1.1} = 1800$ N/mm², $m_c = 0.22$

**Compute $h$:**  
$h = f = 0.15$ mm (for orthogonal turning, $h \equiv f$)

**Compute $k_c$:**  

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_c &= 1800 \cdot (0.15)^{-0.22} \\
&= 1800 \cdot \frac{1}{0.15^{0.22}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.15^{0.22} &= e^{0.22 \cdot \ln(0.15)} \\
&= e^{0.22 \cdot (-1.8971)} \\
&= e^{-0.4174} \\
&= 0.6587
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_c &= \frac{1800}{0.6587} \approx 2733 \, \text{N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute $F_c$:**  

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_c &= k_c \cdot f \cdot d \\
&= 2733 \cdot 0.15 \cdot 1.0 \\
&\approx 410 \, \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cutting Power:**  

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \frac{F_c \cdot v_c}{60000} \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $v_c = 60$ m/min:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \frac{410 \cdot 60}{60000} \\
&\approx 0.41 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Taylor's Tool-Life Equation Applied to AM-Machining

### Step 1 — Taylor's Original Form (1907)

Frederick W. Taylor empirically established that tool life $T$ relates to cutting speed $V$ via:

$$V \cdot T^{n} = C$$

### Step 2 — Solving for Tool Life

$$\log V = \log C - n \cdot \log T$$

### Step 3 — Modified Taylor Equation (Extended with Feed & Depth)

For a more realistic prediction incorporating feed $f$ and depth $d$:

$$V \cdot T^{n} \cdot f^{a} \cdot d^{b} = C'$$

### Step 4 — AM-Specific Correction Factor

Because **AM parts accelerate tool wear by factor 1.5 – 2.5**, the corrected equation becomes:

$$V \cdot T^{n} \cdot f^{a} \cdot d^{b} = \frac{C'}{K_{AM}}$$

where $K_{AM}$ = AM wear-acceleration factor.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

Given:
- $V = 80$ m/min, $n = 0.25$, $C' = 350$, $a = 0.4$, $b = 0.2$
- $f = 0.15$ mm/rev, $d = 1.0$ mm
- $K_{AM} = 1.8$ (SLM Ti-6Al-4V)

**Solve for $T$:**  

$$
\begin{aligned}
80 \cdot T^{0.25} \cdot 0.15^{0.4} \cdot 1.0^{0.2} &= \frac{350}{1.8} \\
80 \cdot T^{0.25} \cdot 0.15^{0.4} &= 194.44
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute $0.15^{0.4}$:**  

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.15^{0.4} &= e^{0.4 \cdot \ln(0.15)} \\
&= e^{0.4 \cdot (-1.8971)} \\
&= e^{-0.7588} \\
&\approx 0.4684
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substitute and isolate $T^{0.25}$:**  

$$
\begin{aligned}
80 \cdot T^{0.25} \cdot 0.4684 &= 194.44 \\
T^{0.25} &= \frac{194.44}{80 \cdot 0.4684} \\
&= \frac{194.44}{37.47} \\
&\approx 5.189
\end{aligned}
$$

**Raise to the 4th power:**  

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= (5.189)^{4} \\
&\approx 725 \, \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison with wrought stock** ($K_{AM} = 1.0$):  

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{wrought} &= (8.461)^{4} \approx 5125 \, \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

➡ **AM stock reduces tool life by ~85%**, justifying dedicated AM-machining parameter sets.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Machining Process for Which AM Feature?

| AM Feature / Defect | Recommended Machining Operation | Tool Type | Critical Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| External stair-step (flat) | **Face milling** | Carbide insert, 45° lead | $a_e$ = full cutter Ø |
| Internal conformal channel | **Ball-end milling** | 4-flute carbide, TiAlN coated | Trochoidal step-over |
| Overhanging lattice strut | **Micro-milling** | Ø 0.3–1.0 mm, coated carbide | $f_z$ ≤ 0.01 mm |
| Threaded hole | **Tapping / Thread-milling** | HSS-E or solid carbide | Cutting fluid mandatory |
| Tight tolerance bore ±0.01 mm | **Boring / Reaming** | Single-point boring bar | Coolant-through |
| Surface finish Ra < 0.8 µm | **Grinding** | SiC / Al₂O₃ wheel | Creep-feed grinding |
| Internal sharp corner R < 0.2 mm | **EDM (Wire / Die-sink)** | Brass / Cu electrode | De-ionized water |
| Hardened deposit (DED) > 50 HRC | **EDM / Abrasive Flow** | — | Non-contact |
| Support-structure removal | **Band-saw + Milling** | Coarse carbide | High $a_p$, low $v_c$ |
| Internal porosity surface | **Reaming / Honing** | Diamond abrasive | Low pressure, light cut |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Surface Roughness in Ball-End Milling of AM Surfaces

For a **ball-end mill** of radius $R$ cutting at feed-per-tooth $f_z$:

**Geometric derivation:** The peak-to-valley height is governed by the scallop geometry left between successive tool passes.

$$R_a \approx \frac{f^2}{32 \cdot R}$$

For $f_z = 0.1$ mm and $R = 4$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_a &= \frac{(0.1)^2}{32 \cdot 4} \\
&= \frac{0.01}{128} \\
&= 7.81 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{mm} \\
&\approx 0.078 \, \mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

But **in practice**, AM surface roughness is dominated by the **built surface profile**, not the cutter geometry. Hence, an effective model:

$$R_{a,\text{total}} = \sqrt{R_{a,\text{AM-built}}^{2} + R_{a,\text{cutter-geo}}^{2}}$$

For $R_{a,\text{built}} = 12 \, \mu\text{m}$ and $R_{a,\text{geo}} = 0.08 \, \mu\text{m}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{a,\text{total}} &\approx \sqrt{144 + 0.0064} \\
&\approx \sqrt{144.006} \\
&\approx 12.0 \, \mu\text{m} \,\, (\text{built profile dominates})
\end{aligned}
$$

➡ **This shows that finishing-pass machining is essential because the cutter geometry alone cannot improve built surface roughness** — at least **0.3–0.5 mm of material must be removed**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hybrid Additive-Subtractive Manufacturing (HASM) — Block Architecture

The diagram below illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of a typical HASM workstation (e.g., DMG MORI LASERTEC 65 3D hybrid).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Powdersupply[Powder Feeder System] --> LaserHead[Laser Deposition Head DED]
    LaserHead --> BuildChamber[Build Chamber with Argon Atmosphere]
    BuildChamber --> LayerMonitor[Closed Loop Layer Thickness Monitor]
    LayerMonitor --> DepositionDecision{Deposit Thickness Reached Target}
    DepositionDecision -- No --> LaserHead
    DepositionDecision -- Yes --> CNCSpindle[CNC Machining Spindle 5 Axis]
    CNCSpindle --> ToolMagazine[Automatic Tool Changer]
    ToolMagazine --> InProcessProbe[In Process Touch Probe]
    InProcessProbe --> CADCompare[CAD Compare Module]
    CADCompare --> DepositionDecision
    CADCompare --> PartRemoval[Finished Part Removal]
    PartRemoval --> FinalQA[CMM CT Scan Final QA]
    CNCSpindle --> CoolantSystem[High Pressure Coolant Through Spindle]
    CoolantSystem --> CNCSpindle
    LayerMonitor --> SafetyInterlock[Safety Interlock and Atmosphere Control]
    SafetyInterlock --> BuildChamber
```

---

## 4.2 Process-Decision Flow for AM-Part Machining

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([As Built AM Part Received]) --> StressRelief{Residual Stress Critical}
    StressRelief -- Yes --> HeatTreat[Stress Relief Heat Treatment]
    StressRelief -- No --> Fixturing[Soft Jaw Fixturing]
    HeatTreat --> Fixturing
    Fixturing --> RoughMach[Rough Milling or Turning]
    RoughMach --> StressCheck{Distortion Detected}
    StressCheck -- Yes --> ReFixture[Re Fixture and Recoordinate]
    StressCheck -- No --> SemiFinish[Semi Finish Machining]
    ReFixture --> SemiFinish
    SemiFinish --> FeatureDecide{Non Conventional Feature Needed}
    FeatureDecide -- Sharp Internal Corner --> EDMProcess[EDM Wire or Die Sink]
    FeatureDecide -- High Tolerance Bore --> BoringReaming[Boring or Reaming]
    FeatureDecide -- Ultra Finish Ra --> GrindingProcess[Grinding or Honing]
    EDMProcess --> FinalInspection
    BoringReaming --> FinalInspection
    GrindingProcess --> FinalInspection[Final Inspection CMM Laser Scan]
    FinalInspection --> Done([Finished Part Ready for Use])
```

---

## 4.3 Tool Wear Progression in AM Machining — Conceptual Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stage1[Run In Phase 0 to 5 min]
        A1[Rapid Initial Wear from Abrasive Powder]
    end
    subgraph Stage2[Steady State 5 to 25 min]
        A2[Linear Flank Wear Progression]
    end
    subgraph Stage3[Failure Phase beyond 25 min]
        A3[Accelerated Wear from Workpiece Hardening]
    end
    Stage1 --> Stage2
    Stage2 --> Stage3
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
```

---

## 4.4 Cross-Section of an AM-Machined Aero Bracket — Functional Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PartFeatures[Hybrid AM Part Features]
        F1[Lattice Core AM Built]
        F2[Outer Skin AM Built]
        F3[Mounting Holes CNC Drilled and Tapped]
        F4[Mating Face CNC Face Milled Ra 0.8]
        F5[Internal Conformal Channel CNC Ball End Milled]
        F6[Threaded Insert Hole EDM Finished]
    end
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F2 --> F4
    F2 --> F5
    F2 --> F6
```

---

## 4.5 KTU-Examiner Friendly Schematic — Sub-Processes of Machining

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Machining[Machining in AM Context] --> Sub1[Conventional Subtractive]
    Machining --> Sub2[Hybrid Combined]
    Machining --> Sub3[Post Processing Finishing]
    Sub1 --> S1A[Milling Turning Drilling]
    Sub1 --> S1B[Grinding Honing Lapping]
    Sub2 --> S2A[DED plus CNC in Single Setup]
    Sub2 --> S2B[PBF plus Subtractive Module]
    Sub3 --> S3A[EDM for Hard to Reach Features]
    Sub3 --> S3B[Abrasive Flow for Internal Channels]
    Sub3 --> S3C[Polishing for Bio Medical Surfaces]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]  
**Why is conventional machining essential even after additive manufacturing? List any three reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. AM-built surfaces exhibit **stair-stepping effect (Ra = 6–25 µm)** due to finite layer thickness; machining achieves **Ra ≤ 0.8 µm** as required for functional surfaces.
2. **Dimensional tolerance** of as-built AM parts is ±0.1 to ±0.5 mm; many engineering applications require **±0.025 mm** achievable only through machining.
3. **Internal conformal channels, sharp corners, fine holes, and threaded features** produced via AM often need **drilling, milling, reaming or EDM** to meet functional specifications.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]  
**What is Hybrid Additive-Subtractive Manufacturing (HASM)? Mention one commercial example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

HASM is a manufacturing approach in which **additive layer-by-layer deposition (DED or PBF)** and **subtractive CNC machining (milling, turning, drilling)** are **integrated into a single machine tool / single setup**, enabling near-net-shape build followed by in-situ finishing without part re-clamping.

**Commercial Example:** *DMG MORI LASERTEC 65 3D hybrid* — combines laser metal deposition with 5-axis milling in one workstation.

(Acceptable alternatives: *Mazak INTEGREX i-AM*, *Optomec Hybrid System*.)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]

**(a)** Explain the **distinctive metallurgical challenges** faced when machining additively manufactured components compared to wrought stock. Discuss how **tool wear** and **surface integrity** are affected. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Ti-6Al-4V part built by **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** is to be finish-milled on a CNC vertical machining centre. Given: cutting speed $v_c = 60$ m/min, feed-per-tooth $f_z = 0.12$ mm/tooth, axial depth $a_p = 0.5$ mm, radial depth $a_e = 5$ mm, cutter diameter $D = 10$ mm, number of teeth $z = 4$. For Ti-6Al-4V AM: $k_{c1.1} = 1800$ N/mm², $m_c = 0.22$. Calculate (i) spindle speed, (ii) feed rate, (iii) MRR, and (iv) cutting power. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Distinctive Metallurgical Challenges (7 marks — break-up given):**

| S.No. | Challenge | Explanation (mark distribution) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Abrasive adhered powder** (1 mark) | Un-melted powder grains (20–63 µm) act as micro-abrasives, accelerating flank wear 2–5×. |
| 2 | **Anisotropic hardness** (1 mark) | Build-direction hardness differs by 5–15% from in-plane, causing uneven cutter loading. |
| 3 | **Residual stress** (1 mark) | Up to 60% of yield stress exists; material removal causes distortion (1–2 mm). |
| 4 | **Sub-surface porosity** (1 mark) | Lack-of-fusion voids cause intermittent impact loads on cutter. |
| 5 | **Hard oxidized layer** (1 mark) | Outer 50–100 µm is oxygen-enriched → harder → increased $k_c$. |
| 6 | **Tool wear effects** (1 mark) | Rapid crater wear from high $k_c$; built-up edge from low $v_c$ range. |
| 7 | **Surface integrity effects** (1 mark) | Micro-hardness alteration, tensile residual stress from machining, risk of micro-cracking at pores. |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — Step-by-Step (7 marks):**

**(i) Spindle speed $N$ (1 mark):**  

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{v_c \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot D} \\
&= \frac{60 \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot 10} \\
&= \frac{60000}{31.416} \\
&\approx 1910 \, \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Feed rate $f$ (1 mark):**  

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= f_z \cdot z \cdot N \\
&= 0.12 \cdot 4 \cdot 1910 \\
&= 916.8 \, \text{mm/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Material Removal Rate $MRR$ (2 marks):**  

$$
\begin{aligned}
MRR &= v_c \cdot f_z \cdot z \cdot a_p \cdot a_e \,/\, 1000 \\
&= \frac{60 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 4 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 5}{1000} \\
&= \frac{72}{1000} \\
&= 0.072 \, \text{cm}^3/\text{s} \\
&= 72 \, \text{mm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Cutting power (3 marks):**  

Compute $k_c$:  

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_c &= k_{c1.1} \cdot h^{-m_c}, \quad \text{where } h = f_z = 0.12 \, \text{mm} \\
k_c &= 1800 \cdot (0.12)^{-0.22}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.12)^{-0.22} &= e^{-0.22 \cdot \ln(0.12)} \\
&= e^{-0.22 \cdot (-2.1203)} \\
&= e^{0.4665} \\
&\approx 1.5945
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_c &= 1800 \cdot 1.5945 \approx 2870 \, \text{N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute cutting force:  

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_c &= k_c \cdot f_z \cdot a_p \cdot z \\
&= 2870 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 4 \\
&= 688.8 \, \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute cutting power:  

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \frac{F_c \cdot v_c}{60000} \\
&= \frac{688.8 \cdot 60}{60000} \\
&= \frac{41328}{60000} \\
&\approx 0.689 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $N$ | ≈ 1910 rpm |
| $f$ | ≈ 917 mm/min |
| $MRR$ | ≈ 72 mm³/s |
| $F_c$ | ≈ 689 N |
| $P_c$ | ≈ 0.69 kW |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture and working principle of a Hybrid Additive-Subtractive Manufacturing (HASM) machine**. Mention at least **two commercial systems**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using **Taylor's extended tool-life equation**, compare the **tool life when machining SLM-built Ti-6Al-4V** vs **wrought Ti-6Al-4V** under identical parameters. Use $V = 80$ m/min, $f = 0.15$ mm/rev, $d = 1$ mm, $n = 0.25$, $a = 0.4$, $b = 0.2$, $C' = 350$. Take $K_{AM} = 1.8$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) HASM Architecture (7 marks — 4 marks diagram + 3 marks explanation):**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**  
- The system uses a **5-axis CNC kinematic platform** that hosts both a **laser/powder DED head** and a **high-speed milling spindle**.  
- Build proceeds by depositing **2–10 layers**, after which the laser head retracts, the milling spindle engages, and **inter-pass finishing** is performed to maintain geometric accuracy and flatness.  
- **Closed-loop probing** measures the as-built profile, compares with CAD, and feeds back to the deposition plan for **adaptive layer-height compensation**.

**Two commercial systems (any two — 1 mark each):**

1. **DMG MORI LASERTEC 65 3D hybrid** — laser metal deposition + 5-axis milling, up to Ø 650 mm × 400 mm build envelope.
2. **Mazak INTEGREX i-100 AM** — multitasking mill-turn centre with fibre-laser cladding head.
3. **Optomec DED Hybrid System** — used with large aerospace deposition.
4. **Hybrid Manufacturing Technologies AMBIT™ multi-task head** — retrofits onto any CNC.

---

**(b) Taylor's Tool Life Comparison (7 marks):**

**Wrought Ti-6Al-4V ($K_{AM} = 1.0$):**  

$$
\begin{aligned}
80 \cdot T^{0.25} \cdot 0.15^{0.4} \cdot 1.0^{0.2} &= 350 \\
80 \cdot T^{0.25} \cdot 0.4684 &= 350 \\
T^{0.25} &= \frac{350}{37.47} = 9.342 \\
T_{wrought} &= (9.342)^4 \\
&= 7615 \, \text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**SLM Ti-6Al-4V ($K_{AM} = 1.8$):**  

$$
\begin{aligned}
80 \cdot T^{0.25} \cdot 0.4684 &= \frac{350}{1.8} = 194.44 \\
T^{0.25} &= \frac{194.44}{37.47} = 5.189 \\
T_{AM} &= (5.189)^4 \\
&\approx 725 \, \text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Condition | Tool Life |
|---|---|
| Wrought | 7615 min |
| SLM-built | 725 min |
| **Reduction** | **~90%** |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Machining SLM-built Ti-6Al-4V reduces carbide tool life by nearly an order of magnitude, justifying the use of **AM-specific cutting parameter libraries, coated tools, and high-pressure coolant delivery**.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**  
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $v_c$ from m/min to mm/min** in the power equation → wrong unit → 1-mark deduction.  
> 2. **Using $f_z \cdot z$ directly as feed rate without multiplying by $N$** → wrong feed → 2-mark deduction.  
> 3. **Skipping the $K_{AM}$ factor in Taylor's equation** → both tool lives identical → full-mark loss in part (b).  
> 4. **Forgetting that $f_z$ and $h$ are equal only in orthogonal cutting**; in milling, $h = f_z \sin(\kappa)$ — examiners mark strictly.  
> 5. **Not stating units explicitly** in numerical answers → 0.5-mark penalty per missing unit.  
> 6. **In HASM diagrams**, students often forget the *closed-loop CAD compare* arrow — this is a frequent 1-mark deduction in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Machining in AM** = post-processing + hybrid in-situ finishing; *not* an alternative to AM.
- ✅ **As-built Ra = 6–25 µm**, **post-machined Ra = 0.4–3.2 µm** — finish pass removes **≥ 0.3 mm** stock.
- ✅ **Tolerance: as-built ±0.1–0.5 mm**, post-machined **±0.025 mm** achievable.
- ✅ **Hybrid systems**: DMG MORI LASERTEC, Mazak INTEGREX i-AM, Optomec, AMBIT™.
- ✅ **MRR (turning)** = $v \cdot f \cdot d$; **MRR (milling)** = $v_c \cdot f_z \cdot z \cdot a_p \cdot a_e \,/\, 1000$.
- ✅ **Kienzle equation**: $F_c = k_{c1.1} \cdot h^{1-m_c} \cdot b$.
- ✅ **Taylor's equation**: $V \cdot T^{n} \cdot f^{a} \cdot d^{b} = C' / K_{AM}$.
- ✅ **AM wear acceleration factor $K_{AM}$** = 1.5–2.5; can reduce tool life by **~85–90%**.
- ✅ **Specific cutting energy $P_s$** for Ti-6Al-4V AM ≈ **1.5–4.5 J/mm³**.
- ✅ **AM-part challenges**: abrasive powder, anisotropy, residual stress, porosity, oxidized skin.
- ✅ **Cutter choice**: TiAlN/AlCrN coated carbide; trochoidal milling for deep pockets.
- ✅ **EDM** is preferred for sharp internal corners (< 0.2 mm) and hardened DED deposits.
- ✅ **High-pressure coolant through spindle** is mandatory for AM-Ti machining.
- ✅ **Interpass temperature** in HASM must remain < **150 °C** for Ti-6Al-4V.
- ✅ Always state **units** in numerical answers; always include $K_{AM}$ in AM-tool-life problems.
- ✅ **Recall diagram**: As-built → Stress-relief → Soft-jaw fixture → Rough mill → Distortion check → Semi-finish → Feature-specific (EDM/grinding/boring) → CMM QA.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Treatment and NDT Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET867 - METAL ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Surface Treatment and NDT Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Surface Treatment in Metal Additive Manufacturing — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surface Treatment** in the context of Metal Additive Manufacturing (MAM) refers to the ensemble of post-process operations performed on as-built parts to modify the topographical, metallurgical, and mechanical characteristics of the surface layer, thereby improving fatigue life, corrosion resistance, dimensional accuracy, and aesthetic appeal. The as-built surface of AM parts is characteristically rough (Ra ≈ 5–25 µm for powder bed fusion, 20–80 µm for DED) due to the **staircase effect**, **balling phenomenon**, and **adhered partially-melted powder particles**.

In KTU 2024 syllabus context, surface treatment is divided into three engineering families:

1. **Mechanical Surface Treatments** — Sandblasting, Shot Peening, Bead Blasting, Tumbling/Vibratory Finishing, Machining, Polishing.
2. **Thermo-Chemical Treatments** — Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP), Heat Treatment (Solutionizing & Aging), Case Hardening.
3. **Electro-Chemical Treatments** — Electropolishing, Electrochemical Machining (ECM), Anodizing.

### 1.2 Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** is a branch of materials engineering comprising inspection techniques used to evaluate the integrity, geometry, composition, and presence of defects in a part **without impairing its future usefulness**. In AM, NDT is critical because the layered, rapid-solidification microstructure creates a unique defect zoo (porosity, lack-of-fusion, cracks, inclusions, delamination) that is statistically different from wrought or cast defects.

The five NDT methods mandated by KTU 2024 Module 3 are:
- **Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT)**
- **Radiographic Testing (RT)** — including X-ray **Computed Tomography (CT)**
- **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)**
- **Eddy Current Testing (ECT)**
- **Visual Testing (VT)**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Newly Plastered Wall" Effect:**
> Imagine an engineer building a wall brick-by-brick (each brick = one molten track). The wall is **structurally solid** but feels like sandpaper because:
> (a) The mortar between bricks is uneven (→ **stair-stepping**),
> (b) Some sand grains stick to the surface (→ **adhered powders**),
> (c) The trowel leaves a wavy pattern (→ **balling**).
>
> Before painting or inspecting the wall, you must:
> 1. **Smooth it** (Surface Treatment — sandblasting, polishing, HIP), then
> 2. **Tap it to find hollow pockets and cracks** (NDT — UT, RT, DPT), then
> 3. **Verify it looks correct** (VT, dimensional CT).
>
> In AM, the molten "tracks" solidify with built-in **tensile residual stresses** and **entrapped gases** — making the "wall" both rough and potentially full of invisible defects. Surface treatment fixes the *skin*; NDT finds the *sickness inside*.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used (Highlighted)

- **Ra** — Arithmetic mean surface roughness (µm)
- **Rz** — Mean peak-to-valley height (µm)
- **HIP Pressure** ≈ **100–200 MPa**
- **HIP Temperature** ≈ **0.6–0.8 × Tm** (Tm = melting point in Kelvin)
- **Penetrant Sensitivity** ≥ **2 µm** (per ASTM E1417)
- **CT Voxel Resolution** ≈ **5–50 µm** for lab systems

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Are AM Surfaces Rough? — Root-Cause Breakdown

The as-built AM surface quality is governed by **three primary physical mechanisms**:

1. **Stair-Stepping Effect (Geometric Error)**
   - Caused by discretization of a curved CAD surface into finite layers of thickness `h`.
   - Mathematical model: For a surface inclined at angle `θ` to the build platform, the theoretical roughness follows a **staircase error** relation.

2. **Balling Phenomenon (Fluid-Instability Error)**
   - Molten track breaks into discrete spheres due to **Rayleigh-Plateau instability** if the line energy density is suboptimal.
   - Plateau criterion: $L \geq \pi \sqrt{2r_0}$ where $L$ is the wavelength, $r_0$ is the cylinder radius.

3. **Adhered Powder (Loose-Particle Error)**
   - Partially melted particles are sintered to the surface by the **heat-affected zone (HAZ)** of adjacent tracks.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow — Why Each Treatment is Needed

> **Step 1 → Identify the surface state**
> Measure **Ra**, **Rz**, and **3D areal parameters (Sa, Sz)** per ISO 25178.

> **Step 2 → Decide treatment based on end-use**
> - Fatigue-critical part → Shot Peening (induces compressive residual stress).
> - Biocompatible implant → Electropolishing + HIP.
> - Aerospace structural → HIP + Machining + DPT.

> **Step 3 → Apply treatment**
> - Mechanical: physically remove material.
> - Thermal: diffusively heal internal porosity.
> - Chemical: dissolve high-points preferentially.

> **Step 4 → Verify by NDT**
> - Surface defects → DPT or VT.
> - Subsurface defects → UT or X-ray CT.

### 2.3 Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP) — The Workhorse of AM Post-Processing

HIP applies **simultaneous high temperature and high inert gas pressure (typically Argon)** to close internal porosity via **plastic deformation, creep, and diffusion bonding**.

**Mechanism:** The external gas pressure (P) acts hydrostatically, and the part behaves as a thick-walled pressure vessel. Spherical pores shrink following the **Sakai-Hobson equation** (rate of densification):

$$\frac{d\rho}{dt} = \left(\frac{3P}{4\eta}\right) \cdot (1-\rho) \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\sqrt{2} \cdot \sigma_y}{P}\right)$$

where:
- $\rho$ = relative density,
- $P$ = applied pressure,
- $\eta$ = effective viscosity of the matrix,
- $\sigma_y$ = yield strength at HIP temperature.

**Real-world utility:** NASA and Rolls-Royce specify HIP for all critical aerospace AM Ti-6Al-4V and Inconel 718 components. Standards: **ASTM F3301** (AM parts), **AMS 2774** (heat treatment), **ASTM F2924** (Ti6Al4V).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | Typical AM Value |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Staircase roughness | $R_{th} = h \cdot \sin\theta$ | $h$ = layer thickness (µm), $\theta$ = surface inclination (°) | $h = 30\,\mu m$ |
| 2 | Ra (arithmetic mean) | $R_a = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$ | $L$ = sampling length, $y$ = profile height (µm) | Powder Bed: 5–25 µm |
| 3 | Rz (peak-to-valley) | $R_z = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5}(Z_{pi} - Z_{vi})$ | $Z_p, Z_v$ = peak/valley heights (µm) | DED: 40–80 µm |
| 4 | Volumetric Energy Density | $VED = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | $P$ = laser power (W), $v$ = scan speed (mm/s), $h$ = hatch spacing (mm), $t$ = layer thickness (mm) | Ti6Al4V: 60–80 J/mm³ |
| 5 | HIP pressure vessel hoop stress | $\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot r}{t_w}$ | $r$ = internal radius, $t_w$ = wall thickness (mm) | $\sigma_{hoop} < \sigma_y / SF$ |
| 6 | Rayleigh instability wavelength | $\lambda_R = 2\pi \sqrt{2} \cdot r_0$ | $r_0$ = molten track radius (µm) | $\lambda_R \approx 3.5\,r_0$ |
| 7 | UT pulse-echo distance | $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | $c$ = sound velocity in metal (mm/µs), $t$ = time-of-flight (µs) | $c_{steel} \approx 5.9\,mm/\mu s$ |
| 8 | CT linear attenuation (Beer-Lambert) | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$ | $\mu$ = linear attenuation coeff. (cm⁻¹), $x$ = thickness (cm) | $\mu_{Al} \approx 0.43$ |
| 9 | Electropolishing removal rate | $RR = \frac{J \cdot M \cdot t}{\rho \cdot n \cdot F}$ | $J$ = current density (A/cm²), $M$ = molar mass, $\rho$ = density, $n$ = electrons, $F$ = Faraday const. (96485 C/mol) | RR ≈ 1–5 µm/min |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that **Formula 1 (Staircase roughness)** is inversely driven by layer thickness `h` — this is why KTU examiners frequently test the link between **process parameters → surface quality**. Halving `h` from 30 µm to 15 µm roughly halves `Ra` from staircase alone, but the **balling** and **adhered powder** contributions remain — meaning the relationship is non-linear in practice.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Aerospace (GE LEAP fuel nozzle)**: HIP + DPT mandatory; surface roughness polished from Ra ≈ 12 µm to Ra ≈ 0.8 µm.
- **Medical (Ti6Al4V spinal cages)**: Electropolishing to meet **ASTM F86** biocompatibility.
- **Automotive (Bugatti brake caliper)**: Post-machining + bead blasting for functional surfaces.
- **Space (Rocket Engine Injectors)**: X-ray CT inspection mandated to detect lack-of-fusion defects down to **50 µm**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Staircase Surface Roughness

Consider an inclined planar surface to be built layer-by-layer at layer thickness `h`. The cross-section reveals a staircase profile with riser `h` and tread `w = h / \tan\theta`.

**Step 1 — Identify the triangle geometry of one step**

For a single step, the ideal surface is the line joining two adjacent layer edges. The maximum perpendicular deviation (peak) is the area-of-excess above the ideal line.

**Step 2 — Set up the height function**

A single step profile can be modeled as a sawtooth. The deviation at any position $x \in [0, w]$ is:

$$y(x) = h \cdot \left(1 - \frac{x}{w}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the arithmetic mean roughness Ra**

Ra is the mean absolute deviation over one period $L = w$:

$$R_a = \frac{1}{w} \int_0^w \left\vert h \cdot \left(1 - \frac{x}{w}\right) \right\vert \, dx$$

Since $y(x) \geq 0$ over the period:

$$R_a = \frac{h}{w} \int_0^w \left(1 - \frac{x}{w}\right) dx$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral**

$$\int_0^w \left(1 - \frac{x}{w}\right) dx = \left[x - \frac{x^2}{2w}\right]_0^w = w - \frac{w}{2} = \frac{w}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back**

$$R_a = \frac{h}{w} \cdot \frac{w}{2} = \frac{h}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Convert the staircase into an inclined surface term**

Since $w = h \cdot \cot\theta$:

$$R_a = \frac{h}{2} = \frac{h \cdot \sin\theta}{2 \cdot \cos\theta} \cdot \cos\theta = \frac{h \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \cos\theta}{2}$$

This simplifies to the **standard staircase roughness formula**:

$$\boxed{R_a = \frac{h \cdot \sin(2\theta)}{4}}$$

where $h$ is the layer thickness and $\theta$ is the inclination angle of the surface to the build platform.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Check:** When $\theta = 90°$ (vertical wall, impossible to build without supports) $\sin(2\theta) = 0$, giving $R_a = 0$ — **WRONG physically**, but the formula is valid only for $\theta < 90°$. For $\theta = 0°$ (horizontal top surface) $R_a = 0$, which is correct as horizontal surfaces have no staircase effect.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — HIP Pore Closure Time Estimate

**Step 1 — Define geometry**

Assume a spherical pore of initial radius $r_0$ embedded in a fully dense matrix. HIP pressure $P$ acts isostatically. The internal pore pressure $p_{int}$ is initially 0 (vacuum from gas-atomized powder) or 1 atm (residual gas in keyhole pores).

**Step 2 — Apply force balance on the pore wall**

The pressure difference $(P - p_{int})$ creates a radial compressive stress that drives densification. The effective pressure driving pore shrinkage is:

$$\Delta p = P - p_{int} - \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$

where $\gamma$ is the surface energy (typically negligible for $r > 1\,\mu m$).

**Step 3 — Use Nabarro-Herring / Coble creep model**

The volumetric strain rate $\dot{\epsilon}$ for diffusion-controlled creep is:

$$\dot{\epsilon} = \frac{A \cdot \sigma^n \cdot D \cdot b^2 \cdot \Omega}{k_B \cdot T \cdot d^m}$$

where $A$ is a material constant, $D$ is the diffusivity, $b$ is Burgers vector, $\Omega$ is atomic volume, $d$ is grain size, $n, m$ are exponents.

**Step 4 — Time to close a pore to zero radius**

By integrating the creep strain from initial porosity $\phi_0$ to zero, the **Ashby HIP map** gives an empirical time estimate:

$$t_{HIP} = \frac{k_B T \cdot r_0^2}{\Omega \cdot P \cdot D_v} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1}{\phi_f}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Plug in typical Ti6Al4V values**

- $T = 1173\,K$ (HIP temp),
- $r_0 = 50\,\mu m$ (typical lack-of-fusion pore),
- $D_v = 10^{-14}\,m^2/s$ (Ti lattice diffusion),
- $P = 100\,MPa$,
- $\phi_f = 0.001$ (residual porosity target = 0.1%).

$$t_{HIP} = \frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \cdot 1173 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-6})^2}{3.5 \times 10^{-29} \cdot 100 \times 10^6 \cdot 10^{-14}} \cdot \ln(1000)$$

$$t_{HIP} \approx 11,600\,s \approx 3.2\,hours$$

This is why KTU-standard HIP cycles for AM Ti6Al4V specify **3–4 hours at 920°C / 100 MPa**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of NDT Method for AM Defect Type

| AM Defect Type | Location | Minimum Detectable Size | Best NDT Method | Limitation |
|----------------|----------|-------------------------|------------------|-------------|
| Lack-of-fusion (irregular) | Subsurface, planar | $\geq 50\,\mu m$ | **X-ray CT / UT** | CT resolution vs. part size trade-off |
| Keyhole porosity (spherical) | Subsurface | $\geq 20\,\mu m$ | **X-ray CT** | Beam hardening artifacts |
| Surface crack (open) | Surface | $\geq 2\,\mu m$ | **DPT (PT)** | Surface-breaking only |
| Subsurface crack (closed) | Subsurface | $\geq 100\,\mu m$ | **UT** | Requires couplant |
| Surface roughness anomaly | Surface | N/A | **VT (3D profilometry)** | Subjective in VT mode |
| Sub-surface delamination | Inter-layer | $\geq 200\,\mu m$ | **UT phased array** | Requires flat entry surface |
| Surface contamination (Fe) | Surface | $\geq 0.5\,\mu m$ thick | **ECT** | Conductive materials only |
| Internal inclusions | Subsurface | $\geq 80\,\mu m$ | **X-ray CT** | Material-dependent contrast |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Matrix Insight:** No single NDT method covers all defect types. KTU 2024 examiners love to ask: *"Which NDT for surface vs. subsurface cracks?"* The definitive answer: **DPT for surface-open, UT or X-ray CT for subsurface closed**. The selection matrix above is high-yield for 14-mark Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — AM Post-Processing Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **decision-driven interaction** between surface treatment operations, NDT inspection stages, and the feedback loop to the AM build process (since photorealistic part drawings are not feasible as Mermaid flowcharts).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[As-Built AM Part] --> B{Initial Visual Inspection VT}
    B --> C[Loose Powder Removal - Bead Blasting]
    C --> D{Surface Criticality}
    D --> E[Mechanical Polishing]
    D --> F[Shot Peening - Compressive Stress]
    E --> G[Heat Treatment - Stress Relief]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Hot Isostatic Pressing HIP]
    H --> I{Targeted NDT Stage}
    I --> J[DPT - Surface Crack Detection]
    I --> K[X-ray CT - Volumetric Porosity]
    I --> L[UT - Subsurface Defects]
    I --> M[ECT - Surface Conductivity]
    J --> N{Defect Found}
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Reject and Re-machining or Scrap]
    N --> P[Accept Part - Final Machining]
    P --> Q[Final Dimensional CT Scan]
    Q --> R[Certified Part - End Use]
    O --> S[Feedback to AM Process Parameters]
    S --> A
```

### 4.2 NDT Method Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DefType[Defect Type Identification] --> SurfaceOpen{Surface-Open Crack}
    DefType --> Subsurf{Subsurface Defect}
    DefType --> Volumetric{Internal Volumetric Defect}
    DefType --> SurfaceCond{Surface Conductivity Change}
    SurfaceOpen --> DPT[Dye Penetrant Testing per ASTM E1417]
    Subsurf --> UT[Ultrasonic Testing per ASTM E494]
    Volumetric --> XCT[X-ray CT Scanning per ASTM E1441]
    Volumetric --> RT[2D Radiographic Testing per ASTM E1742]
    SurfaceCond --> ECT[Eddy Current Testing per ASTM E566]
    DPT --> Result[Defect Map and Sizing Report]
    UT --> Result
    XCT --> Result
    RT --> Result
    ECT --> Result
```

### 4.3 Process Flow for AM Surface Treatment Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Depowdering] --> B[Compressive Stress Relief Heat Treatment]
    B --> C[Housing Removal and Support Cutting]
    C --> D[Bead Blasting or Sandblasting]
    D --> E[Hot Isostatic Pressing HIP]
    E --> F[Solutionizing and Aging Heat Treatment]
    F --> G[Machining to Net Shape]
    G --> H[Surface Polishing or Electropolishing]
    H --> I[Shot Peening for Fatigue Resistance]
    I --> J[Final NDT Inspection]
    J --> K[Anodizing or Coating if Required]
    K --> L[Acceptance and Certification]
```

### 4.4 HIP Process Schematic (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Pressure Vessel Furnace] --> B[Argon Gas Supply at 100-200 MPa]
    A --> C[Heating Elements to 900-1200 C]
    A --> D[Workload of AM Parts on Fixtures]
    D --> E[High Temperature Inert Environment]
    E --> F[Isostatic Pressure Application on All Surfaces]
    F --> G[Plastic Deformation and Creep of Pore Walls]
    G --> H[Diffusion Bonding of Internal Surfaces]
    H --> I[Cooling under Pressure to Retain Densification]
    I --> J[Part Removal and Density Verification]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List any three post-processing surface treatments used for metal AM parts and state one specific benefit of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP):** Eliminates internal porosity (down to < 0.1% by volume) and improves fatigue life by 2–10× by closing lack-of-fusion defects through diffusion bonding at ~100 MPa and ~0.7 Tm.
2. **Shot Peening:** Induces beneficial **compressive residual stresses** to a depth of 50–250 µm, dramatically increasing fatigue endurance limit and stress-corrosion cracking resistance.
3. **Electropolishing:** Selectively dissolves the micro-peaks of the surface, reducing Ra by 30–50% and creating a **passive oxide layer** for corrosion resistance — critical for medical implants per ASTM F86.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT) and Eddy Current Testing (ECT) in three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | DPT (Dye Penetrant) | ECT (Eddy Current) |
|-------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Detects **surface-breaking** defects only (open to surface). | Detects **surface and near-surface** defects up to ~3 mm depth. |
| 2 | Works on **all non-porous materials** — metals, ceramics, plastics. | Works only on **electrically conductive** materials. |
| 3 | Uses capillary action of penetrant dye; visual indication under UV/white light. | Uses electromagnetic induction; requires reference coil calibration. |
| 4 | Slower (~30 min cycle); labor-intensive. | Fast, real-time inspection; easily automated. |
| 5 | Per **ASTM E1417** / **ASTM E165**. | Per **ASTM E566** / **ASTM E215**. |

---

### Part B — Module 3 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks) — [Choice 1]**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Question]**

**(a)** With neat sketches explain the **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP) process** for AM parts. Discuss the process parameters and the mechanism of pore closure. Cite at least one relevant ASTM/AMS standard. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Explain in detail the **X-ray Computed Tomography (CT)** technique for inspection of AM parts. Discuss the principle, defect detection capability, and limitations. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

**Definition:** HIP is a thermo-mechanical post-process that subjects parts to simultaneous high temperature (0.6–0.8 Tm) and high isostatic gas pressure (typically argon at 100–200 MPa) inside a pressure vessel.

**Process Parameters:**
- **Temperature:** 920°C for Ti6Al4V; 1160°C for Inconel 718; 1130°C for AlSi10Mg.
- **Pressure:** 100–200 MPa.
- **Hold time:** 2–4 hours.
- **Cooling rate:** Slow cool under pressure to retain densification.

**Mechanism of Pore Closure:**
1. **Stage 1 — Initial plastic collapse:** Pressure difference $\Delta p = P - p_{int}$ causes plastic yielding of pore walls.
2. **Stage 2 — Power-law creep:** Pores shrink by dislocation creep $\dot{\epsilon} = A\sigma^n \exp(-Q/RT)$.
3. **Stage 3 — Diffusion bonding:** Final closure by **Nabarro-Herring and Coble creep** with simultaneous atomic diffusion across the pore surface.
4. **Stage 4 — Equiaxed grain growth:** Slightly coarsens microstructure.

**Standards:**
- **ASTM F3301** — Standard for post-processing methods of metal AM parts.
- **AMS 2774** — Heat treatment of wrought and AM parts.
- **ASTM F2924** — Ti6Al4V AM specification.
- **ASTM F3055** — Inconel 718 AM specification.

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.4 HIP Process Schematic.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Principle:**
X-ray CT uses the **Beer-Lambert law**: $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$ where $\mu$ is the linear attenuation coefficient. A part is rotated 360° in a conical X-ray beam and 2D projections are reconstructed into a 3D voxel map using **filtered back-projection** or **iterative reconstruction algorithms** (e.g., FDK, SIRT).

**Setup:**
- Microfocus X-ray tube (80–300 kV, 1–200 W).
- Rotating sample stage with sub-micron precision.
- Flat-panel or CCD detector (2000 × 2000 pixels typical).
- Reconstruction software: VGStudio, Avizo, Simpleware.

**Defect Detection Capability:**
- **Resolution:** 5–50 µm voxel size (lab systems); 50–200 µm (production systems).
- **Detects:** Lack-of-fusion (irregular), keyhole porosity (spherical), inclusions, dimensional metrology.
- **Minimum detectable porosity:** ~3× voxel size (e.g., 15 µm for 5 µm voxel).

**Limitations:**
1. **Beam hardening artifacts** in dense materials (Inconel, Tungsten).
2. **Cone-beam artifacts** in parts > 100 mm.
3. **Long scan time** (30 min – 4 hr per part).
4. **High capital cost** (€200k – €1.5M).
5. **Cannot detect crack orientation easily** — orientation-dependent attenuation.
6. **Radiation safety** — licensed X-ray source.

**Standards:** **ASTM E1441**, **ASTM E1570**, **ISO 15708**.

---

#### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks) — [Choice 2]**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the various **mechanical surface treatment methods** used for metal AM parts. Discuss their effect on surface roughness values. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Describe the **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** method in detail. With a diagram explain pulse-echo and through-transmission modes. Discuss its applicability and limitations in AM part inspection. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Mechanical Surface Treatments for AM Parts:**

1. **Bead Blasting / Sandblasting**
   - Glass beads or alumina particles accelerated at 4–8 bar onto the surface.
   - **Effect:** Removes loosely adhered powder, reduces Ra from 15 µm → 8–10 µm.
   - Introduces mild compressive stress.

2. **Tumbling / Vibratory Finishing**
   - Parts placed with ceramic media in a rotating/vibrating bowl.
   - **Effect:** Uniform deburring; reduces Ra to 4–6 µm; gentle — preserves geometry.
   - Cycle time: 2–8 hours.

3. **Shot Peening**
   - Steel/ceramic/glass shot propelled at high velocity (Almen intensity 8–16 A).
   - **Effect:** Ra slightly increases (0.5–1 µm) but introduces **compressive residual stress** to ~200 µm depth → **2–10× fatigue life improvement**.

4. **Polishing (manual or robotic)**
   - Progressive grit (P180 → P3000) abrasive polishing.
   - **Effect:** Ra reduces to 0.4–1.6 µm; mirror finish possible.

5. **Machining (Milling/Turning)**
   - CNC post-machining of functional surfaces.
   - **Effect:** Ra < 0.8 µm; eliminates staircase effect on critical faces.

**Summary Table:**

| Treatment | Ra Reduction | Mechanical Benefit |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| Bead Blasting | 30–50% | Deburring only |
| Tumbling | 50–70% | Edge radiusing |
| Shot Peening | Increases Ra slightly | **Compressive stress** |
| Polishing | 80–95% | Cosmetic + functional |
| CNC Machining | 95–99% | Final tolerance ±0.02 mm |

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Ultrasonic Testing — Principle:**
High-frequency sound waves (1–25 MHz) are coupled into the part via a piezo-electric transducer. The waves reflect from **acoustic impedance mismatches** — i.e., from **defects** (voids, cracks, inclusions) — and the reflected signal is analyzed for time-of-flight and amplitude.

**Modes of UT:**

1. **Pulse-Echo Mode (most common for AM)**
   - Single transducer acts as both emitter and receiver.
   - Distance to defect: $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$, where $c$ = longitudinal velocity in the material.

2. **Through-Transmission Mode**
   - Separate transmitter and receiver on opposite sides of the part.
   - Detects defects by **reduction in received signal amplitude**.

3. **Phased Array UT**
   - Multiple elements electronically steered; **C-scan** imaging.
   - Used for complex AM geometries.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pulse Generator] --> B[Transducer]
    B --> C[Couplant Gel]
    C --> D[AM Part Wall]
    D --> E[Internal Defect]
    E --> F[Reflected Wave Echo]
    F --> G[Same Transducer Receives]
    G --> H[Signal Amplifier]
    H --> I[Display Screen A-Scan]
    D -.-> J[Back Wall Echo]
    J --> I
```

**Time of Flight Calculation Example (Steel):**
For a defect at 10 mm depth in steel (c = 5900 m/s):

$$t = \frac{2 \cdot d}{c} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.010}{5900} = 3.39\,\mu s$$

**Applicability in AM:**
- ✅ Excellent for **lack-of-fusion defects** (irregular morphology gives strong reflection).
- ✅ Locates **delamination** between layers.
- ✅ Used in **ASTM F2924** qualification of AM aerospace parts.
- ❌ **Limitation 1:** Requires couplant (gel or water immersion) — difficult for complex internal channels.
- ❌ **Limitation 2:** Rough as-built AM surfaces scatter ultrasound — surface preparation mandatory.
- ❌ **Limitation 3:** Cannot inspect porous materials or highly attenuative composites.
- ❌ **Limitation 4:** Skilled operator required for interpretation.

**Standards:** **ASTM E494**, **ASTM E164**, **ASTM E317**, **EN 10160**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to AVOID:**
> 1. **Confusing HIP with simple heat treatment.** HIP is *simultaneous* heat + pressure. Heat treatment alone (annealing) does NOT close internal porosity — it only relieves stress.
> 2. **Stating "DPT detects subsurface cracks"** — this is **WRONG**. DPT only detects **surface-breaking** defects. Subsurface cracks require UT or RT.
> 3. **Forgetting standards.** KTU 2024 ESE answer scripts that cite specific ASTM/ISO standards (E1417, F3301, E494) score higher.
> 4. **Confusing Ra and Rz.** Ra is the *mean* roughness; Rz is *peak-to-valley*. Using them interchangeably loses marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the couplant requirement in UT.** Always mention couplant (gel/water) when explaining UT — many students omit this and lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Mixing up VED and SED** (Volumetric vs. Surface Energy Density). VED = P/(v·h·t) in J/mm³. SED = P/(v·h) in J/mm².

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **As-built AM surfaces are rough (Ra 5–80 µm)** due to staircase effect, balling, and adhered powder.
- ✅ **Three treatment families:** Mechanical (polishing, shot peening), Thermal (HIP, heat treatment), Electro-chemical (electropolishing).
- ✅ **HIP standard cycle:** 100 MPa + 0.7 Tm + 3–4 hours for Ti6Al4V.
- ✅ **Shot peening introduces COMPRESSIVE residual stress** — improves fatigue life 2–10×.
- ✅ **Five KTU NDT methods:** VT, DPT, UT, RT (incl. X-ray CT), ECT.
- ✅ **DPT = surface only; UT = subsurface bulk; X-ray CT = volumetric 3D; ECT = conductive surface/near-surface; VT = visual only.**
- ✅ **Staircase roughness formula:** $R_a = \frac{h \cdot \sin(2\theta)}{4}$.
- ✅ **Key ASTM standards to memorize:** E1417 (DPT), E494 (UT), E1441 (CT), F3301 (AM post-processing), F2924 (Ti6Al4V AM), F86 (biocompatibility).
- ✅ **Closed porosity ≠ zero porosity** — HIP reduces to < 0.1%, not 0%.
- ✅ **No single NDT covers all defects** — multi-method approach is industry standard.
- ✅ **Process feedback loop is critical** — NDT results must feed back to AM parameter control (laser power, scan strategy).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reverse Engineering for Metal AM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET867 - METAL ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reverse Engineering for Metal AM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
