# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Volumetric Energy Density (VED) Calculation

> **Problem:** An LPBF machine operates with laser power $P = 200\ W$, scan speed $v = 800\ mm/s$, hatch spacing $h = 0.1\ mm$, and layer thickness $t = 0.03\ mm$. Compute the Volumetric Energy Density (VED) delivered to the powder bed.

### 🔹 Step 1 — Identify Given Parameters

$$P = 200\ W = 200\ J/s$$
$$v = 800\ mm/s$$
$$h = 0.1\ mm$$
$$t = 0.03\ mm$$

### 🔹 Step 2 — Recall the VED Formula

$$VED = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

This represents the **energy delivered per unit volume** of powder bed melted.

### 🔹 Step 3 — Substitute Numerically

First, compute the denominator:

$$\begin{aligned}
v \cdot h \cdot t &= 800 \times 0.1 \times 0.03 \\
&= 80 \times 0.03 \\
&= 2.4\ mm^3/s
\end{aligned}$$

Now divide power by the volumetric scan rate:

$$\begin{aligned}
VED &= \frac{200\ J/s}{2.4\ mm^3/s} \\
&= 83.33\ J/mm^3
\end{aligned}$$

### 🔹 Step 4 — Interpretation

> **Result: $VED \approx 83.3\ J/mm^3$**

**Interpretation Table:**

| VED Range (J/mm³) | Outcome for Ti-6Al-4V |
|---|---|
| < 40 | Lack of fusion, high porosity |
| 40 – 120 | **Optimal range** — dense parts |
| 120 – 200 | Keyholing porosity |
| > 200 | Over-melting, balling, evaporation |

Our result of **83.3 J/mm³** lies in the **optimal window**, predicting a part with relative density > 99.5%.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Material Utilization Efficiency (MUE)

> **Problem:** To produce a Ti-6Al-4V aerospace bracket by LPBF, 850 g of powder was loaded into the build chamber. After the build, 640 g of unused powder was recovered (sieved and ready for re-use). The final part weighs **210 g**. Compute the Material Utilization Efficiency.

### 🔹 Step 1 — Identify Given Data

- Powder loaded: $m_{loaded} = 850\ g$
- Recovered powder: $m_{recovered} = 640\ g$
- Final part mass: $m_{part} = 210\ g$

### 🔹 Step 2 — Verify Mass Balance

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{consumed} &= m_{loaded} - m_{recovered} \\
&= 850 - 640 \\
&= 210\ g \quad ✓ \quad (\text{matches } m_{part})
\end{aligned}$$

### 🔹 Step 3 — Apply Material Utilization Efficiency Formula

$$\eta_{material} = \frac{m_{part}}{m_{loaded}} \times 100\%$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{material} &= \frac{210}{850} \times 100\% \\
&= 24.7\%
\end{aligned}$$

### 🔹 Step 4 — Compare with Conventional Machining

For the same bracket by CNC machining (block of 2.5 kg machined to 210 g part):

$$\eta_{material, CNC} = \frac{0.210}{2.500} \times 100\% = 8.4\%$$

### 🔹 Step 5 — Sustainability Verdict

> **AM is ~3× more material-efficient** than CNC for this part, and the unused 640 g of powder can be **recycled for the next build** — making the **effective wastage** even lower over multiple cycles.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) and Carbon Footprint

> **Problem:** An LPBF machine consumes 22 kWh to build a batch of 10 identical Inconel 718 parts. Each part weighs **180 g**. The build is in Kerala, where the grid emission factor is $0.65\ kg\ CO_2/kWh$. Compute (a) the Specific Energy Consumption (SEC), and (b) the carbon footprint per part.

### 🔹 Part (a): SEC

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{total} &= 10 \times 180\ g = 1800\ g = 1.8\ kg \\
SEC &= \frac{E_{total}}{m_{total}} \\
&= \frac{22\ kWh}{1.8\ kg} \\
&= 12.22\ kWh/kg
\end{aligned}$$

> **Result: SEC = 12.22 kWh/kg** (lower end, indicating an efficient run)

### 🔹 Part (b): Carbon Footprint per Part

$$\begin{aligned}
CF_{total} &= SEC \times EF_{grid} \\
&= 12.22 \times 0.65 \\
&= 7.94\ kg\ CO_2/kg\ of\ part
\end{aligned}$$

Per part:

$$\begin{aligned}
CF_{part} &= \frac{7.94 \times 0.180}{1\ part} \\
&= 1.43\ kg\ CO_2/part
\end{aligned}$$

### 🔹 Sustainability Comment

> For comparison, if this Inconel 718 part were **cast** (with significant re-melt and post-machining), the LCA-emission estimate is **8–12 kg CO₂/part**. However, the AM advantage compounds when **lightweighting** is considered — a topology-optimized AM bracket can reduce in-service fuel burn by 15–30%, yielding **lifetime CO₂ savings far exceeding** the production-stage footprint.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Build Rate and Productivity Estimation

> **Problem:** A DED-LMD machine deposits a Ti-6Al-4V aerospace rib of volume **45 cm³**. The build takes **38 minutes**. Compute the build rate and estimate the throughput per 8-hour shift (assuming 25 minutes of setup between parts).

### 🔹 Step 1 — Build Rate

$$\begin{aligned}
BR &= \frac{V_{part}}{t_{build}} \\
&= \frac{45\ cm^3}{38\ min} \\
&= 1.184\ cm^3/min \times 60 \\
&= 71.05\ cm^3/h
\end{aligned}$$

### 🔹 Step 2 — Cycle Time per Part

$$t_{cycle} = t_{build} + t_{setup} = 38 + 25 = 63\ minutes/part$$

### 🔹 Step 3 — Parts per Shift

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{parts} &= \frac{8 \times 60}{63} \\
&= \frac{480}{63} \\
&\approx 7.6 \Rightarrow 7\ parts\ per\ shift
\end{aligned}$$

### 🔹 Interpretation

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Build Rate | **71 cm³/h** (typical DED range: 50–500 cm³/h) |
| Cycle Time | 63 min |
| Throughput | 7 parts / 8 h shift |

> [!NOTE]
> LPBF has build rates of typically **5–20 cm³/h** — much slower than DED but with finer resolution. Choose the process based on **part size and feature resolution requirements.**

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Exam-Style Computations

| Problem Type | Key Formula | Typical Numerical Trap | KTU Examiner's Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| VED calculation | $VED = P/(v \cdot h \cdot t)$ | Unit mismatch (W vs J/s) | Always convert power to J/s |
| Material efficiency | $\eta = m_{part}/m_{loaded}$ | Confusing loaded vs consumed | Loaded mass is denominator |
| Carbon footprint | $CF = SEC \times EF$ | Forgetting grid factor | Mention state/region |
| Surface roughness | $\Delta h = t \cdot \cos\theta$ | Using degrees vs radians in advanced models | Convert to radians for trig |
| Porosity | $P = 1 - \rho_{rel}$ | Sign confusion | Always $0 \leq P \leq 1$ |
| Build rate | $BR = V/t$ | cm³/h vs cm³/s | Convert to consistent units |
| BFR | $BFR = m_{feedstock}/m_{part}$ | Confusing with MUE | BFR ≥ 1, MUE ≤ 100% |

# 🎓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**Define Buy-to-Fly Ratio (BFR). Compare the BFR of a Ti-6Al-4V aerospace bracket manufactured by (a) CNC machining from a forged billet and (b) LPBF. Why is this metric central to aerospace sustainability arguments?**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1 Mark):**
The Buy-to-Fly Ratio (BFR) is the ratio of the mass of starting raw material to the mass of the final functional part.

$$BFR = \frac{m_{feedstock}}{m_{part}}$$

**Comparison (1 Mark):**
- CNC from billet: BFR ≈ **10:1 to 40:1** (most material becomes chips)
- LPBF: BFR ≈ **1.5:1 to 3:1** (near-net-shape with reusable powder)

**Significance (1 Mark):**
In aerospace, where titanium costs ₹4,000–6,000/kg and energy-intensive machining dominates cost, a lower BFR directly translates to (i) reduced raw material cost, (ii) lower energy for material extraction, and (iii) reduced carbon footprint — making BFR the **single most cited sustainability metric** in aerospace AM case studies.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**List any THREE key performance differences between as-built LPBF parts and their wrought counterparts. Suggest ONE post-processing technique for each difference to bring performance closer to wrought levels.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

| # | Performance Gap (As-Built LPBF) | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Higher surface roughness (Ra 5–20 μm vs 0.8 μm) | CNC machining / polishing / shot peening |
| 2 | Internal porosity (~0.5–2%) | Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP) |
| 3 | Lower fatigue life due to defects and residual stress | HIP + stress-relief heat treatment |

(1 mark for clearly stating the gap, 1 mark for the correct remedy, 1 mark for completeness and presentation)

---

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **🔷 QUESTION A (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]*

**Question:**
**(a)** Discuss in detail the **mechanical performance characteristics** of metal AM parts produced via Laser Powder Bed Fusion (LPBF), with specific reference to **density, tensile strength, fatigue behavior, and anisotropy**. Compare these properties with conventionally manufactured (wrought) counterparts and explain the microstructural origins of the observed differences.

**(b)** Explain the **sustainability framework** for metal AM using **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)**. With the help of a typical numerical example, compute the **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** and **Carbon Footprint** for an LPBF-built Ti-6Al-4V aerospace part. Discuss at least **three** strategies to improve the sustainability of metal AM.

---

#### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — PART (a) [7 Marks]

**(1) Density [1 Mark]:**
LPBF parts typically achieve relative densities of **99.0–99.8%** using optimized parameters. Wrought parts are near 100%. Remaining porosity (~0.2–1%) arises from:
- **Lack-of-fusion defects** (insufficient VED)
- **Keyholing** (excessive VED, vapor recoil)

**(2) Tensile Strength [2 Marks]:**
LPBF Ti-6Al-4V shows:
- UTS: **950–1100 MPa** (wrought: 895–965 MPa)
- Yield Strength: **880–1050 MPa** (wrought: 825–925 MPa)
- Elongation: **8–14%** (wrought: 10–18%)

LPBF strength is **often higher** than wrought due to fine martensitic α' microstructure (rapid cooling, ~10⁴–10⁶ K/s). However, **ductility is lower** due to the brittle martensite and residual porosity.

**(3) Fatigue Behavior [2 Marks]:**
LPBF fatigue strength is **15–40% lower** than wrought due to:
- Surface roughness acting as crack initiation sites
- Internal porosity as crack nuclei
- Residual tensile stresses from rapid solidification

**Mitigation:** HIP (Hot Isostatic Pressing at ~900°C, 100 MPa) closes internal pores and can restore fatigue life to within **90–95% of wrought values**.

**(4) Anisotropy [2 Marks]:**
Properties differ along the **build direction (Z)** vs **in-plane (X, Y)**. Vertical builds have lower fatigue and tensile properties because lack-of-fusion defects lie perpendicular to the load direction. Horizontal builds show more isotropic behavior. **Heat treatment** (above β-transus for Ti-alloys) can reduce anisotropy by promoting grain recrystallization.

---

#### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — PART (b) [7 Marks]

**(1) LCA Framework [2 Marks]:**
Per ISO 14040/14044, LCA consists of four phases:
1. **Goal & Scope Definition** — e.g., 1 kg of Ti-6Al-4V bracket
2. **Life Cycle Inventory (LCI)** — energy, material, emissions
3. **Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA)** — global warming, acidification, etc.
4. **Interpretation** — improvement opportunities

Stages covered: Powder atomization → Build → Support removal → Heat treatment → Use phase → End-of-life recycling.

**(2) Numerical Example [3 Marks]:**

Given:
- Part mass $m_{part} = 0.5\ kg$ (Ti-6Al-4V bracket)
- Build energy = 110 kWh (machine + atmosphere control)
- Powder atomization energy = 12 kWh
- Post-processing (HIP + machining) = 25 kWh
- Total $E_{total} = 110 + 12 + 25 = 147\ kWh$

**SEC Calculation:**

$$SEC = \frac{147\ kWh}{0.5\ kg} = 294\ kWh/kg$$

**Carbon Footprint (Kerala grid, EF = 0.65 kg CO₂/kWh):**

$$CF = 294 \times 0.65 = 191.1\ kg\ CO_2\ for\ the\ part$$

This is comparable to casting+machining baseline (~180–220 kg CO₂), but AM advantage emerges in the **use phase** through lightweighting.

**(3) Sustainability Strategies [2 Marks]:**
1. **Use renewable electricity** — switching Kerala grid from coal-heavy to solar/hydro reduces CF by 50–70%.
2. **Powder recycling** — closed-loop powder recovery reduces raw-material LCA burden by 30%.
3. **Topology optimization** — lightweight AM brackets reduce in-service aerospace fuel burn by 15–25%, yielding lifetime CO₂ savings far exceeding production impact.

---

### **🔷 QUESTION B (14 Marks)** *[Alternative Choice for Internal Selection]*

**Question:**
**(a)** "Metal AM is sustainable only when its performance matches or exceeds conventional manufacturing." Critically evaluate this statement with reference to **performance benchmarks** for LPBF Ti-6Al-4V and Inconel 718 components.

**(b)** Discuss the **future directions** of metal AM under the following headings: (i) In-situ process monitoring and closed-loop control, (ii) Hybrid additive-subtractive systems, (iii) Multi-material and functionally graded AM, and (iv) AI/ML-driven parameter optimization. For each, explain the engineering rationale and one industrial application.

---

#### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — PART (a) [7 Marks]

**(1) Statement Critique [1 Mark]:**
The statement is **partially valid**. While matching performance is a **necessary condition** for replacing conventional parts, sustainability is an **independent axis** that considers material waste, energy, and lifecycle — so a high-performance AM part with poor sustainability metrics may still be rejected on environmental grounds.

**(2) Ti-6Al-4V LPBF Benchmarks [2 Marks]:**
- Density: 99.5%+ achievable
- UTS: ~1000 MPa (vs wrought ~950 MPa) ✓
- Fatigue: ~80–85% of wrought (mitigated by HIP)
- Surface: Ra 5–15 μm (requires post-finishing)
- Performance: ✓ for static aerospace brackets; △ for fatigue-critical rotating parts without HIP

**(3) Inconel 718 LPBF Benchmarks [2 Marks]:**
- Density: 99.3–99.7%
- UTS: 1300–1400 MPa (vs wrought 1280 MPa) ✓
- Creep resistance: Comparable at < 650°C; inferior above (due to lack of γ' strengthening control in AM)
- Hot-fatigue: ~75–90% of wrought

**(4) Verdict [2 Marks]:**
Performance parity is achieved for **static-loaded, moderate-temperature** applications. For fatigue-critical and high-temperature service, AM still requires **HIP + post-machining** to match wrought. Hence, sustainability of AM is best leveraged in **complex, lightweight, low-volume** parts (e.g., fuel nozzles, brackets, implants) where conventional manufacturing cannot match the geometric freedom.

---

#### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — PART (b) [7 Marks]

**(i) In-situ monitoring & closed-loop control [2 Marks]:**
- **Rationale:** Real-time detection of porosity, lack-of-fusion, and cracking during build enables immediate correction via parameter adjustment.
- **Technology:** High-speed CMOS cameras (>10 kHz), photodiodes, IR pyrometers.
- **Application:** GE Aviation LEAP fuel nozzles — every build is monitored, with 0.1% defect rate achieved.

**(ii) Hybrid additive-subtractive systems [2 Marks]:**
- **Rationale:** Combines AM's geometric freedom with CNC's surface finish and tolerance.
- **Technology:** Machines like DMG MORI LASERTEC 65 3D hybrid.
- **Application:** Large aerospace structural parts where as-built AM surfaces are machined to Ra < 1 μm in the same setup.

**(iii) Multi-material & FGM AM [1.5 Marks]:**
- **Rationale:** Functional gradation of composition/properties (e.g., porous bone-side + dense body for implants).
- **Application:** Conformal cooling inserts in injection molds (Cu-alloy core + tool-steel skin).

**(iv) AI/ML-driven parameter optimization [1.5 Marks]:**
- **Rationale:** Reduce trial-and-error cost (each LPBF experiment costs ₹50,000–2,00,000); ML can predict optimal VED, hatch, scan strategy.
- **Application:** Siemens and EOS use Bayesian optimization to find optimal parameters for new alloys in 5–10 iterations vs 100+ traditional.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones in "Performance & Sustainability" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "sustainability" with "energy efficiency" alone** — full marks require LCA discussion covering material, energy, and end-of-life.
> 2. **Quoting LPBF properties without specifying build orientation** — always state the orientation (horizontal/vertical) because properties are anisotropic.
> 3. **Ignoring post-processing impact on sustainability** — HIP, heat treatment, and machining add 30–50% to total SEC; mention this explicitly.
> 4. **Presenting AM as universally greener** — wrong. AM is greener for complex parts; CNC wins for simple bulk parts. Always justify.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical problems** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 6. **Not mentioning grid emission factor origin** — CF depends on electricity source; India ≠ France.

- ✅ **BFR (Buy-to-Fly Ratio)** is the aerospace metric for material efficiency; AM reduces BFR from 20:1 to ~2:1.
- ✅ **SEC** of LPBF = **150–250 kWh/kg**; DED = **50–100 kWh/kg**; CNC = **10–50 kWh/kg** — context-dependent.
- ✅ **Carbon Footprint** = $SEC \times EF_{grid}$ — depends on regional electricity mix.
- ✅ **LCA stages** per ISO 14040: Powder → Build → Post-processing → Use → End-of-life.
- ✅ **Sustainable AM strategies**: renewable electricity, powder recycling, topology optimization, hybrid manufacturing.
- ✅ **Future directions**: in-situ monitoring, closed-loop ML control, hybrid AM+CNC, multi-material/FGM, AI-driven parameter optimization, digital twins, circular powder economy.
- ✅ **ASTM F42** standards govern AM process classification and testing protocols.
- ✅ **For exam answers**, ALWAYS quote **numerical values** with **units** for credibility.
- ✅ **Mention** powder recyclability, build orientation, post-processing, and grid EF for full marks on sustainability.
